nodes.title: Evolution and determinants of firm-level systemic risk in local production networks
nodes.abstract: Recent crises like the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions have exposed vulnerabilities and caused disruptions of supply chains, leading to product shortages, increased costs, and economic instability. This has prompted increasing efforts to assess systemic risk, namely the effects of firm disruptions on entire economies. However, the ability of firms to react to crises by rewiring their supply links has been largely overlooked, limiting our understanding of production networks resilience. Here, we study dynamics and determinants of firm-level systemic risk in the Hungarian production network from 2015 to 2022. We use as benchmark the stripe-corrected gravity model [1], a heuristic maximum entropy null model that generates an ensemble of production networks at equilibrium, by preserving the total input (demand) and output (supply) of each firm at the sector level. By computing the Economic Systemic Risk Index (ESRI) [2] we can see how the empirical values align well with the null model until the onset of the pandemic, as shown in Figure 1 A. It becomes significantly smaller afterwards, as the adaptive behavior of firms leads to a more resilient economy – both in absolute terms and with respect to equilibrium values. Furthermore we show that the fairly stable set of firms with highest systemic risk undergoes a structural change during COVID-19, as those enabling economic exchanges become key players in the economy – a result which can be seen in Figure 1 B and that is not reproduced by the null model. Moreover, regressions highlight how firms’ international trade volume (being a subject of disruption) becomes a significant predictor of their systemic risk. However, international links cannot provide an unequivocal explanation for the observed trends, as imports and exports have opposing effects on local systemic risk through the supply and demand channels.

nodes.title: Hypergraph reconstruction from dynamics
nodes.abstract: A plethora of methods have been developed in the past two decades to infer the underlying network structure of an interconnected system from its collective dynamics. However, methods capable of inferring nonpairwise interactions are only starting to appear. Here, we develop an inference algorithm based on sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) to reconstruct hypergraphs and simplicial complexes from time-series data. Our model-free method does not require information about node dynamics or coupling functions, making it applicable to complex systems that do not have a reliable mathematical description. We first benchmark the new method on synthetic data generated from Kuramoto and Lorenz dynamics. We then use it to infer the effective connectivity in the brain from resting-state EEG data, which reveals significant contributions from non-pairwise interactions in shaping the macroscopic brain dynamics.

nodes.title: Belief Coevolution in a Social Network of Generalist and Specialist Large Language Models
nodes.authors: Savcisens, Germans*; Dies, Samantha; Maynard, Courtney; Eliassi-Rad, Tina
nodes.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) now generate a significant portion of online content. AI-generated text appears in official press releases, scientific articles, and social knowledge-sharing platforms. As LLMs become more accessible, they are increasingly used in multi-agent settings alongside humans, where collaboration occurs through sustained multi-turn interactions. This environment creates an ongoing feedback loop in which LLMs shape users’ beliefs while simultaneously adapting to user-generated content. However, we still lack a systematic understanding of how LLMs form and propagate beliefs. Moreover, existing research on interactions between LLMs focuses primarily on behavioral proxies (e.g., decisions LLMs make in collaborative tasks)  rather than the underlying belief states that drive these behaviors. Additionally, experiments often examine settings where LLM agents interact in pairs or share information globally, overlooking network-mediated scenarios in which agents interact only with immediate neighbors. Belief dynamics are further complicated by the heterogeneity of LLMs’ expertise. Some LLMs are finetuned on domain-specific corpora (e.g., biomedical literature, cybersecurity reports), while others are optimized for particular capabilities (e.g., storytelling or coding). When such diverse agents interact within a social network, their expertise and social roles (how LLMs present themselves to others) create a complex interactive environment.  We propose CoevolveSim, an agent-based framework for simulating the coevolution of beliefs in a social network composed of LLMs. At each iteration, an LLM agent receives a summary of its neighbors’ beliefs along with their social roles. The agent then updates its belief based on local information and broadcasts the updated belief to its neighbors in the next round. CoevolveSim disentangles the effects of three factors: (1) expertise:  domain knowledge embedded through finetuning, (2) social roles: identity labels that signal credibility to neighbors, and (3) network structure: who interacts with whom.   Using both global and local measures of influence, we identify conditions under which belief diffusion leads to (1) stable convergence, (2) sharp phase-transition-like cascades, or (3) fragmented disagreement. Our preliminary results on 16 generalist and specialist LLMs connected by Erdős–Rényi graphs show that generalist LLMs without finetuning but with social roles exhibit rapid belief convergence toward consensus, while specialist LLMs with finetuning and corresponding social roles resist updating beliefs, allowing errors to persist throughout interactions.

nodes.title: Real-Space Renormalization and Sparsification of Complex Networks
nodes.abstract: Graph sparsification and renormalization are fundamental tools for reducing the complexity of networks and network models, both to make them more amenable to numerical simulation and to reveal how macroscopic behavior scales with system size [1]. Existing renormalization schemes rely either on heuristic blocking, which yields poorly controlled approximations, or on spectral constructions, which can show poor performance on heterogeneous networks. We introduce a general real-space sparsification and renormalization framework based on message passing, which provides a recipe for performing local surgery on networks---removal of nodes or edges---while preserving the values of physically meaningful quantities. The method can be applied to any network process that can be represented using message passing.  As examples, we apply the approach to edge percolation and to the solution of the Ising model at finite temperature.  We also show that when our method is applied to the calculation of effective conductance in networks, the well-known Spielman–Teng spectral sparsifier [2] emerges as a limiting case.  [1] Andrea Gabrielli et al. Network renormalization. Preprint arxiv:2412.12988. 2024. [2] Daniel A. Spielman and Shang-Hua Teng. “Spectral sparsification of graphs”. In: SIAM J. Comput. 40 (2011), pp. 981–1025

nodes.title: Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning for Resilience-Oriented Decision-Making in Critical Infrastructure Systems
nodes.authors: Fan, Xudong*; Hackl, Jürgen; Yu, Xiong (Bill)
nodes.abstract: Critical infrastructure systems (CIS), such as water distribution networks, road networks, and power networks, are often referred to as lifelines because they are essential for supporting the daily functioning of communities. The rapid expansion of CIS driven by urbanization has increased their vulnerability to high-consequence hazards, including earthquakes, flooding, and wildfires, thereby highlighting the urgent need for effective resilience-enhancement strategies. The resilience of a CIS is commonly defined as its ability to recover functionality following disruptive events, and sound repair-sequence decisions play a crucial role in achieving rapid and efficient recovery. This study presents the development of a Graph Convolutional Neural Network--integrated Deep Reinforcement Learning (GCN--DRL) framework to support optimal repair decisions aimed at improving CIS resilience under multiple hazard scenarios.  To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed GCN--DRL model, a water distribution network (WDN) and a road network subjected to earthquake and flooding hazards are used as case studies. Resilience evaluation frameworks for both systems are first developed, explicitly capturing the dynamic evolution of system performance during post-event recovery. For the WDN, the performance indicator accounts for the relative importance of service nodes and the extent to which post-earthquake water demands are satisfied. For the road network, the performance indicator reflects network connectivity under road segment closures. Within the GCN--DRL framework, the GCN encodes the structural and operational information of both the WDN and the road network. Network topology and node-level performance states (i.e., water demand satisfaction levels and road segment availability) are provided as inputs to the GCN, which outputs action-specific reward values. These values are then used by the DRL agent to identify optimal repair sequences from a large action space, with the objective of maximizing overall system resilience.  The repair strategies generated by the proposed GCN--DRL model are compared with those obtained using four conventional decision-making approaches. The results demonstrate that the GCN--DRL model consistently achieves the highest system resilience index (SRI) values and enables the fastest recovery of system performance. Furthermore, by leveraging transfer learning from pre-trained models, the proposed framework exhibits high computational efficiency when determining optimal repair sequences under new damage scenarios. Overall, the GCN--DRL model demonstrates strong robustness and generalizability, providing an effective decision-support tool for enhancing the resilience of interdependent infrastructure systems under multiple hazard conditions.

nodes.title: Philanthropic Influence of Art Collectors on U.S. Art Museums
nodes.abstract: Art museum collections evolve through complex social relationships [1]. In particular, art collectors play a key role: their philanthropic donations sustain many museum holdings [2], yet their influence on artists’ institutional careers remains poorly quantified. Here, we compile a large-scale dataset of historical museum acquisitions from 15 major publicly funded art museums in the United States, selected to balance institutional prestige and geographic diversity. We extract artists and collectors names from unstructured text through an AI-human name disambiguation pipeline. The final data comprise 1,341,762 artworks linked to 100,686 artists, 47,123 collectors, and 3,640 organizations. Each artwork in the data includes its acquisition year, title of the artwork, artist name, and name of the collector, who donated or provided resources for the respective museum acquisition. From these data, we map the network of collectors linking artists with museums over time through philanthropic donations. We characterize connectivity in the museum network along two general dimensions: scope and frequency. Scope captures whether connections remain local, concentrated within a single museum, or global, spanning multiple institutions. Frequency captures whether these connections occur one-time, or repeat over time. Together, these dimensions define four connectivity types: local one-time, local repeat, global one-time, and global repeat. Applied jointly to collectors and artists, these dimensions describe how relationships between artists and museums are established and sustained. We find that 20% are local repeat collectors, who act as institutional anchors, supporting 56% of the long-term growth of museum collections. A 69% are one-time donors (local or global) who contribute occasionally with a 12% of total donations. In contrast, 11% are global repeat collectors responsible for 32% of total donations, who emerge as cultural connectors by linking museums and introducing artists into multiple institutional contexts. On the artist side, 75% of artists are distributed in only one museum, whereas 25% of artists achieve global institutional presence. We observe that wider artists’ visibility is closely associated with the giving patterns by global repeat collectors, who enable the circulation of artworks across institutions. Interestingly, global repeat collectors form connections with artists and institutions through mixed selective patterns (Fig. 1a), demonstrating that collectors’ donation choices can significantly influence artists’ institutional career success (Fig. 1b). Collectors’ selective connectivity evidences that they function as bridges, or gatekeepers, for artists’ visibility across institutions, forming or reinforcing the artists-museum connection. Overall, our network-based framework captures fundamental dimensions of collector-mediated connectivity, offering a quantitative lens on how artistic visibility is gained and why artists’ careers diverge within museum systems shaped by philanthropic giving. More broadly, this work points to new ways of turning cultural data into structured knowledge, creating building blocks that can feed into future AI systems, data science tools, and new forms of computational art.

nodes.title: Graph evolution rules meet communities: assessing global and local patterns in the evolution of dynamic networks
nodes.authors: Galdeman, Alessia*; Zignani, Matteo; Gaito, Sabrina
nodes.abstract: Dynamic networks often exhibit modular structure, yet the mechanisms driving the evolution of their communities remain poorly understood. Graph evolution rules (GERs) capture recurrent local transformations in evolving networks in a data-driven and interpretable way. In this work, we combine GER mining with community detection to analyze network evolution at both global and mesoscopic scales. Using EvoMine and the Leiden algorithm, we derive evolutionary profiles that characterize networks and their communities. Our results show that communities may follow heterogeneous or uniform evolutionary patterns depending on the system, and that structurally connected communities tend to evolve similarly. This approach provides a fine-grained perspective on how local temporal processes shape community evolution.

nodes.title: The Extended Community of Diet Culture Around Eating Disorders: A Network Analysis of Reddit Forums
nodes.authors: Qiu, Jiaxing*; Levinson, Cheri; Kropko, Jonathan; Henry, Teague; Gates, Alexander
nodes.abstract: Clinical studies suggest a history of dieting is a known risk factor for eating disorders (ED), yet the relationship between diet culture and ED in online spaces remains underexplored. While ED online communities offer insight into psychological and behavioral experiences about the illness, broader dieting culture---often shaped by restrictive eating norms and body hierarchies---may inadvertently promote disordered behaviors.  We investigate the structural and thematic connections between ED and diet-culture online communities by combining network analyses with large language model (LLM)-assisted topic modeling. We analyzed posts and comments from 12 ED and 105 dieting or body-image forums on Reddit. We constructed two forum-level networks: a semantic similarity network, where edge weights are defined as the average cosine similarity between Sentence-BERT embeddings of posts across forum pairs, and a co-engagement intensity network, where edge weights capture normalized cross-posting and co-commenting activity from shared users based on each forum’s newest posts and associated comments. To identify clusters of forums with dense semantic or behavioral connectivity, we detected communities by optimizing the modularity objective function using the Louvain method, selecting the resolution parameter that maximized modularity. We further quantified between-group connectivity using average edge weight and assessed statistical significance via a resampling-based null model. To interpret these clusters, we examined the prevalence of 13 topics using annotations by advanced LLMs, aggregated into forum-level prevalences.   Results show significantly higher semantic similarity and co-engagement intensity between ED and dieting forums than random topics. The detected community structures on both networks revealed extended communities in which a subset of dieting forums consistently clustered with ED forums. Within these communities, ED and ED-adjacent dieting forums shared elevated discussions of 'nutritional restriction', 'food cravings', 'body dissatisfaction', and 'family or social relationships'. These findings highlight the entanglement of ED and diet culture in online discourse and underscore the need for nuanced public health approaches to monitoring not only ED but general dieting discussions in digital spaces.

nodes.title: Unifying Information-Theoretic and Pair-Counting Community Similarity
nodes.abstract: Comparing community structures is fundamental to evaluating unsupervised methods in network science, yet existing similarity measures often yield inconsistent or even contradictory assessments[1].  Community similarity measures are typically divided into two broad families: pair-counting and information-theoretic, depending on whether agreement is quantified through element pairs or through aggregate information over community contingency tables.  While prior work has noted empirical relationships between these families and proposed normalization or chance-correction schemes, their underlying analytical connection remains incomplete[2].  Here, we introduce a unifying framework that links pair-counting and information-theoretic community similarity measures through two complementary perspectives. First, we show that both families can be written as weighted expansions of observed versus expected co-occurrences: pair-counting measures arise as low-order approximations, whereas information-theoretic measures correspond to higher-order, frequency-weighted expansions of community residuals (Fig 1C,D).  Second, we generalize pair-counting to $k$-tuples and demonstrate that information-theoretic measures can be interpreted as systematically accumulating higher-order co-assignment structure beyond the pairwise level (Fig 1A,B).  We illustrate these connections analytically for the Rand index and Mutual Information, and show how other commonly used measures in each family emerge as natural extensions of this framework.   Our key insight is that reported agreement between community detection algorithms can be fundamentally scale-biased.  In networks with heterogeneous community sizes, high similarity scores may indicate consensus on a few large communities while concealing substantial and systematic disagreement in mesoscale and peripheral organization.  Our framework clarifies when and why pair-counting and information-theoretic measures diverge, links their behavior directly to weighting and approximation order, and establishes a principled foundation for evaluating and designing community similarity measures that faithfully reflect network structure across scales.

nodes.title: Graph-aware proper scoring rules for node selection tasks
nodes.authors: Hilfiker, Lorenz*; Brack, Nathan; Sterchi, Martin
nodes.abstract: Let $G=(V,E)$ be a graph with $N=|V|$ nodes. A \emph{node selection task} on $G$ consists of inferring a single node $i\in V$. This subsumes important graph learning problems such as epidemic source detection, influence maximisation, or fault detection. Any edge selection task can also be recast as a node selection task on the line graph. Many approaches to such problems are probabilistic (e.g., Monte Carlo methods or graph neural networks), which means they estimate $i$ by providing a probability distribution $\vec{p}\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^N$, $\|\vec{p}\|_1=1$, over $V$. Standard evaluation metrics in this setting such as top-$K$ error rate or the rank of $i$ are unsatisfactory for at least two reasons: (a)~these metrics do not take into account the graph structure when assessing how closely a prediction matches the observed true node, and (b)~they do not penalise unjustified overconfidence appropriately.  We address this gap by introducing two graph-aware scores for node selection tasks: firstly, the \emph{resistance score} $S(\vec{p},i)=-\frac{N}{2}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)^\top\Omega(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)$ where $\vec{e}_i$ is the $i$-th unit vector and $\Omega$ is the matrix of resistance distances between all pairs of nodes~[1]; secondly, the \emph{diffusion score} $S_\tau(\vec{p}, i)=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)^\top e^{-\frac{\tau}{2}(L-\mathbb{I})}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)$ with scaling parameter $\tau\in(0,\infty)$ where $L$ denotes the Laplacian of $G$. We show that these scores are \emph{proper scoring rules} [2] in the statistical sense, i.e., they are optimised when $\vec{p}$ coincides with the true distribution of $i$, discouraging unjustified overconfidence. On the complete graph both scores reduce to the well-known multi-class Brier score for $N$ independent classes. The resistance score admits an intuitive interpretation in terms of the recently introduced variance of Devriendt et al. [1] and reduces on the path graph $P_N$ to the standard ranked probability score for ordinal data [3]. The diffusion score, meanwhile, behaves like the Brier score at different scales: it coincides with the Brier score for $\tau\to 0$, while for $\tau\to\infty$ it only assesses whether $\vec{p}$ is concentrated on the correct global cluster. In this sense, it connects to recent work on Laplacian coarse-graining [4].  Figure~\ref{fig:fig1} illustrates the improved discriminative ability of our scores in a toy example. Further simulation experiments for source detection on empirical networks demonstrate that our scores successfully distinguish between models that traditional metrics fail to separate. Moreover, we demonstrate that both scores can be efficiently approximated even for very large networks using techniques based on Krylov subspaces and Laplacian solvers as explored in [5]. Their use as loss functions in graph neural networks is left for future work.

nodes.title: Integrating comparative protein biology with network medicine to elucidate the therapeutic role of plant chemicals for cardiovascular disease
nodes.abstract: Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading cause of death globally. Epidemiological studies demonstrate that plant-based diets provide cardioprotective benefits, yet our inability to predict precise molecular mechanisms limit precise dietary interventions. While network medicine offers a powerful framework for predicting how compounds modulate disease, the poor protein target characterization of plant chemicals creates severe data sparsity that prevents the systematic analysis of potential cardiovascular effects. We developed a computational framework that addresses this critical data limitation by integrating comparative protein biology with network medicine. This approach leverages the shared evolutionary origin and the co-evolutionary history of plants and humans, offering insight into how plant chemicals are recognized in human processes despite not being endogenously produced. Evolutionary conserved proteins and their associated functions between humans and plants account for 17{\%} of the human genome, allowing us to use the substantial body of plant biochemistry research to expand phytochemical-protein interaction data in humans. We identify a conserved homology module of 5,077 orthologous proteins within the human protein-protein interactome, creating a statistically significant largest connected component for use in network medicine analysis. For ten commonly consumed plants, we identify their plant chemicals and their human protein targets. We find that their targets are enriched within the homology module, indicating their importance to the modulation of the plant-human homology module. Additionally, the homology module is enriched with genes from cardiovascular disease modules. While the homology module contains many genes essential to eukaryotic life whose alternations typically associate with pathophenotypically severe Mendelian disorders or death rather than chronic, complex diseases, these orthologous proteins also include less connected proteins that contribute to CVD pathophysiology. By integrating plant-human homology with network medicine we create a systematic framework for predicting how plant chemicals affect cardiovascular health.

nodes.title: Higher-order information-theoretic organization of motor modularity in health and stroke
nodes.authors: Deshpande, Raj*; Huber, Maximilian; Yarossi, Matthew; Petri, Giovanni
nodes.abstract: Motor coordination subserving even simple activities of daily living requires navigating a high-dimensional space of muscle activations through nonlinear and task-dependent transformations. Despite this apparent complexity, the human motor system achieves robust, flexible, and transferable behavior across a wide range of tasks and contexts.  A leading theoretical explanation for this efficiency is the existence of muscle modules: low-dimensional coordination patterns that constrain and organize muscle activity (d'Avella, A., & Bizzi, E.; 2005). Traditionally, muscle modules have been extracted using linear dimensionality-reduction techniques, which provide compact representations but are limited in their ability to characterize multivariate and higher-order dependencies among muscles.  Here, we adopt a higher-order network perspective to investigate muscle coordination, leveraging information-theoretic tools that explicitly disentangle redundancy and synergy in multivariate interactions.  We analyze publicly available data comprising electromyography (EMG) recordings from 12 upper-limb muscles during 30 distinct motor tasks, performed by 23 healthy controls and 21 individuals with mild stroke (Wrist/Hand Fugl-Meyer: 14-20) (Averta, et al., 2021).  For each participant, we computed the O-information, a multivariate measure quantifying the balance between redundant and synergistic interactions across muscle subsets (Rosas et al.,2019). From the top-k multiplets, we constructed participant-specific hypergraphs capturing dominant redundancy- and synergy-based interactions (Figure 1A). Interpreting redundancy as reflecting stable muscle modules and synergy as a marker of inter-module coordination, we observed marked reorganization in stroke participants. At the group level, stroke patients exhibited increased redundancy alongside significantly reduced synergistic interactions (Figure 1B), consistent with more rigid or stereotyped motor control.  Crucially, at the participant level, this imbalance was highly heterogeneous (Figure 1C), revealing substantial inter-individual variability in higher-order coordination patterns following stroke—variability that may inform individualized rehabilitation strategies. Together, these results demonstrate that higher-order information-theoretic networks provide a principled framework for characterizing motor coordination beyond pairwise interactions, revealing clinically relevant alterations in the balance between stability and flexibility of muscle control after stroke.

nodes.title: Diverse Human Dilemmas, Uniform AI Preferences: Mapping Value Trade-Off Networks in Online Advice-Seeking Communities and Large Language Models
nodes.authors: Chen, Zhiyi; Choi, Eun Cheol*; Luo, Yingjia; Wang, Xinyi; Xiao, Yulei; Yang, Aizi; Luceri, Luca
nodes.abstract: People increasingly seek advice online, not only from other users but also from large language model (LLM)–based chatbots [1], which now mediate how individuals navigate personal, relational, and professional dilemmas. Such advice rarely involves identifying a single correct answer; instead, it typically requires navigating trade-offs among competing values. We aim to characterize how LLMs navigate value trade-offs across different advice-seeking contexts.  In this study, we examine the value trade-off structure underlying advice-seeking using a curated dataset [2] drawn from four advice-oriented subreddits. Each dilemma is represented in a structured format consisting of a real-life scenario and two alternative courses of action. Building on the bottom-up approach of prior work [3], we inductively construct a hierarchical value framework by aggregating thousands of fine-grained values extracted from individual advice options into progressively higher-level value categories.  We find substantial heterogeneity in value trade-off structures across advice-seeking contexts: a women-focused subreddit exhibits the highest network density, indicating more complex value conflicts; women’s, men’s, and friendship-related subreddits show highly correlated patterns; and career advice forms a more distinct structure, with values such as security, respect, and commitment often in tension with autonomy, change, or opportunity.   We then evaluate LLM value preferences against these human dilemmas and find that, across models and contexts, LLMs consistently prioritize values related to 'Exploration & Growth over' 'Benevolence & Connection'. This prioritization remains stable across dilemmas drawn from different subreddits, suggesting cross-model and cross-context consistency rather than domain-specific adaptation.  Taken together, these findings highlight important societal implications as online communities and LLMs become increasingly embedded in advice-seeking ecosystems. This uniform value orientation highlights a potential risk of value homogenization in AI-mediated advice, raising concerns about how such systems may shape decision-making and normative outcomes at scale.  [1] Kumar, H., Chahal, J., Zhao, Y., Zhang, Z., Wei, A., Tay, L., \& Anderson, A. (2025). When AI Gives Advice: Evaluating AI and Human Responses to Online Advice-Seeking for Well-Being. arXiv:2512.08937. [2] Bhatia, S., van Baal, S. T., Wang, F., and Walasek, L. (2025). Computational analysis of 100K choice dilemmas: Decision attributes, trade-off structures, and model-based prediction. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 122(17). [3] Huang, S., Durmus, E., Handa, K., McCain, M., Tamkin, A., Stern, M., Hong, J., \& Ganguli, D. (2025). Values in the wild: Discovering and mapping values in real-world language model interactions. Conference on Language Modeling.

nodes.title: Are scale-free networks also timescale-free?
nodes.abstract: Our understanding of a physical system's dynamic behavior is often unraveled through its intrinsic timescales, as it responds to external excitation. The classic example is the harmonic oscillator, which is characterized by two timescales: its decay rate (damping) and its oscillation period. But what happens when the system is complex and heterogeneous, such as a scale-free network. Such systems lack a characteristic structural scale, but do they also lack a dynamic timescale? In simple terms, are scale-free networks also timescale-free?  To address this, we investigate the network’s response to arbitrary forms of excitation. Our analysis shows that complex systems fall into distinct dynamic classes: Homogeneous dynamics, where, despite the scale-free network structure, the system's actual dynamics exhibit uniform timescales.  Timescale-free dynamics, where the systems, indeed, lacks a typical timescale and, instead, features a coexistence of vastly diverse excitation lifetimes. Most excitations are extremely short-lived, but some live on for extended periods, sustained for orders of magnitude above the system's average timescale.    These newly discovered timescale-free networks feature special modes, that the network is dynamically wired to preserve for extended periods. These modes, capturing timescale-hubs, decay extremely slowly, and are thus preserved for orders of magnitude longer than all other dynamic states. More broadly, our analysis offers a systematic translation of topology (degree distribution) into dynamics (timescale distribution), that helps us predict the system’s uniquely long-lasting vs. short-lived dynamic states.

nodes.title: Construction of Hidden Metric Space with Community Structure
nodes.abstract: Recent research has demonstrated that many empirical networks possess a hidden metric space (HMS)—a metric space that underlies and shapes the observable network topology. Distances within the HMS generally reflect the similarity between nodes, making it particularly well-suited for network routing via the greedy routing strategy (GRS). When the HMS of a given network is known, the GRS can establish routes between any pair of nodes without relying on global information, a key advantage over alternative routing methods. Various approaches have been proposed for constructing the HMS, yielding different metric spaces such as planar, hyperbolic, and high-dimensional spaces. However, no principled method exists for selecting the appropriate metric space, and embedding a network into an unsuitable metric space can substantially degrade the GRS routing performance.  This work introduces a hierarchical approach for computing the HMS of a given network. Unlike traditional methods that embed networks into a single metric space, our approach relies on the network's community structure to construct a hierarchical HMS, thereby avoiding the performance limitations imposed by a predetermined metric space. Fig. 1(a) illustrates the application of our algorithm to a US population model network, where the result is a two-layer HMS. We demonstrate that this approach can be applied to an arbitrary network regardless of its physical embedding, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Compared to existing embedding algorithms, the hierarchical HMS approach consistently achieves superior routing performance while significantly reducing computational time, particularly for large-scale networks. Moreover, for certain networks, routing performance with the hierarchical HMS surpasses that achieved using real-space coordinates, suggesting that the constructed HMS captures network structure more effectively than physical embedding.

nodes.title: Bridge Criticality in a Spatial Road Network
nodes.abstract: Transportation network vulnerability has become increasingly important given the critical role infrastructure plays in national mobility and economic resilience and remains an important problem to understand how these systems can withstand and recover from failures and disruptions. While extensive research has examined the robustness of road networks through various frameworks, the critical role bridges play in road networks and the risks posed by bridge infrastructure to transportation network connectivity is understudied. As structural bottlenecks in highway systems, bridge failures can disproportionately impact network connectivity, making their assessment vital for transportation planning and risk management. Here, using National Bridge Inventory data from the Long Term Bridge Performance project of the Federal Highway Administration of the US, we study the National Highway System and develop a percolation analysis framework to study the impact of removal of bridges on the network. Different removal scenarios are compared, and we describe a combined risk score accounting for increases in distance from detours caused by removal of bridges, which can be used to identify critical bridges based on their position in their network as well as features of their infrastructure such as condition and age. Accounting for bridge feature conditions and daily traffic volume highlights the bridges that are critical in disconnecting the network and delays in travel time caused by their disruption can be identified. The figure shows the case for a single county in the state of Iowa, where a small subset of bridges removed lead to non-trivial delays and disconnection of the network. This framework when extended to the entire system can be used to provide actionable insights for infrastructure resilience and planning in the event of disruptions caused by systemic failures or natural disasters.

nodes.title: All contacts are not equal: Spatiotemporal constraints shape network structure and transmission dynamics
nodes.authors: Taube, Juliana*; Tiu, Andrew; Bansal, Shweta
nodes.abstract: Contact network structure is widely studied because of its influential role in infectious disease transmission. Since the POLYMOD study [1], routine contact surveys have consistently used similar distance (e.g., 2 meters) and duration (e.g., 5 minutes) constraints to define close contacts relevant to respiratory transmission. This standardization facilitates comparison between studies but critically overlooks the unique transmission profiles of respiratory viruses [2]. We hypothesize that pathogens may be limited to different contact network structures based on their spatiotemporal transmission constraints and thus demonstrate distinct transmission dynamics. Using a dataset with fine-scale interaction data, we investigate how changes in contact definition impact contact network structure and respiratory disease dynamics.   To characterize how network structure varies with different contact distance and duration thresholds, we use app-based GPS location data from anonymized and opted-in mobile devices aggregated to protect individual privacy. We focus our analysis on locations relevant to public health (e.g., restaurants, grocery stores), transmission constraints that are realistic for respiratory pathogens (e.g., influenza, measles, coronaviruses), and network features known to predict epidemic outcomes (e.g., mean degree, degree heterogeneity, transitivity).  After adjusting for network sampling biases [3], we find that contact network structure is sensitive to contact definition (Figure 1). Pathogens capable of transmission over large distances or through brief exposures have access to denser and more heterogeneously connected contact networks, whereas pathogens requiring close proximity or longer interactions are restricted to sparser networks with lower degree heterogeneity. Through network disease modeling, we show that this structural variation leads to distinct transmission dynamics, which can inform the inference of transmission modes for emerging pathogens from epidemiological data. These findings shed light on the evolutionary pressures faced by different respiratory pathogens and help characterize which pathogens are more likely to exhibit superspreading.  [1] Mossong J, et al. Social contacts and mixing patterns relevant to the spread of infectious diseases. PLOS Medicine. 2008 Mar;5(3):e74. [2] Leung NHL. Transmissibility and transmission of respiratory viruses. Nat Rev Microbiol. 2021 Aug;19(8):528–45. [3] Kolaczyk ED. Statistical Analysis of Network Data: Methods and Models. New York, NY: Springer; 2009.

nodes.title: Evidence of Gender-based Network Biases in Respondent-driven Sampling Data
nodes.abstract: Respondent-driven sampling (RDS) is a network-based method for studying hidden populations, such as people who inject drugs (PWID) (Heckathorn, 1997; Gile et al., 2010). Statistical inference using RDS relies on modeling recruitment as a random walk on the network, assuming participants accurately report their network composition and recruit peers approximately at random (Heckathorn, 1997; Volz and Heckathorn, 2008). In practice, reported networks may not fully reflect the true network structure, and some subgroups may face barriers to participation, potentially biasing statistical inference. Using a uniquely rich RDS dataset (Akiyama et al., 2025) spanning multiple sites and several types of network data, we investigate gender differences in network structure, reporting, and participation, and assess potential biases impacting inference. We analyze RDS data from 3,152 PWID across 17 sites in Kenya, collected as an extension of the TLC-IDU study (NCT01557998), focusing on the reported gender composition of participants’ social networks. Our analysis evaluates whether the observed proportion of women in the sample is consistent with expectations of random recruitment and the observed gender–degree structure of the sample. We further compare gender differences in reported 30-day networks, close contact data, and observed network ties. Population estimates were calculated using standard RDS methods (Volz and Heckathorn, 2008), and network simulation tests were used to assess discrepancies in participation and reporting. Results indicate that participants report more women in their networks than expected, suggesting potential over-reporting or under-participation of women. Across multiple sites, results were more nuanced, but indicate that women report more women in their 30-day networks, close contacts, and observed ties compared to men. These discrepancies highlight challenges in creating valid inferences from reported social network data and provide insight into the complexity of designing targeted interventions for PWID.

nodes.title: From Ultra-Processed Foods to Processing Pathways: A Network-Aware Inference Framework
nodes.authors: Ispirova, Gordana*; Aldana, Andres; Menichetti, Giulia
nodes.abstract: Ultra-processed foods (UPFs) are consistently associated with an increased risk of metabolic, cardiovascular, hepatic, and inflammatory diseases. However, dominant frameworks for characterizing food processing rely on qualitative and descriptive criteria that collapse highly heterogeneous industrial practices into coarse categorical labels. Foods that differ markedly in ingredients, nutrient profiles, and industrial processing steps and sequences are therefore treated as equivalent, obscuring meaningful variation and potentially masking differential disease risk. Moreover, large-scale product-level analyzes, including our study of more than 70,000 U.S. grocery products, show that food processing information is often incomplete or inconsistently annotated, underscoring the need for systematic methods that can infer fine-grained processing steps. To address these limitations, we constructed a harmonized food-processing database comprising more than 62,000 foods annotated with LanguaL-derived processing descriptors, nutrient profiles, and food-group information aggregated from heterogeneous international sources. Processing annotations are inherently relational and sparse: each food is associated with multiple semantically related descriptors reflecting combinations and sequences of operations, while many plausible food–process associations remain unobserved. This structure naturally induces a bipartite network linking foods and processing descriptors, where observed edges encode partial processing pathways and missing edges represent absent or latent operations. Inferring food processing, therefore, becomes a network learning problem under extreme label sparsity, with the central challenge of distinguishing true absences from missing annotations—a limitation that fundamentally constrains feature-based classifiers lacking access to relational structure. Here, we present a unified inference pipeline that integrates network-aware models with top-k multilabel classification baselines trained on BERT-derived embeddings from curated food metadata. By leveraging network topology, the proposed framework substantially improves recovery of known food–process associations by reducing confusion between absent and unobserved processing operations. The best-performing model achieves Top-5 and Top-10 accuracies of 88.3% and 94.4%, respectively, and a Top-10 recall of 70.9%, despite foods being annotated with only ~5 labels on average out of 269 possible descriptors—corresponding to an 8–15X improvement over random ranking. By reframing food processing as a problem of missing-edge inference in hierarchical bipartite networks, this framework moves beyond coarse UPF categories and enables systematic identification of fine-grained processing steps within and across food groups, informing targeted interventions in food formulation and public health policy. More broadly, it illustrates how network topology can be leveraged to recover latent relational structure from sparse, incomplete annotations, a setting common to many large-scale data integration problems in network science.

nodes.title: Early Rules Cast Long Shadows: Dependency Dynamics in Wikipedia’s Regulatory Network
nodes.authors: Bhattacharya, Shambhobi*; Youn, Hyejin ; Yoon , Jisung
nodes.abstract: Do rules have their own dynamics? Administrative codes in universities grow slowly as problems get absorbed, shift with leadership, and respond to external shocks [1]. But rules do not exist in isolation. In legal codes, manuals, and online platforms, rules reference each other for justification or exception. These cross-references create dependencies: a rule’s meaning is incomplete without the rules it cites. Prior work showed that Wikipedia’s regulatory pages form exactly such a network, with early rules occupying central positions [2]. Why do some rules become foundational while others remain peripheral? Here we trace the temporal evolution of Wikipedia’s regulatory dependency network to understand how this hierarchy forms and stabilizes. We construct a directed network where nodes are Wikipedia regulatory pages (policies, guidelines, etc.) and edges are cross-references in the text. Figure 1a illustrates this: the Neutral Point of View (NPOV) policy contains links like “please read [[WP:NOT]]” and “content should be [[WP:V]] verifiable.” Each link creates a directed edge. We reconstruct it yearly from 2001 to 2016. Figure 1b shows that the network grows rapidly until 2008, then saturates, consistent with problem absorption [1,2]. The inset shows that in-degree inequality (Gini) rises from 0.59 to 0.81 by 2008, then stabilizes [2]. This reflects cumulative advantage: early rules gain visibility, attract citations, and become difficult to displace. Once formed, the hierarchy persists. Figure 1c traces how this hierarchy forms. In 2001, the emerging core was dominated by NPOV and What Wikipedia Is Not (NOT). By 2003, Verifiability (V) and No Original Research (NOR) emerged, operationalizing neutrality by specifying how to achieve it. By 2008, NPOV was cited by 309 pages, and this core remained stable even as the network grew from 30 to 1,294 pages. Figure 1d reveals the dependency structure. There are asymmetric connections: some rules have far more in-degree than out-degree. This asymmetry indicates hierarchy among rules [3]. Rules with high in-degree but low out-degree are foundational: others depend on them, but they depend on few. NPOV has 452 incoming and 34 outgoing citations (13:1). All four fall below the diagonal, anchoring the network while other rules elaborate on them. Together, foundational rules established in the first two years remain central, making them costly to change. New rules do not displace existing foundations; they attach to them. Wikipedia offers a lens to trace how regulatory hierarchy emerges and persists: what drives this lock-in, is it universal, and what happens when foundations must change?

nodes.title: Assessing (im)balance in signed brain networks
nodes.authors: Di Vece, Marzio; Agrimi, Emanuele; Tatullo, Samuele; Gili, Tommaso; Ibanez-Berganza, Miguel; Squartini, Tiziano*
nodes.abstract: Many complex systems (be they financial, natural, or social) are composed of units (such as stocks, neurons, or agents) whose joint activity can be represented as a multivariate time series. An issue of both practical and theoretical importance concerns the possibility of inferring the presence of a static relationship between any two units solely from their dynamic state.  This contribution tackles such an issue within the frame of traditional hypothesis testing: briefly speaking, our suggestion is that of linking any two units if behaving in a sufficiently similar way. To achieve such a goal, we project a multivariate time series onto a signed graph by i) comparing the empirical properties of the former with those expected under a suitable benchmark and ii) linking any two units with a positive (negative) edge in case the corresponding series shares a significantly large number of concordant (discordant) values. To define our benchmarks, we adopt an information-theoretic approach that is rooted into the constrained maximisation of Shannon entropy, a procedure inducing an ensemble of multivariate time series that preserves some of the empirical properties on average, while randomising everything else.  We showcase the possible applications of our method by addressing one of the most timely issues in the domain of neurosciences, i.e. that of determining if brain networks are frustrated or not. As our results suggest, this is indeed the case, with the major contribution to the underlying negative subgraph coming from the subcortical structures (and, to a lesser extent, from the limbic regions). At the mesoscopic level, the minimisation of the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), instantiated with the Signed Stochastic Block Model, reveals that brain areas gather into modules aligning with the statistical variant of the Relaxed Balance Theory; besides, the revealed modules are capable of capturing up to the 80% of certain Yeo's regions. Notice that minimising the frustration (F) leads to isolate a number of communities as well: in this second case, however, these modules appear as quite uninformative as six of them are very small groups of nodes fragmenting a single Yeo's region in a quite unrealistic fashion, while the seventh and the eighth ones gather all the remaining regions together.  It is, thus, of interest wondering which principle is obeyed by the brain to self-organise its functioning: Yeo's partition aligns (better) with the one returned by BIC minimisation, an advantage of which is that of gathering signed connections in a maximally "agnostic" way - differently from F minimisation, that seeks for the group-wise structure best aligning with the Traditional Balance Theory.

nodes.title: Predictability of human movement in multilayer mobility networks based on industry sectors
nodes.authors: Sejunti, Maisha Islam*; Butler, Melissa ;  Hu, Yingjie ; Taylor, Dane
nodes.abstract: Understanding human movement patterns is important for a myriad of applications including social/cognitive science, urban design, disease control, and response planning for extreme weather events. In this study, we investigate the extent to which human movements are predictable by using statistical techniques and machine learning (ML) to predict the structural patterns of multilayer networks in which the layers encode the different industry sectors including movements, e.g., to locations associated with education, health, food, retail trade, and so on. We construct these time-varying, multiplex networks using the approach developed in that utilizes the SafeGraph dataset (which contains weekly human movements derived from mobile-device GPS locations) and converts that data into time-varying multilayer networks in which nodes represent  geographical regions called census tracts (CTs), and for each industry category, directed edges encode the observed movements between CTs during a given week. Given such data, we train ML models to predict the network layers in- and out-degrees (as well as those for layer-aggregated networks), using two types of model input data: social factors obtained from the U.S. Census and infrastructure information obtained from Safegraph (e.g., the number of schools in a given CT). Our study compares and contrasts the performance of different predictive models, the effects of different training procedures, and reveals which industry categories for movement are easier/harder to predict.

nodes.title: Modeling and Learning Dynamics of Online–Offline Social Systems on Networks
nodes.authors: Tian, Moyi*; Rodríguez, Nancy; Bortz, David; Mohler, George; Brantingham, P. Jeffrey
nodes.abstract: With the increasing use of social media, the boundary between the online and offline worlds becomes blurred. Activities in one domain strongly influence the other. From the online world, we see social media transforming offline life, from everyday decisions to the escalation of conflicts. Conversely, offline events in the physical world drive online discussion and engagement. It is thus essential to understand the mechanisms underlying the coupled dynamics of online and offline social activities.  We present our attempts at disentangling this problem from two perspectives. (1) Model-driven: We propose a framework for modeling the population-level dynamics of participation on coupled online and offline layers, where agents interact through online networks. We develop corresponding mean-field approximations that allow outburst analysis using the reproductive number. To link mathematical models with the real world, we complement our study with data-driven approaches. (2) Data-driven: First, motivated by the high activity and vast influence of bots on the internet, we infer mathematical models for information spread among multi-agent large language models (LLMs) in response to real-world events, where mean-field models have proven to be an effective framework. Second, we use system identification, specifically Weak Form Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (WSINDy), to learn models from noisy data, allowing us to identify effective continuum models as approximations of stochastic processes on networks.  Overall, our studies demonstrate initial steps toward bridging simulation, modeling, and data-driven learning for coupled online–offline social dynamics.  [1] Moyi Tian, P. Jeffrey Brantingham, and Nancy Rodríguez. Modelling the spillover from online engagement to offline protest: stochastic dynamics and mean-field approximations on networks. Journal of Complex Networks, in press (2025).

nodes.title: The dynamics of the Reddit collective action leading to the GameStop short squeeze
nodes.authors: Desiderio, Antonio; Aiello, Luca; Cimini, Giulio*; Alessandretti, Laura
nodes.abstract: Online social media has transformed financial markets by accelerating the spread of information, sentiment and investment strategies, and by enabling new forms of collective behavior among retail investors [1,2]. While these platforms can enhance price discovery, they also amplify behavioral biases and shared narratives, particularly during periods of market stress and heightened attention. This study [1] investigates the January 2021 GameStop (GME) short squeeze, largely driven by the Reddit community WallStreetBets (WSB), to determine whether users merely reacted to ongoing market movements, or actively coordinated a financial collective action, therefore anticipating market dynamics. We analyze the temporal interplay among stock price movements, trading volume, WSB activity, and public attention using both linear methods (Granger causality, Vector Autoregression) and nonlinear techniques (Detrended Cross-Correlation Analysis, Convergent Cross-Mapping). Our results show that WSB activity systematically forecast price and volume surges in GME and related stocks, and that the episode unfolded through three distinct phases: discussion, action, and visibility. A sharp transition occurred on January 13, when WSB activity began to anticipate changes in GME trading volume, marking the shift from decentralized online debate to coordinated market action. At this stage, the collective financial exposure of WSB users became economically significant, and the subsequent surge of broader investor participation amplified the market impact. The event entered a global visibility phase following Elon Musk’s January 27 tweet. Overall, we show that online communities can act as catalysts for coordinated collective action with direct, measurable effects on financial markets. These findings highlight the growing systemic relevance of socially coordinated retail investors and underline the importance of fine-grained temporal analysis for understanding the evolving structure and dynamics of modern financial systems.

nodes.title: When a New Line Helps and the Others Harm: Network Determinants of Grid Stability
nodes.authors: CHOI, YUNJU*; Kim, Heetae; Lee, Daekyung
nodes.abstract: Modern power grids face growing structural complexity driven by increased renewable and distributed power generation, creating substantial challenges for maintaining grid stability. While a new transmission line construction seems to reinforce the power grid stability, the consequences of the new line placement for the synchronization performance of the power grid remain elusive. Critically, reinforcing a wrong line could trigger catastrophic chain reactions, small overloads escalating to cascading blackouts, where well intentioned additions paradoxically worsen system performance (Braess’s paradox). We investigate how transmission line additions affect synchronization across scenarios using the second order Kuramoto model and basin stability analysis with systematically varied network size, power distribution, and topology. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals a robust placement principle: connecting power centers of generation and consumption clusters consistently improves stability across all configurations by up to 75%. Critically, we identify context dependent phenomena where suboptimal placements specifically, connecting nodes at cluster edges trigger Braess’s paradox, degrading stability by up to 49%. These findings contradict the conventional belief that additional transmission will naturally strengthen grids, demonstrating that poorly positioned lines can paradoxically reduce stability. Our results highlight critical considerations for transmission planning through comprehensive observations across diverse network topologies and operational conditions.

nodes.title: The Functional Advantage of Dimeric Architecture in Photosystem II
nodes.authors: Kim, Jonghoon*; Kim, Eunchul; Kim, Heetae
nodes.abstract: Green plants capture sunlight and convert it into chemical energy through photosynthesis. This process relies on Photosystem II (PSII), where light energy absorbed by chlorophylls is transferred to reaction centers (RCs) for charge separation. PSII consists of protein subunits that hold numerous chlorophylls, forming an energy transfer network that guides excitation energy toward two RCs. This complex exhibits a symmetric and dimeric architecture with two identical RCs [1](Fig 1(a)), yet the functional advantage of this structural arrangement remains unclear. Here, we apply network analysis to investigate how this dimeric structure contributes to robust performance under various RC failure scenarios. We modeled PSII as a network in which individual pigments serve as nodes and energy transfer pathways serve as weighted edges, with charge separation at RCs and heat dissipation representing terminal sinks. By systematically analyzing network flow dynamics under different single-RC operational states, we quantified changes in energy transfer efficiency. We examined three functional states characterized by their charge separation rates: fully functional (0.33 ps−1, the open state)[2], partially inhibited (0.005 ps−1, the closed state)[3], and completely inactive (0 ps−1, the damaged state). Our analysis reveals that when one RC is closed(OC) or damaged(OD) while the other remains open, the overall efficiency is maintained at approximately 78.30 − 81.32% relative to when both RCs are open(OO)(Fig 1(b)). This robustness demonstrates that the open RC can effectively compensate for its closed or damaged counterpart. Furthermore, non- photochemical quenching (NPQ)—heat dissipation for photoprotection—increases as the system becomes more closed, indicating a balance between energy utilization and protective dissipation(Fig 1(c)). Critically, we identified the structural basis for this robustness by analyzing energy transfer pathways under different RC states. When both RCs are open, energy flows through the network via symmetric and distributed pathways across the dimeric structure(Fig 1(d)). However, when one RC becomes closed or damaged, a shortcut pathway emerges that directly connects nodes on the impaired side to those on the functional side, effectively rerouting excitation energy toward the open RC(Fig 1(e), red line). These findings demonstrate that PSII’s dimeric architecture functions as a fault-tolerant network, illustrating how symmetric redundancy in biological systems can ensure robust performance under component failure.

nodes.title: The Stage and the Closet: Visibility Regimes of Pornography on Twitter Networks
nodes.abstract: Social media trace data are central to computational social science, yet a growing literature warns that sharing logs reflect filtered visibility rather than actual consumption. We investigate how "visibility regimes"—shaped by stigma, platform governance, and economic incentives—distort sharing-based domain–similarity networks, and how these distortions can mislead inferences about underlying behavior.  We use pornography sharing on Twitter (X) as an extreme test case. Pornography is widely consumed but highly stigmatized, subject to strict platform governance (e.g., age-gates, shadow bans), and tightly coupled to monetization models that reward visibility. Consequently, public sharing is a strategic, high-cost choice, restricted primarily to a small subset of accounts (creators, promotional networks) navigating these constraints.  Employing large-scale domain-sharing data linked to estimated demographic indicators, we quantify visibility distortion by comparing volume, concentration (Gini), and group over-representation across creator-oriented (e.g., OnlyFans), consumer-oriented (e.g., Pornhub), and mainstream entertainment domains. We further construct a domain–similarity network where nodes represent domains and weighted edges encode the cosine similarity of their demographic profiles, allowing us to visualize a backbone of the most visible domains connected by strong similarity ties. The resulting network comprises 27,414 nodes and 111,481 edges, dominated by a giant component of 26,242 nodes. Within this structure, we visualize a backbone of the most visible domains connected by strong similarity ties.  Our results suggest a sharp visibility filter. Relative to mainstream entertainment, porn platforms appear rare in public traces and highly concentrated in who shares them. Pornhub emerges as "The Closet": it generates high estimated traffic but exhibits low, moderately concentrated public sharing. In contrast, OnlyFans functions as "The Stage": despite lower traffic, it shows extraordinarily concentrated sharing consistent with a small promoter elite. Notably, inferred user profiles suggest OnlyFans sharers are strongly male-skewed and disproportionately Black relative to demographic baselines, indicating potential intersectional structures in stigma costs and promotional incentives. In the domain–similarity network, OnlyFans appears to occupy a distinct lifestyle cluster tied to sports and games, indicating the cultural worlds in which sexual visibility is normalized.  We introduce visibility regimes as a network-level property of stigmatized behaviors and demonstrate how inequality, demographic skew, and network position can decouple promotion from consumption. This framework offers a lens for analyzing other high-cost or stigmatized visibility behaviors—such as misinformation, gambling, or self-harm content—that occupy distinct positions in the stigma–governance–incentive space.

nodes.title: Optimal dismantling of directed networks
nodes.abstract: As a fundamental problem in network science, network dismantling focuses on identifying a set of critical nodes whose removal sharply reduces a network's connectivity and functionality. Potential applications include stopping rumor spread, blocking sentiment propagation, and controlling epidemics and pandemics. Previous studies have mainly focused on undirected networks, whereas many real-world networks are inherently directed, such as the World Wide Web and the global trade system. Moreover, the functionality of directed networks depends on the giant strongly connected component (GSCC), where nodes are mutually reachable. Considering both the directionality and heterogeneity of these networks, we propose a novel centrality measure—network incoherence (NI) centrality—and develop a trophic analysis-based dismantling (TAD) method, in which nodes are removed in descending order according to their NI centrality scores, aiming to efficiently dismantle directed networks by reducing the GSCC. When applied to a wide range of benchmark synthetic networks with varying degree heterogeneity and 15 real-world directed networks, our TAD method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Significantly, TAD also induces the largest maximum avalanches during the dismantling process, highlighting its ability to capture structurally critical nodes. These findings provide new insight into the structure-function relationship of directed networks and inform the design of more resilient systems against perturbations.

nodes.title: Uncovering simultaneous breakthroughs with a robust measure of disruptiveness
nodes.authors: Kim, Munjung; Kojaku, Sadamori; Ahn, Yong-Yeol*
nodes.abstract: Progress in science and technology is punctuated by disruptive innovations and breakthroughs. Identifying such contributions has attracted significant attention, yet challenges remain, such as incorporating complex, indirect impacts and properly attributing disruptiveness when simultaneous discoveries happen. Addressing these challenges, we introduce an embedding-based measure of disruptiveness (EDM). Conceptually, EDM estimates how far the “future” of a focal study (represented by the embeddings of the papers that cite the focal study) is from the “past” (the embeddings of the references of the focal study). By quantifying the distance between these two representations (denoted as ∆ here), EDM captures the extent to which the subsequent scientific trajectory from the focal paper departs from prior work (see Fig. 1A). This approach not only leverages non-local, multi-step patterns in the citation network to capture a broader and richer spectrum of transformative influence, but also remains robust to noise while maintaining high resolution. Applying EDM to more than 50 million scientific publications, we demonstrate that the measure substantially improves upon a widely used local-structure-based indicator, the disruption index [1](denoted as D). First, EDM more effectively identifies canonical landmark contributions, including Nobel Prize–winning papers (Fig. 1B), primarily by being robust against the impact of structural artifacts such as simultaneous discovery or multi-part contributions (Fig. 1C). Second, EDM provides substantially higher resolution than the disruption index, which exhibits pronounced degeneracy from its reliance on paper counts. Third, the learned embedding space enables the identification of simultaneous discovery pairs, which tend to have similar future vectors (cited similarly). Using APS (American Physical Society) data, we identify approximately 50 such pairs, illustrating how shared future trajectories can reveal common underlying contributions that are difficult to detect using local measures alone (Fig. 1D). Together, these results demonstrate that EDM offers a quantitative framework for capturing transformative scientific influence by exploiting higher-order information encoded in the citation network.

nodes.title: Robustness & Generalization of Uncertainty-Aware Message Passing Neural Networks
nodes.authors: Chernikova, Alesia; Laber, Moritz*; Sabhahit, Narayan; Eliassi-Rad, Tina
nodes.abstract: Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) are the central architecture in graph machine learning. Numerous studies have characterized their theoretical properties, such as expressivity and generalization. A central assumption in previous work is that node features and network structure are known with absolute certainty. However, in real-world applications, node features and network structure may be uncertain due to noise, finite measurement precision, or coarse-graining. Here, we address uncertainty in node features in the context of MPNNs [1]. First, we show how uncertainty in input node features translates into uncertainty in node embeddings and downstream predictions by adapting moment-propagation techniques to MPNNs. Second, we use the moments of the node embedding distribution to establish a node-wise certified adversarial robustness radii in L2-norm. Specifically, we compute a ball in feature space around each node such that, for feature perturbations within a ball of this radius, the node will still be correctly classified with a fixed probability. Finally, we establish generalization bounds for the Simplified Graph Convolution (SGC) architecture on inductive node-level tasks by defining a new optimal transport-based pseudo-metric, the Feature Convolution Distance (FCDp). SGC is an MPNN without nonlinear activation functions between hidden layers. FCD_p is defined in the space of nodes of attributed graphs. This distance allows us to extend existing results on covering number-based generalization bounds [2] for MPNNs to the case of uncertain node features. Our result bounds the maximal difference between the empirical loss distribution on a given dataset and the population loss distribution under the data-generating process in terms of properties of the sample space and the architecture. In all three cases—moment propagation, robustness, and generalization—we validate our theoretical contributions with experiments on seven real-world networks and synthetic data with a given noise-correlation structure. For moment propagation, we find that, when considering approximation error and computational efficiency, different methods are preferable depending on network size. For robustness, we find that we can establish certificates for a majority of nodes in the network, but these radii can be small compared to the size of adversarial attacks necessary to induce node misclassification, meaning the method tends to underestimate the robustness of MPNNs. For generalization, we show that FCD_1 in the space of nodes correlates strongly with the distance in embedding space and that SGC is Lipschitz continuous in this distance. In addition, we empirically test the tightness of the generalization bound. Together, these findings show that a proper treatment of node feature uncertainty in graph machine learning requires new theoretical tools but provides insights into model robustness and guarantees for within-distribution performance.  [1] Chernikova A. et al. (2025) Uncertainty-Aware Message Passing Neural Networks; NPGML NeurIPS 2025 [2] Vasileiou A. et al. (2025) Survey on Generalization Theory for Graph Neural Networks; arXiv:2503.15650

nodes.title: Universal expansion of human mobility across urban scales
nodes.abstract: Human mobility reflects interactions between individuals and their environments, yet the influence of egocentric preferences—particularly home location—remains poorly understood. Using millions of anonymized mobile phone trajectories from multiple countries and demographic groups, we develop a network-based framework that decomposes individual mobility into spatial modules. We uncover a universal scaling relationship, the inflation law, in which the module radius increases sublinearly with distance from home,  $r_c \sim d_c^\kappa$, consistently across regions and populations. Analysis of movements within and between modules reveals heterogeneous mobility dynamics, challenging assumptions of uniform movement behavior. Incorporating a biologically inspired switch mechanism, our model reproduces both the modular network structure and observed spatial patterns. These findings highlight the central role of home-centered constraints in shaping mobility and have implications for urban planning, traffic prediction, and disease modeling.

nodes.title: Controlling the spread of deception-based cyber-threats on time-varying networks
nodes.abstract: Deception-based attacks such as phishing, baiting, and file masquerading have become one of the most diffuse types of cyber-threats. These are designed around ingenious strategies that target human nature. For example, the classic phishing scheme tries, via trusted access, to lure victims to open a malicious link and/or to download a malware which might affect personal accounts and devices, reveal sensitive data, and, unbeknownst to the victims, allow the threat to spread further. The literature devoted at modelling these processes and gathering insights to contrast them is vast, but presents two main limitations. First, most of previous work neglects the temporal nature of online contact patterns focusing instead on aggregated networks. Second, most of the literature assumes users to be equally susceptible (i.e., gullible). Here, we study the efficacy of strategies aimed at controlling the spread of deception-based cyber-threats unfolding on online social networks. We model directed and temporal interactions between users using a family of activity-driven networks featuring different gullibility classes and tunable homophily levels among them. We simulate the spreading of cyber-threats using classic Susceptible-Infected-Susceptible (SIS) models. We explore and quantify the effectiveness of four control strategies. Akin to vaccination campaigns with a limited budget, each strategy selects a fraction of nodes with the aim to increase their awareness and provide protection from cyber-threats. The first strategy picks nodes randomly. The second assumes global knowledge of the system selecting nodes based on their activity. The third picks nodes via egocentric sampling by using the connections of a fraction of nodes that act as probes. The fourth selects nodes based on the outcome of standard security awareness tests (SAT), customarily used by institutions to probe, estimate, and raise the awareness of their workforce. We quantify the impact of each strategy by deriving analytically how they affect the spreading threshold. Analytical expressions are validated via large-scale numerical simulations (see Figure 1). Interestingly, we find that targeted strategies, focusing on key features of the population such as the activity, are extremely effective. Egocentric sampling strategies, though not as effective, emerge as clear second best despite not assuming any knowledge about the system. In addition, we find that networks characterized by highly homophilic interactions linked to gullibility might expand the range of transmissibility parameters that allows for macroscopic outbreaks. At the same time, they reduce the reach of these spreading events. Hence, rather isolated patches of the network formed by highly gullible individuals might provide fertile grounds for the propagation and survival of cyber-threats.

nodes.title: Bounded-confidence models with two-state node activations
nodes.authors: Yu, Shilong; Chu, Weiqi; Masuda, Naoki*
nodes.abstract: Please see attached pdf.

nodes.title: Detecting Global Trade Vulnerabilities via Graph Neural Networks
nodes.abstract: The past century has witnessed an unprecedented globalization of the world economy, binding nations together through intricate commercial relationships. The destruction of trading ties -- whether due to accidents, conflicts, or policy changes -- can negatively impact the livelihood of millions of people, often significantly so. Understanding precisely how trade disruptions spread and which nations stand most vulnerable can provide valuable insights for policymakers aiming to protect their population from economic disasters. Traditional macroeconomic tools -- while useful for broad economic assessments -- are ill-suited to describe the systemic vulnerabilities lurking inside complex systems -- such as global trade. Complex systems exhibit behaviors that cannot be explained solely by analyzing their individual parts in isolation.  We represent international trade as a complex network, to enhance the prediction of economic vulnerabilities. Specifically, we frame the problem as a node classification issue: given a network representing exports (edges) by countries (nodes), can we label a country as either "safe" or "at risk'"? With this framing, we can sketch an approach by deploying graph neural networks (GNN). We choose to use GNNs because they excel in the node classification task. We enrich the representation with a number of economically-relevant attributes on both nodes and edges, creating node-attributed multilayer directed networks, where layers represent exports in different commodities. The node and edge features we consider include: the economic complexity [1] of both the countries and the products, the geographical location of the countries, trade agreements, corruption levels, and more. For ablation purposes, we also make versions of this network without edge direction and represented as multi-graph instead of multilayer, to test how performance varies (Figure 1(a)).   For the research question "Given the trade links a country has, what is the likelihood they will experience a trade disruption in a specific commodity class this year?" we define a disruption if an importer either stops buying a product or is paying more to get the same amount of that product. This is a hard question to answer, because disruptions are not necessarily embedded in the network -- like wars, natural disasters, and so on. Figure 1(b) nonetheless shows we can not only outperform a random classifier, but also a neural network that does not take the topology of the trade network into account. This advances the hypothesis that such trade disruptions might leave early warning signs in the choices countries make for their trading partners.  The easier question "Given the trade links a country has, what is the likelihood they will experience any trade disruption this year?" provides us with more training data and therefore higher predictive power (Figure 1(c)). Also in this case, we can outperform both random expectation a neural network classifiers.  While only a starting point, our work provides an interesting avenue to estimate the vulnerability of global trade, by using our prediction scores to create a global trade risk outlook. Such a risk score is meaningful, as we can see traces of real world events in it. With the Russo-Ukrainian war as a test case, we see that the countries we label as "high risk" are much more likely to be affected than the low risk countries (27.5% vs 4% for selected products highly dependent on Russia as an exporter). We can also see that the countries we predicted to be at risk but not being affected are those who are defying international sanctions, which validates our method as it shows the reasons for the failed predictions. In future work, we can expand our method by including more features such as conflicts, natural disasters, shared history (colonial or cultural similarities) and more.  [1] Hausmann, Ricardo, César A.
