Hidalgo, Sebastián Bustos, Michele Coscia, and Alexander Simoes. The atlas of economic complexit

nodes.title: Measuring the Scapegoat Mechanism in Temporal Online Networks via Concentration and Relinking Signatures
nodes.abstract: Scapegoating episodes on social media often combine abrupt attention concentration on a target with rapid reorganization of interaction patterns. Yet scapegoating is rarely operationalized in a way that is both measurable in temporal networks and comparable to mechanistic models. We present a mixed-methods protocol that bridges mimetic crisis theory and network science by (i) extracting a compact set of temporal “bridge metrics” from real online interaction networks and (ii) validating these signatures against a parsimonious small-world agent-based model (ABM).  We analyze four Brazilian cancellation episodes on X/Twitter (2017–2025) and construct directed temporal interaction graphs from retweets, replies, quotes, and mentions. Edges are labeled by stance (e.g., accuser vs. skeptic/defender) to quantify segregation and cross-community overflow. We compute concentration and power-structure metrics including engagement Gini (per user and per tweet), in-degree and Freeman centralization, assortativity by stance, peak indicators (e.g., peak/median), top- 𝑘 k participation shares, and distance-to-victim measures that capture isolation residues. In parallel, we implement an ABM on a small-world topology in which collective tension grows endogenously, leaders emerge contingently, victims and substitute victims are selected, and a “rite” temporarily relieves tension while rewiring ties; we also vary the proportion of skeptic agents.  To compare empirical and synthetic dynamics, we align empirical time series at the event peak and ABM series at the rite tick, and evaluate similarity across time-series trajectories and distributional summaries. Across cases, empirical networks exhibit star-like concentration profiles characterized by simultaneous increases in inequality (Gini) and centralization, consistent with focal convergence on a target. The ABM reproduces the qualitative regime shifts observed in the data—pre-rite multi-community structures followed by post-rite re-centralization—and skeptic agents systematically dampen spread and reduce stance assortativity. These results suggest that scapegoating is detectable through robust temporal signatures combining concentration and relinking/isolation patterns, enabling reproducible measurement and supporting early-warning monitoring rules for online harassment dynamics.

nodes.title: How our bones build networks: interplay between architecture and growth
nodes.authors: Tits, Alexandra; Fenwick, Jack; Buenzli, Pascal; Weinkamer, Richard*
nodes.abstract: Many biological tissues are permeated by a physical network of fluid-filled channels that facilitates not only transport but also mediates signaling [1] and mechanosensation [2]. In bone, this network – known as lacunocanalicular network (LCN) (Fig. 1a) consists of lacunae, which serve as hubs (Fig. 1b), and canaliculi, narrow channels connecting them. This porosity network accommodates the osteocyte network (cell bodies and their dendrites). Characteristics of the osteocyte network are similar to the neural network of our brains: one cubic centimeter of human bone comprises 74 kilometers of canaliculi [3]. Our research focus is on human osteons - cylindrical building blocks of bone with a diameter of approximately 300 µm (Fig. 1a). During our lifetime, osteons are constantly formed: a cylindrical cavity is first created, then progressively filled by bone-forming cells, which deposit concentric layers of bone lamellae (Fig. 1d). The process terminates leaving a central canal, which remains open to house a blood vessel for nutrient supply. During osteon closure, some of the bone-forming cells get embedded into the bone matrix, while maintaining connections with cells at the bone surface, becoming osteocytes and thereby building up a network structure. Given the variability in the shape of osteons, we ask: How can bone formation velocity influence the resulting network architecture? Can we understand the network formation dynamics under the hypothesis that the existing network structure guides the embedding of new bone cells?  We have developed an experimental workflow that transforms three-dimensional images of the LCN, acquired with confocal microscopy, into a spatial network graph consisting of tens of thousands of nodes and edges. Using the custom-built software TINA [3] we quantify network architecture in terms of density and spatial heterogeneity. Our analysis of human osteons with non-circular shape (Fig. 1c, d) revealed that regions with higher bone deposition (typically along the long axis of ellipsoidal geometries) exhibit denser network (compared to regions along the short axis).  To explore the dynamics of bone deposition, we developed a mathematical model of network growth within an osteon. This model simulates the embedment of a bone-forming cell, becoming an osteocyte - i.e. creating a new hub in the network, under different hypotheses. Notably, when we assume that bone-forming cells with the weakest connectivity to the already existing network are preferentially becoming new network hubs, the model generates network architectures resembling those observed in experimental data.   Our analysis of the bone network structure suggests that cells carry information about their position during their embedment. This enables them to actively adapt the network architecture to ensure sufficient connectivity to the source of nutrients in the central canal. Combined with the result from our model that the existing network guides the embedding of new cells, these findings indicate a strong dynamic interplay between network architecture and growth.   [1] Mehrpooya, A., Challis, V.J. and Buenzli, P.R., 2024, Proceedings A 480, 20230906. [2] Van Tol, A.F., Schemenz, V., …, Willie, B.M. and Weinkamer, R., 2020, PNAS 117, 32251. [3] Repp, F., Kollmannsberger, …, Wagermaier, W. and Weinkamer, R., 2017, Bone Reports 6, 101.

nodes.title: Knowledge-Based Gene Set Intersection Graphs Reveal Functional Master Regulators by Measures of Centrality and Self-Similarity
nodes.abstract: Our current study leverages computational and network-science methods to reveal molecular insights into gene function by analyzing knowledge-based networks derived from biomedical literature. Specifically, we aimed to determine how individual gene functions can be inferred based on their topological integration into complex networks constructed from curated gene signatures reported in approximately 4,000 scientific publications (MSigDB C2: canonical pathways, chemical and genetic perturbations) [1,2]. Using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) [3] topic modeling, we identified 27 distinct biomedical contexts, including prominent themes such as 'molecular cancer gene signatures', 'Wnt and TGFβ signaling pathways', and 'hematopoiesis and leukemogenesis'. Gene sets corresponding to each biomedical context were transformed into complex intersection-based networks, where modularity analyses and functional annotation uncovered biologically meaningful clusters. Comparative topological analyses across all networks revealed universal connectivity patterns linked to fundamental cellular processes, alongside specialized network configurations that reflect unique biomedical contexts. Centrality [4] and local self-similarity [5] measures were applied to pinpoint genes crucial to network structure and functionality. Perturbation analyses (network rewiring simulations) further assessed the reliability of these topological metrics, particularly highlighting understudied genes with potentially significant biological roles. We developed a user-friendly web application for interactive exploration of these networks. By connecting computational biology and network theory, our approach demonstrates how network topology can illuminate gene function and regulation in complex biological systems, contributing valuable tools and insights to the NetSci2026.

nodes.title: Mapping the Bureaucratic Topology of Urban Data Access
nodes.abstract: The transition from Kevin Lynch's "legible city" (where urban form is cognitively and directly readable by inhabitants) to the "accessible city" of platform urbanism marks a fundamental epistemic shift in how urban knowledge is produced and distributed (Hamilton et al., 2014). Where Lynch's city waited to be read through embodied navigation, the smart city waits to be accessed through APIs, datasets, and remote sensing infrastructures. Yet this accessibility is unevenly distributed: while open data initiatives promise transparency, significant layers of urban information remain locked behind institutional, technical, and bureaucratic barriers. Data accessibility therefore emerges not as a binary condition but as a stratified privilege, structured by technological literacy, institutional position, security concerns and economic capacity. This raises a critical question for network science: if urban systems are increasingly mediated by data flows, what are the topological properties of the networks that govern access to those flows?  This research investigates the network structure of data accessibility by treating the bureaucratic process of requesting urban datasets as itself a network to be mapped. Using Grand Paris open data portal as a starting point, we identify datasets relevant to urban design and development, then systematically contact stakeholders through email (such as municipal departments, private operators, state agencies) to request access to more granular or complete information layers. Over a two-month period, these email exchanges will generate a relational dataset: each request, response, referral, and non-response constitutes an edge in an emergent network of data governance. We analyse this network using standard centrality measures (degree, betweenness, closeness) to identify institutional gatekeepers; response time distributions to characterise bureaucratic friction; and referral chain analysis to map the topology of data silos. Qualitative coding of response content (justification types, tone, alternative pathways offered) supplements the quantitative structure. The resulting network visualisation represents what we call a "bureaucratic topology" (graph structure that reveals how urban data access is institutionally mediated), where bottlenecks occur and which actors occupy positions of informational control. Preliminary analysis suggests that data governance networks exhibit high clustering (siloed departments) combined with low global connectivity (few cross-institutional pathways), creating structural barriers to integrated urban practices.  The governance of urban data infrastructures has typically been studied through qualitative policy analysis. This research proposes a methodological inversion: rather than analysing urban datasets, we analyse the institutional process of requesting them, treating beaurocratic correspondance as a primary data source. This action-based research approach generates a network that would otherwise remain invisible, one that no existing dataset documents. By applying graph methods to this emergent structure, the methodology enables quantitative comparison of data governance across cities, sectors and institutional contexts, offering a scalable and replicable frameworks for studying platform urbanism's hidden architecture. The work contributes to network science by demonstrating how network analysis can render legible the very systems that determine what urban information becomes accessible and how.

nodes.title: When AI Becomes Urban Compass: Socio-Spatial Biases in LLM-based Place Recommendations
nodes.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly becoming everyday interfaces for decision-making, from choosing restaurants to planning trips. As these systems begin to mediate human interactions within urban networks, their biases may inadvertently reshape socio-spatial patterns. In this project, we present a systematic analysis of representation and demographic biases in LLM-based place recommendations.   We prompt an LLM to generate restaurant recommendations across neighborhoods in different cities, including Boston and New York. For each query, we simulate realistic user profiles by sampling synthetic individuals from census data, each defined by a home location, age, sex, and household income. We conduct two complementary analyses: (1) Neighborhood-level analysis: We examine how match rates vary across urban areas with distinct socio-demographic compositions. Our results show that LLMs produce uneven match rates across neighborhoods, favoring areas with higher population density, larger proportions bachelor-degree residents, smaller proportions of black residents, and better walkability. These findings suggest that the LLM’s “mental map” of the city is biased toward well-documented, affluent, and digitally visible areas. (2) Demographic-level analysis: We examine how recommendations differ for users of different income, age, and gender groups, controlling for spatial location. Our results reveal that low-income users are steered toward low-spending, high-frequency, crowded places, whereas female users are directed slightly farther and to more frequented places. This reflects the inherent biases carried by LLMs, potentially reinforcing existing biases in urban space.  By revealing how representation and demographic biases are interwoven in LLM-based place recommendations, we offer a new lens for understanding algorithmic mediation of urban life. As LLMs become increasingly embedded in human mobility and consumption, their internal representations may become a hidden layer of the city’s socio-spatial network, subtly steering where different groups go and what kinds of places they discover. Such biased mediation can potentially erode the network fabric, reinforcing inequalities through social segregation and uneven local business survival.

nodes.title: Wikipedia and AI for Mapping Cultural Diaspora
nodes.authors: Herrera Guzman, Yessica*; Peng, You; Lamba, Manika
nodes.abstract: Cultural diaspora emerges from long-term patterns of human mobility and institutional opportunity [1]. While prior work has examined cultural diffusion using historical datasets [2], much relevant information remains in unstructured sources. In this exploratory study, we structure data from public web sources using an AI-based pipeline to construct and analyze mobility networks to map cultural diaspora. Our goal is to assess whether AI tools can structure text data and classify entities in domain-specific fields to support systematic studies of how migration contributes to the globalization of cultural expressions. In particular, we use Wikipedia as a global knowledge repository that encodes historical information in a relatively standardized format. We use classical ballet as a case study because it is a historically rich and highly transnational performing art, with well-documented patterns of migration and artistic production that lend themselves to network analysis. Beyond data availability, ballet is a remarkable example of a dance form that evolved from an elite European tradition into a culturally mainstream global art, despite the wide diversity of dance practices worldwide.  We scrape data from 518 ballet titles listed on the English Wikipedia’s ‘List of ballets by title’ (accessed March 15, 2024 [3]). To structure the data, we input the text body of Wikipedia ballet entries into GPT-based models via the OpenAI API. In specific, we use prompt templates that instruct the GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 models to identify named artists, creative roles, countries of activity, and production-related attributes from each Wikipedia entry and to output these fields in a fixed tabular format, which are then compiled into a structured CSV file and evaluated against a manually verified subset of entries. Through this process, we extracted 1,042 artist names spanning multiple creative roles (27% composers, 22% choreographers, 19% designers, 12% muses, 7% librettists, 2% regisseurs, and 11% unspecified). The resulting structured data enabled the construction of the geography-based artists network (Fig. 1a).  The historical trajectory of ballet production highlights the migration of ballet’s creative center from France and Italy to Russia, Britain, and ultimately the United States, which currently dominates ballet production alongside increasing activity in other regions (Fig. 1b). Through network metrics, we identify a small set of globally connected artists that mediate a large fraction of transnational creative flows, sustaining ballet as a globally connected art form. Moreover, Wikipedia correctly captures canonical figures such as Igor Stravinsky and George Balanchine as high-centrality nodes, reflecting their roles in transferring artistic practices from Eastern Europe to the United States. This work demonstrates that narrative public web data, when structured with AI and combined with network analysis, can recover meaningful patterns of creative mobility and support large-scale studies of cultural diaspora across artistic fields.

nodes.title: Investigating the Drivers of Information Source Popularity in Telegram Communities Through Forwarding Networks
nodes.abstract: The messaging app Telegram has become a popular digital space for information sharing outside of large social media platforms [1, 3]. In particular, Telegram’s channel function enables one-to-many broadcasting to hundreds of thousands of platform users [2], while message forwarding allows channels to exchange information. Forwarding between channels is frequent, accounting for a fifth to a third of all messages in public channels on average [1, 2]. Such forwards more frequently contain external links than non-forwarded messages, emphasizing Telegram’s role as an information hub [2]. Understanding how different information sources achieve visibility is fundamental to characterizing the information ecosystem of digital platforms. However, conventional approaches of measuring popularity rely on aggregate metrics such as the frequencies or total views of sources [3], which can mask fundamentally different diffusion mechanisms. We demonstrate that the combined analysis of network topology and engagement metrics reveals distinct pathways to visibility that engagement metrics alone cannot capture. Using message data from German and English public Telegram channels in the publicly available TeleScope dataset [1], we construct domain-specific forwarding networks where nodes represent channels and directed edges represent forwarding relationships. We identify distinct models of source popularity that differ in both network topology and forwarding behavior. Low-amplification reference patterns show frequent sharing but low to moderate forwarding, such as the British outlet ‘independent.co.uk’ (2% forwards), the German outlet ‘spiegel.de’ (6% forwards), or the US outlet ‘nypost.com’ (15% forwards). Hub-centric amplification sources achieve visibility through concentrated networks with moderate to high forwarding rates. For the US outlet ‘thegatewaypundit.com’ (31% forwards), a single channel originates 16% of all forwards and accounts for 42% of all views. For the German outlet ‘auf1.tv’ (Figure 1, left; 54% forwards), 41% of all forwards originate from one channel accounting for 83% of all views. Multi-community utility sources show distributed forwarding across many channels. For ‘youtube.com’ (Figure 1, right; 24% forwards), the top forwarding channel accounts for only 1% of forwards and the most popular channel for 5% of all views. These topological differences reflect platform engagement strategies: the outlet ‘auf1.tv’ actively leverages Telegram for distribution, while ‘youtube.com’ is passively referenced as an external information hub. Ultimately, our findings advance the empirical understanding of how Telegram is shaped as a platform within the digital information ecosystem.  [1] Susmita Gangopadhyay et al. “TeleScope A Longitudinal Dataset for Investigating Online Discourse and Information Interaction on Telegram”. In: Proceedings of the ICWSM (June 2025). DOI: 10.1609/icwsm.v19i1.35945.  [2] Mohamad Hoseini et al. “Characterizing Information Propagation in Fringe Communities on Telegram”. In: Proceedings of the ICWSM (May 2024). DOI: 10.1609/icwsm.v18i1.31336.  [3] Silvan Wehrli et al. “Information Pathways and Voids in Critical German Online Communities During the COVID-19 Vaccination Discourse: Cross-Platform and Mixed Methods Analysis”. In: JMIR (Oct. 2025). DOI: 10.2196/76309.

nodes.title: Topological data analysis of multiplex Markov chains encoding brain activity across frequency bands
nodes.abstract: Alzheimer's Disease (AD), the most common of dementia, affected millions of people worldwide is becoming the burdensome not just for individual but for the whole society. In our work, we utilized tools from topological data analysis visualized through persistent barcodes or Persistence Diagrams to automate the detection and summary of convection cycles in Alzheimer's dataset that can arise for irreversible Multiplex Markov chains (MMCs) constructed by coupling together sets of Markov chains (MCs). Specifically, we place the TDA-interpolation through the total imbalance of induced network that is described as a single-layer net to explore the homological varieties. Our pathway had been proven as a robust approach in distinguishing normal people and AD patients via topological toolsets as well as a relaxation in computational cost by playing with just one-layer network. Alzheimer's disease, as well as the others progressive neuro-degenerative disease, is increasingly received high attention via various approaches. Among these methods, considering the corresponding models under the prism of multilayer network is carved through a bunch of recent deployments. However, combining between Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and Multiplex Markov chains (MMCs) is still unveiled. Our article will discover the structural changes in multiscale way and compare the differences in homological characteristics between the normal person group and Alzheimer's group. The survey is not just to make the visualization hinging on varying of coupling strength but also the time-steps for convergence. Furthermore, the birth- and the death-time of these groups will be emphasized via the Bottleneck distance, a critical measurement in computational topology. Our TDA-assisted approach makes a promise for opening a bunch of potential developments in combining various disciplines. Notably, our approach convinces the usefulness of the utilization of PH into portraying the structural changes in a MMCs via quantifying the total imbalance of networks. It not just unifies unprecedentedly these different disciplinary into the specific application, but also tackling the challenge of the computational cost as facing with a large number of nodes scattering into various layers.

nodes.title: Community Detection in Gene Co-methylation Networks Reveals Modules Associated with Human Aging
nodes.authors: Sadat Fatemi Nasrollahi, Fatemeh; Vila Miñana, Meritxell*; Beshkar, Nikki; DeMeo, Dawn; Fortunato, Santo
nodes.abstract: DNA methylation patterns provide a powerful window into the biological aging process. In this work, we aim to identify genes and pathways associated with aging by constructing gene-level networks from human source DNA methylation data from 282 samples. We use k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and linear correlation thresholding to construct the networks and apply Leiden community detection to recover gene modules. We then characterize the functional pathways of these modules and systematically compare the findings with those obtained from Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA). Figure 1 demonstrates the pipeline implemented (top panel) and the results of this analysis (bottom panel). We begin with the normalized human blood methylation matrix and construct interpretable gene-level features by grouping CpGs by genes and extracting each gene’s first principal component. Using this gene-level representation, we implement two methods of constructing networks--kNN and linear thresholding. Leiden is then applied to identify modules in these networks; this is a flexible multi-scale framework: kNN allows tuning via k, linear thresholding via the correlation cutoff, and Leiden via its resolution parameter, enabling exploration of modular structure across scales. We identify age-associated gene modules by correlating module eigengenes with chronological age, followed by Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analyses across all three methods. The kNN-based approach reveals enrichment in pathways related to transcriptional regulation, neuronal differentiation, ion transport, and cardiomyopathy signaling, including Wnt, Hippo, and adrenergic pathways. The linear-thresholding method emphasizes neural development, neuron projection morphogenesis, and major signaling cascades such as mTOR, Wnt, and Hippo. In contrast, WGCNA identifies broader enrichments in RNA processing, synaptic signaling, and neurodegenerative pathways, such as MAP kinase signaling and transcription misregulation in cancer.  Overall, integrating kNN and linear-threshold networks with Leiden yields a robust and adaptable paradigm for dissecting the modular architecture, pathway organization, and functional complexity of the aging methylome beyond the constraints of WGCNA.

nodes.title: Decolonizing Network Theory in Ecology and Sustainability
nodes.abstract: Ecology studies organisms interacting within their environments. The sustainability of these systems—including humans—depends on complex networks of interactions that continually regenerate the conditions for life. Yet European colonization and its legacies1 continue to destroy the sustainability of vast numbers of species and peoples through habitat annihilation, genocidal extraction, and the industrial disruption of the carbon and nitrogen cycles that sustain all life. The resulting crises—from climate change and biodiversity loss to poverty and political upheaval—have elevated ecological network theory to a central role in sustainability science. Yet this theory’s dominant forms remain deeply compromised by their blindness to their own role in producing these crises.  Ecological and sustainability theory—including its most influential network formalisms—was forged in service to exploration, extraction, and imperial governance, promoting ideologies of scarcity, domination, and control. Core concepts such as logistic growth (Fig. 1), carrying capacity (K, Fig. 1), overpopulation,2 competition,3 and predation3 enshrined zero-sum logics while obscuring equally fundamental processes by which organisms actively enhance the capacities of environments to support life. From cyanobacteria’s oxygenation of the planet and plant–fungal mutualisms that built terrestrial ecosystems to the ongoing CO₂–O₂ cycling among plants and animals, provision, facilitation, and reciprocity are not peripheral—they are structurally central. Yet ecological network theory marginalizes these processes, mirroring the marginalization of non-Europeans and their knowledge from the construction of theory itself. Darwin summarized the roots of this framing and its framers when he predicted that “the civilized races of man will almost certainly exterminate, and replace, the savage races throughout the world …as we may hope.”4 This embrace of domination and competitive exclusion including White supremacy was seamlessly extended from European empires to all of nature, entrenching hierarchy, violence, and genocidal exclusion as ecological law.  Network theory formalizes these extensions most pointedly by embracing logistic growth—and especially its fiction of carrying capacity (K, Fig. 1)—as a foundation for competition3 and random matrix theories5 of stability and neo-Malthusian theories of sustainability2 that deflect responsibility from colonial economies to impoverished colonized populations—explicitly “blaming the victim” for colonial plundering.1  Rather than fully decolonizing ecological theory, this talk identifies specific network-theoretic paradigms where colonial constructions of organisms, interactions, and networks formed by these nodes and links continue to cripple predictive understanding—and points toward more mechanistically grounded alternatives. First, I show how the logistic growth equation contrives scarcity and rigid limits as default conditions for organisms’ existence. Second, I revisit two-species competition and coexistence theories3 and contrast their definitional instabilities and empirical failures of phenomenological interactions with consumer-resource and nutrient regeneration mechanisms that capture a far fuller range of ecological interactions. Finally, I turn to whole-system stability, where classic network theory holds that competition, self-limitation, and simplicity stabilize ecosystems, whereas mutualism, diversity, and complexity destabilize them.3,5 I then show how mechanistic trophic and biogeochemical network models bypass some of these failures by explicitly representing material flows and reciprocal feedbacks.6   Overall, I describe how foundational ecological network theory and metaphors it inspires in business ecosystems and social hierarchies misapprehend organisms as takers rather than makers in a fixed, zero-sum world. Instead, organisms are nutrient transformers—producers as much as consumers—continually modifying, r

nodes.title: Degree Organization Score: A Baseline-Aware Measure for Evolving Networks
nodes.abstract: Understanding how network structure evolves over time is central to the analysis of social, technological, and biological systems. While degree-based measures such as inequality and centralization are widely used to characterize network structure, their absolute values are often difficult to interpret and compare across networks or time, as they lack explicit normalization against meaningful reference configurations.  We introduce the Degree Organization Score (DOS), a continuous and distribution-agnostic measure that quantifies the relative organization of a network’s degree structure. DOS is defined by normalizing observed degree inequality, measured via the Gini coefficient, between two interpretable reference models: an Erdős–Rényi (ER) random graph, representing a stochastic baseline of minimal organized structure, and a star graph, representing an extreme reference of centralized degree organization.

nodes.title: Modeling Group-level Behavioral Responses in the COVID-19 Pandemic: The role of coordinated social bubbles
nodes.authors: Zhu, Hongan*; Cao, Qingtao; Heydari, Pedram; Heydari, Babak
nodes.abstract: Human behavioral response plays a critical role in the transmission and containment of epidemics. While considerable research has examined the impact of behavioral responses at individual level, coordinated group-level responses have received less attention. This study explores how group-level behavioral dynamics, in the form of early formation of social \textit{bubbles}, contribute to explaining variations in epidemic trajectories among two sets of European countries. To capture these dynamics, we propose an agent-based computational model leveraging cooperative game theory to simulate the formation and transformation of pandemic-related social bubbles. Our model is validated using real-world infection data from the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic. The results demonstrate that this compact, simple, and computationally efficient model effectively reproduces both epidemic trajectories and ordinal rankings observed in the selected countries. Further validation with mobility data and robustness tests indicates that the proposed framework possesses explanatory power regarding the recovery patterns of social interactions prior to the official lifting of non-pharmaceutical interventions.

nodes.title: Network perturbation predicts shifts in chondrocyte bioenergetic pathways in response to extracellular aging cues
nodes.authors: McNeill, Tyler*; Ambrosio, Fabrisia; Iijima, Hirotaka
nodes.abstract: Introduction: Identifying the causal mediators of aging and tracing their downstream effects in complex, low-turnover tissues—like articular cartilage—remains a significant challenge. Recent advances in omics-based technologies have enabled comprehensive profiling of transcriptomic and proteomic alterations in aging tissues1,2, including those within the knee joint.3 Although differential expression analyses are becoming increasingly common in both aging and orthopaedic research, they fail to dynamically simulate the propagation of extracellular perturbations through tissue-specific molecular pathways. Here, we present a computational framework for in silico perturbation modeling designed to predict distinct transcriptional responses to age-specific extracellular environmental cues. We exemplify applications of this framework using articular chondrocytes exposed to secretomes derived from infrapatellar fat pads (IFPs)—integral components of the cartilage microenvironment—excised from the knee joints of young and aged animals.  Methods: To establish a foundational model for aging-associated signaling in cartilage, publicly available transcriptomic profiles from knee joints of healthy and osteoarthritic adults4 were used to construct a weighted gene co-expression network.5 To identify physiologically relevant perturbation inputs, we performed mass spectrometry-based proteomics on media conditioned with IFPs excised from the knee joints of young and aged male mice (n=4/group), following IACUC approval. We previously found that male IFPs had a greater influence on markers of chondrogenicity in aged chondrocytes.6 Thus, only IFPs from male mice were used to generate conditioned media. All in vivo and in vitro studies were performed in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines to ensure rigor, reproducibility, and transparent reporting. Using the SEPDB secreted protein database7 and the NicheNet ligand-target model8, we identified receptors corresponding to secreted proteins identified via mass spectrometry. We then modeled the intracellular propagation of these extracellular signals using the Random Walk with Restart (RWR) algorithm9 and evaluated the biological relevance of the network inference using Gene Ontology enrichment analysis on ranked gene list outputs. To validate the results from this network model, we benchmarked the in silico outputs against RNA-sequencing data from aged chondrocytes treated with IFP-conditioned media that we have previously reported.6  Results: A total of 1,294 genes were differentially expressed between healthy and osteoarthritic patients, of which 800 were upregulated and 494 were downregulated in osteoarthritic knee cartilage. This includes the downregulation of chondrogenic genes, such as Sox9, and upregulation of catabolic and fibrocartilaginous genes, such as Mmp13, Col1a1, and Fap (Fig. 1A). We then constructed a cartilage-specific transcriptional network using topological overlap matrices and retained the top 50,000 weighted interactions for subsequent analyses. Genes encoding for core components of mitochondrial complex I involved in oxidative phosphorylation, Ndufb9 and Ndufab1, emerged as top hub genes within the network (Fig. 1B). 30 secreted proteins were identified in IFP-conditioned media. After mapping these ligands to their corresponding receptors, we employed the RWR algorithm to simulate the impact of these extracellular ligands on intracellular transcriptional responses and generated a ranked list of genes based on their proximity to receptor seeds in the network. Through GO enrichment analysis of top-ranked genes from the RWR model and differentially expressed genes identified by RNA-sequencing of murine chondrocytes treated with IFP-conditioned media in vitro, we identified 50 pathways enriched in silico and 46 pathways enriched in vitro. Of these, 18 pathways overlapped between the two datasets. Subsequent semantic similarity analysis implicated an additional 9 pathways from the i

nodes.title: Effects of central node selection for network robustness against localized attacks
nodes.authors: Chujyo, Masaki*; Liu, Shu; Toriumi, Fujio
nodes.abstract: Localized attacks on complex networks, where nodes are sequentially removed based on their topological or spatial proximity to a designated seed node, have garnered increasing attention across the fields. Existing studies of localized attacks average robustness over randomly chosen seeds, leaving the effect of the seed’s location largely unexplored; yet in spatially embedded networks, attacks initiated near the topological center can be far more damaging than those at the periphery. We introduce the Localized Attacks Vulnerability Index (LAVI), which quantifies seed-specific damage by the resulting link removals. Across extensive simulations, LAVI correlates more strongly with realized damage than standard centrality metrics and reveals vulnerability patterns those measures miss.

nodes.title: Structure-Dependent Effects of Frequency Shuffling on Synchronization
nodes.authors: Kim, Juseong*; Kim, Cook Hyun; Park, Jong-Min; Kim, Heetae
nodes.abstract: Synchronization is a universal phenomenon observed in various real-world systems, including power grids, neural networks, and cardiac systems [1, 2]. Given that synchronous states ensure stable and efficient operation, there has been significant interest in developing methods to enhance and maintain synchronization in these networks. Previous research has demonstrated that frequency shuffling, which involves oscillators intermittently exchanging their natural frequencies over time, can both enhance the degree of synchronization and reduce the relaxation time to the synchronized state [3]. However, these findings are based on globally coupled systems where every oscillator interacts with all others. To understand how network topology influences the effects of frequency shuffling on synchronization, we investigate a Kuramoto model on a one-dimensional periodic regular lattice with nearest-neighbor interactions. Frequency shuffling is implemented as a random exchange of oscillators’ natural frequencies occurring at a constant rate. Our results show that, unlike in all-to-all networks, the order parameter in a ring network exhibits a nonmonotonic dependence on the shuffling rate λ For small λ, increasing the shuffling rate enhances synchronization, consistent with previous results for all-to-all networks. At larger λ, however, the dynamics become qualitatively different. In addition to the synchronized state, new metastable fixed points emerge, characterized by nonzero winding numbers q, known as twisted states. As a result, while some realizations relax to the synchronized state, others become trapped in twisted states for extremely long times before eventually synchronizing. To confirm this mechanism, we numerically analyze the transition probabilities between the in-phase state (q = 0) and the typical twisted states (|q| = 1) by initializing the system in each configuration and counting the number of remaining or transitioning samples at a given time (Fig. 1, center and right panels). We find that transitions from the synchronized state to the twisted states become increasingly rare and eventually vanish as λ increases, whereas transitions from twisted to synchronized states persist with a finite probability. Importantly, the decreasing escape probability from the twisted states implies that the lifetime of these metastable states grows dramatically as the shuffling rate increases. Consequently, the relaxation time to synchronization increases with λ, in contrast to all-to-all networks, where larger shuffling rates monotonically accelerate synchronization. It results in a persistent but transient nonmonotonic dependence of the order parameter on λ, on finite time scales relevant to realistic systems (Fig. 1, left panel, black dashed line). These results demonstrate that network topology plays a crucial role in determining the effectiveness of frequency shuffling. In particular, in translationally invariant networks such as regular lattices, the emergence of twisted metastable states fundamentally alters the relaxation dynamics, limiting the benefits of frequency shuffling.

nodes.title: How Do Network Mechanisms Respond to Shocks? Local Projections Meet Relational Event Models
nodes.abstract: How do network formation mechanisms respond to external shocks? Existing approaches either compare static snapshots before and after perturbations—losing dynamic information—or detect that parameters change without characterizing response trajectories [1,2]. We introduce a framework combining Relational Event Models (REM) with Local Projections (LP) to estimate impulse response functions on mechanism parameters themselves. The two-step procedure first estimates REM parameters in moving windows, yielding time series of coefficients for reciprocity, homophily, and transitivity [3,4]. We then apply Jord` a’s [5] LP method to trace how these coefficients respond at multiple horizons following a shock. LP’s robustness to dynamic misspecification is valuable: we need not assume how parameters evolve, only that shocks have estimable effects. We validate through simulations demonstrating recovery of canonical IRF shapes—permanent shifts, tempo- rary spikes with exponential decay, and gradual transitions—under realistic network sizes and event densities. We establish power curves for shock detection and compare performance against naive pre/post estimation, showing LP+REM’s advantage in distinguishing temporary perturbations from permanent structural breaks. As empirical illustration, we apply the method to Twitter interactions among Turkish-American foreign policy experts (2010–2021). Figure 1 shows interaction volume with perturbations at political crisis points. LP+REM en- ables tracing how specific mechanisms—cross-national reciprocity, professional vs. national homophily—respond to bilateral crises, testing predictions from threat-rigidity theory versus epistemic community resilience. [1] Meijerink-Bosman et al. (2022). Dynamic relational event modeling. PLOS ONE, 17(8). [2] Haunss & Hollway (2023). Multimodal mechanisms of political discourse dynamics. Network Science, 11(1). [3] Butts (2008). A relational event framework for social action. Sociological Methodology, 38(1). [4] Lillo & Rizzini (2024). Modelling shock propagation in financial temporal networks. arXiv :2407.09340. [5] Jord` a (2005). Estimation and inference of impulse responses by local projections. AER, 95(1).

nodes.title: Synchronization Enhancement by Phase Lag in Coupled Oscillators with Inertia
nodes.authors: Yi, Sudo*; Kim, Cook Hyun; Kim, Heetae; Kahng, B.
nodes.abstract: Coupled-oscillator systems with inertia offer a realistic framework for describing many physical systems, including electrical power grids. Although inertia is essential to capture realistic dynamical behavior, it suppresses global synchronization, so the system can be synchronized only at relatively large coupling strengths and in a discontinuous manner, often accompanied by the formation of several clusters locked in frequency [1–3]. The effect of phase lag has been extensively explored in the first-order Kuramoto model—most notably in the Sakaguchi–Kuramoto variant—where it is typically observed to suppress synchronization and modify the critical coupling threshold [4]. In this study, we demonstrate that introducing a phase lag to only a subset of oscillators can, conversely, promote synchronization in the second-order Kuramoto model with inertia.  We consider a heterogeneous population comprising oscillators with no phase lag and those with a uniform, nonzero phase lag. The dynamics are investigated using a post-lag protocol, in which the phase lag is imposed only after the initially lag-free system has relaxed to a steady state. Figure 1 shows that introducing a phase lag in only part of the population alters the structure of the primary frequency-entrained cluster. For large inertia, numerical results indicate that the phase lag displaces the left boundary of the primary entrained cluster in the intrinsic-frequency domain toward more negative values. As a result, oscillators with negative natural frequencies—both drifting units and those originally forming secondary or higher-order clusters—are recruited into the synchronized state [2,3]. In contrast, oscillators with sufficiently large positive natural frequencies do not depinning, so the right boundary of the primary cluster stays fixed, yielding a net expansion of the primary synchronized cluster.  These findings demonstrate that introducing a partial phase lag can qualitatively alter the configuration of synchronized clusters in inertia-oscillator systems, leading to an expansion of the central frequency-locked cluster in the large-inertia regime. By revealing how phase lag reshapes cluster boundaries without triggering depinning on the positive-frequency side, this work clarifies the impact of partial phase lag on synchronized cluster organization in the second-order Kuramoto model, providing guidance on synchronization control for inertial systems, such as power grids.

nodes.title: Operational time and criticality in physics co-authorship networks
nodes.abstract: Many real-world complex networks display either fractal or small-world structure, but rarely both. Prior work explains this incompatibility through static architectural constraints: fractality emerges from critical branching trees, whereas small-world structure requires supercritical expansion supported by long-range shortcuts. Here we show that this dichotomy is not merely structural but dynamical. Using more than a century of APS co-authorship data, we construct a sequence of network time slices by tuning aggregation to the percolation transition. Measuring a set of macro-level parameters -- including percolation observables and small-world indicators -- we find that all scale as power laws with the networks’ distance from criticality. Networks remain fractal near criticality but acquire small-world shortcuts only when aggregated beyond the critical point. We further show that the critical time scale emerging from this aggregation procedure constitutes an operational time to which the system self-organizes: citation avalanches obey dynamical scaling theory only when defined in this intrinsic time. These results indicate that static analyses are insufficient whenever the evolutionary time scale of a network is empirically accessible. A dynamical scaling framework is required to correctly identify and characterize critical and supercritical network states.

nodes.title: On the Structure of China's High-Speed Rail-Aviation Composite Network
nodes.abstract: This study constructs China’s high-speed rail(CRH)-aviation composite network. By analyzing its topological properties and comparing them with those of the single-modal CRH and aviation networks, we identify the multiple network roles of transportation hubs and reveal the hub-periphery structural pattern.  We collected schedule data from China’s CRH timetables and flight plans to construct the aviation and CRH networks. Nodes are cities with CRH stations, airports, or both; edges mean direct CRH or flight connectivity, directed and unweighted. Railway stations and airports within the same city are considered interconnected in the composite network,   Topological analysis shows CRH network and aviation network both have scale-free and small-world features—key for efficient transport. Differences exist: The CRH network has extremely high clustering, ensuring high local efficiency. The aviation network has a lower clustering, and no clear communities, supporting long-distance travel. The composite network expands coverage by leveraging the complementary advantages of these two transportation modes.  We then assigned multiple network roles to nodes based on their multiplex degrees and distributions. From a multiplex network perspective, we identified four distinct roles of national transportation hubs: Focused nodes (R1), Multiplex nodes (R2), Focused hubs (R3), and Multiplex hubs (R4). This analysis reveals the uneven distribution of these hub types across geographical space and network hierarchical structures. We further explored the different roles of air and rail transportation in organizing passenger network structures; the inherent differences between the two modes have led to the structural differentiation of China’s CRH-aviation composite network, forming a fundamental pattern consisting of a hub core and peripheral clusters.  This study addresses the limitations of single-modal transportation network research. It uncovers the hub-peripheral structural pattern and uneven distribution characteristics of the composite network, providing theoretical support for the optimization of comprehensive transportation planning and the precise layout of hub systems.  Future research will further explore the weighted properties of the composite network and the influencing factors of regional differences.

nodes.title: The mesoscopic structure of the Carbon Trade Network across the last twenty years
nodes.abstract: As emissions from burning fossil fuels are the primary cause of global warming~[1], the last decades have witnessed a considerable effort to mitigate carbon emissions. At country level, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change attributes emissions to countries producing goods and services~[2]; recent literature has, however, advocated the adoption of a consumption-aware accounting, that re-distributes the responsibility for the emissions between producers and users~[3]. Studies have attempted to understand to what extent a `consumption principle' may help reducing disparities in carbon accounting, estimating the amount of traded carbon via Multi-Regional Input-Output (MRIO) tables. As noticed by Caro et al.~[4], MRIO tables are very sensitive to the accuracy of the available data on trading sectors; interestingly enough, data requirements can be relaxed by considering aggregate measures: specifically, the amount of carbon embedded into a country export can be simply quantified by multiplying it by the related \emph{GDP-induced carbon intensity}, measuring the kilograms of carbon per dollar of GDP released by a given country during a given year.  Inspired by the work of Caro and co-authors, we have constructed the Carbon Trade Network (CTN), i.e. the graph induced by the trade-embedded carbon exchanges between world countries, with the aim of investigating it over the past two decades. More specifically, we have limited ourselves to analyze the mesoscopic organization of the CTN Strongly Connected Component (SCC), by optimizing the binary, weighted, and enhanced variants of the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) on it. In all cases, the SCC is characterized by an additional, nested structure constituted of a core and a periphery - the core (periphery) subgraph being characterized by the highest (lowest) level of reciprocity (see fig.~1).  In the purely binary case, the core block gathers the $84$ richest, and more polluting, countries (among which Brazil, Canada, China, Europe, India, Japan, South-Africa, USA); if, instead, we leave the BIC instantiated with the directed, binary variant of the Stochastic Block Model (bSBM) free to find the optimal number of groups, we recover ten modules - only five of which are assembled in the aforementioned core. Optimizing BIC instantiated with the directed, weighted variant of the Stochastic Block Model (wSBM) confirms the results obtained by running the bSBM, the subset of core-nodes concentrating the highest percentage of the total weight ($\simeq 10\%$) now including China, India, South-Africa, USA: an important exception is represented by Russia, no longer part of this subset - as the year considered for the present analysis is $2023$, this may be due to the sanctions suffered by Russia in consequence of the war with Ukraine. As a last exercise, we have instantiated BIC with the directed, enhanced variant of the Stochastic Block Model (eSBM), constraining both binary and weighted properties in a block-wise fashion: in such a case, the algorithm recovers China, India, Russia, USA as the nodes constituting the subgraph summing up $\simeq 10\%$ of the total weight (see fig.~1).  As an additional exercise, we have individuated the countries playing the role of hubs (top exporters) and authorities (top importers) by running both the binary and weighted versions of the HITS algorithm: accounting for weights reveals that \emph{i)} China is becoming increasingly central in the category of authorities as well, closely followed by India; \emph{ii)} Vietnam is becoming the second most central country in the category of hubs.

nodes.title: Across-Visit Consensus Community Detection in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) Gene Co-expression Temporal Network Data
nodes.abstract: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex autoimmune disease characterized by strong heterogeneity and time-varying immune dysregulation. While gene co-expression network analysis has proven useful for identifying disease-associated modules, most existing approaches rely on static or cross-sectional representations and do not explicitly leverage longitudinal structure.  Here, we propose a time-aware multilayer network framework to identify stable gene communities and underexplored hub genes in longitudinal whole-blood transcriptomic data from SLE patients (GSE65391). GSE65391 is a publicly available longitudinal whole-blood gene expression dataset in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), where patients are profiled repeatedly across multiple clinical visits (i.e., the same individuals contribute samples at different time points), alongside healthy controls. This longitudinal design enables constructing visit-specific co-expression layers and then identifying time-stable communities by aggregating evidence across visits rather than relying on a single static network [1,2].  Gene co-expression networks are constructed independently for each clinical visit, yielding a sequence of visit-specific network layers defined over a shared set of genes. Instead of optimizing a coupled multislice modularity with explicit inter-layer identity edges [3], we adopt a consensus-across-time strategy [4]: communities are detected separately within each visit layer using the Leiden algorithm [5], and a weighted consensus graph is subsequently built based on gene–gene co-assignment fractions across layers. Re-clustering this consensus graph produces time-stable gene communities that summarize recurrent co-expression patterns across visits.  Applying this framework, we identify eight robust communities corresponding to major immune processes, including interferon signaling, neutrophil activation, plasmablast-related programs, and platelet-associated pathways. Beyond these well-characterized structures, our approach highlights unexpected hub genes that exhibit high within-community centrality, despite having a limited prior association with SLE. To systematically assess novelty, we integrate automated literature and GWAS-based screening to quantify existing disease-specific evidence for each candidate hub, revealing several highly ranked genes with minimal representation in prior SLE studies [6,7].  We further verify that the identified hub genes remain highly central when analyses are restricted to female samples, which constitute the majority of the cohort, indicating that hub detection is not driven by a small subset of subjects. Together, these results demonstrate that time-aware consensus community detection provides a robust and interpretable alternative to identity-coupled multislice methods for longitudinal omics data, enabling the discovery of stable network structure and novel candidate regulators in complex autoimmune diseases. This abstract also aligns with our prior RA study in which network community structure was leveraged to prioritize underexplored hub genes via systematic evidence screening [8].

nodes.title: Characterizing Open-Ended Evolution Through Undecidability Mechanisms in Random Boolean Networks
nodes.authors: Lopez-Diaz, Amahury*; Rivera Torres, Pedro Juan; Febres, Gerardo; Gershenson, Carlos
nodes.abstract: Open-ended evolution (OEE) is the sustained production of novel phenotypes rather than convergence to a fixed repertoire. In finite-state network dynamics, novelty is easily confounded with noise, while asymptotic diagnostics are often computationally intractable. We introduce a simple, substrate-agnostic metric, $\Omega$, that scores a trajectory by the residence-time-weighted contribution of each distinct attractor’s cycle length, rewarding persistent new cyclic phenotypes while penalizing both single-attractor dynamics and fleeting random jumps. Using Random Boolean Networks (RBNs) as a unifying testbed, we compare classical Boolean dynamics with biologically motivated non-classical mechanisms (probabilistic context switching, annealed rule mutation, paraconsistent logic, modal necessary/possible gating, and quantum-inspired superposition/entanglement) under deterministic homogeneous and stochastic heterogeneous updating schemes. As Figure 1 shows, in homogeneous structures PBN stochastic switching dominates in the sparse/near-critical regime, while modal gating supports sustained novelty at higher connectivity. These results suggest that OEE-relevant dynamics in networked regulatory models are promoted not by noise alone, but by structured, state-dependent uncertainty that continually creates new, persistent attractor families, supporting recent theoretical results showing that only self-referential mechanisms enable authentic OEE.

nodes.title: Understanding Urban-Rural Disparities in Mobility Inefficiency for Colombia, Mexico, and India
nodes.authors: Iyer, Nandini*; Luca, Massimiliano; Di Clemente, Riccardo
nodes.abstract: As urbanisation accelerates globally, understanding mobility disparities between urban and rural areas becomes crucial for equitable development. Previous approaches to defining urban-rural boundaries rely on population density thresholds [1] or built environment contiguity [2], yet these static methods fail to capture functional interdependencies between regions. This critical gap leaves policymakers without actionable insights about where and how mobility infrastructure fails to serve rural communities, particularly in Low and Middle-Income Countries where informal employment and multi-purpose trips shape travel patterns differently than in Global North contexts. We address this gap by investigating mobility inefficiency inequalities across Colombia, India, and Mexico using aggregated Location-Based Services data from November-December 2019 [3]. We develop a novel framework that defines urban-rural boundaries through network percolation of mobility patterns rather than static characteristics, capturing functional integration of settlements. Our classification shows strong agreement with established Global Urban Rural Settlement datasets, validating our mobility-based approach. We introduce mobility inefficiency as a metric measuring deviation between actual trip lengths and optimal shortest paths. Importantly, high inefficiency values do not imply poor individual routing choices but reveal aggregated systemic constraints such as congestion, limited infrastructure, geographic barriers, or trip chaining patterns. Our analysis reveals that rural areas consistently exhibit higher mobility inefficiency than urban areas, particularly for longer trips (>5km) and later in the day (Figure 1A visualises the case of Colombia). These disparities only emerge when analysing both trip distance and time of day simultaneously, while aggregate analyses mask important inequalities.   For urban areas, we construct vector fields to analyse transit-efficiency alignment. We calculate public transit accessibility scores combining coverage area (convex hull of transit stops) and stop density from OpenStreetMap data, then create spatial gradient vectors showing how transit access and mobility efficiency vary across neighbouring areas. Our transit metric was validated against General Transit Feed Specification data in Bogotá and Mexico City, showing strong correlations during operational hours. Comparing angular differences between these vector fields reveals four distinct urban patterns, with Colombian cities showing stronger alignment likely due to Bus Rapid Transit systems (Figure 1B). For rural areas, we analyse all rural geohashes within 256km of major cities, linking each to its nearest urban centre. Using OLS regression, we examine how distance from city centre, population, directional orientation (east-west and north-south positioning), and proximity to alternative cities relate to inefficiency. Results reveal context-specific patterns: distance matters in Bogotá, population in New Delhi (Figure 1C), while directional patterns in all three cities align with documented geographic barriers and socioeconomic distributions. Our framework demonstrates that measuring mobility inequality requires both consistent metrics and context-appropriate analytical methods, providing actionable insights for targeted infrastructure investment.

nodes.title: The Specialization of Interdisciplinary Innovators in Science and Technology
nodes.abstract: Interdisciplinary research is widely recognized as essential for science and society, yet our quantitative understanding of who drives such work remains limited. Funding agencies have traditionally emphasized assembling teams of disciplinary specialists, while recent studies point to the importance of individual generalists. Analyzing 52 million publications and 7 million patents, we uncover a striking regularity: the timing of a researcher’s most interdisciplinary work follows a uniform distribution across the career, implying that interdisciplinarity is best understood as a persistent, individual-level trait. Building on this universal pattern, we derive a quantitative index, Λ that captures each researcher’s interdisciplinary specialization. Λ remains stable across careers and fields, is strongly associated with the production of highly interdisciplinary works across team sizes, and sharply predicts the quantity and impact of future interdisciplinary work. Large-scale analyses leveraging Λ further reveal that interdisciplinary output is specialized at the individual level: a small set of high-Λ innovators disproportionately drive boundary-spanning contributions; impact peaks when the interdisciplinarity of work aligns with individual Λ; and collaboration networks exhibit strong homophily in Λ, reinforcing this specialization. Together, these findings demonstrate that interdisciplinarity is not only a collective property of teams but also a measurable, persistent individual trait, offering a new framework for understanding how interdisciplinary knowledge is produced, evaluated, and rewarded.

nodes.title: Community Detection in Labeled Growing Hypergraphs
nodes.abstract: We consider a model of growing hypergraphs in which in which hyperedges form as noisy copies of prior hyperedges. Our model extends prior work [1] by incorporating binary community node labels. The formation of new edges is affected by homophily with respect to these labels. Unlike standard generative community detection methods which assume that edges form independently conditioned on node labels, our model instead explicitly assumes that new interactions are most likely to arrive between similar nodes which have previously iteracted. This generative model is thus well-suited for community-detection for higher-order data sets collected from systems which may be reasonably believed to exhibit path-dependence, in which which interactions happen in the future are causally influenced by which interactions occurred in the past. This setting is common in many higher-order social and information systems; for example, in a network of scientific collaborations, whether a group of researchers collaborates on a new paper depends strongly on whether or not that same group, or some variant thereof, has collaborated in the past. The community structures which result from this model are very different from those than which would be generated by standard stochastic blockmodel variants.  To perform community detection under this model, we develop a likelihood-based inference method. Due to the computational complexity of computing the likelihood, we develop an approximation based on edge intersection sizes, which we show to have good empirical performance. We perform the maximization via simulated annealing. This approximated likelihood is highly correlated with the true labels of the graph, allowing the maximization of the approximate likelihood to yield an effective approximation of the true labels. The latent-variable structure of this model also allows us to estimate the genealogy of edges, giving an approximate branching process describing the emergence of new interactions represented in the data.  On synthetic data, we find that likelihood maximization in this model can result in much higher-fidelity reconstruction of ground-truth communities than standard methods such as Louvain modularity maximization (Fig. 1(a)-(b)). We also apply our methods to two classes of node-labeled data sets which do not obey standard community detection assumptions: legislate cosponsorship in the US congress (tagged by party aﬀiliation) and co-authorship data from arXiv (tagged by inferred author gender). In sum, our results indicate that methods for community detection in edge-dependent hypergraphs can outperform standard methods for some systems, and suggest future work for modeling and inference in such systems.

nodes.title: Modeling the Effect of Social Contagion in Solar Adoption through Commuter and Socioeconomic Networks
nodes.abstract: The widespread adoption of residential solar panels is critical for the global energy transition, but its progress is hindered by challenges such as high customer acquisition costs and slowing demand, with a market contraction of 31% in 2024, the first contraction since 2017. While social contagion is known to be a powerful driver of increasing solar adoption, the exact mechanisms within this “peer effect” remain poorly distinguished. Specifically, a gap exists in understanding whether influence spreads primarily through Information Flow – that is, through interaction and connections – or through Socioeconomic Similarity (Homophily), where influence is stronger within groups of more similar socioeconomic characteristics, such as income, race, and education levels.  This study aims to quantitatively distinguish between these two competing mechanisms for solar adoption, hypothesizing that if the principle of homophily – as opposed to information flow – was the dominant mechanism in social contagion for solar adoption, then a network model built on socioeconomic similarity would produce communities with more uniform solar adoption rates and correlate more strongly with real adoption data.  Three networks were created at a U.S. county level to compare adoption: a Socioeconomic Similarity Network connecting geographically proximate counties based on a multi-dimensional Jenson-Shannon Divergence similarity score from socioeconomic profiles; an Information Flow Network from county-to-county commuter flows; and a State-Level Network for a geographic baseline. Then, a static analysis using the Louvain algorithm identified community structures and measured their homogeneity, and then a Susceptible-Infected-Resistant (SIR) epidemiological model was used to test which network structure's diffusion patterns best correlate with the real-world solar adoption data. Rather than designating beta as a global constant, the probability of transmission between two counties is made proportional to the strength of their connection and calculated as:  P(transmission) = base_beta * (edge_weight / max(network_weight).  Static analysis showed that the Socioeconomic Similarity network produced highly homogeneous clusters (lower intra-cluster standard deviation of adoption rates), confirming that adoption concentrates within specific socioeconomic groups. However, dynamic SIR simulations revealed that the Commuter Network was the only model to yield a strong, statistically significant positive correlation with real-world adoption rates, with the Commuter Network having correlation r = 0.18 (p < 0.01) to real-world results while the Socioeconomic Network had correlation r = -0.24.  These findings suggest that while socioeconomic homophily dictates receptivity, information flow via commuter ties drives propagation. Commuter routes act as “weak ties” bridging distinct communities, while socioeconomic similarity represents “strong ties” required for validation. Policy interventions must therefore be hybrid: leveraging high-mobility commuter corridors for broad information dissemination while tailoring incentives to specific socioeconomic peer groups to ensure local uptake.

nodes.title: FORGE: Foundational Optimization Representations from Graph Embeddings
nodes.abstract:
Combinatorial optimization (CO) problems are fundamental across science and engineering, yet learning-based approaches face critical limitations: they require extensive training data from solving numerous difficult instances, demand dedicated models for each problem distribution and task, and depend on the very solvers they aim to accelerate. These constraints severely limit scalability and generalization to new domains. We therefore introduce 	extsc{Forge}: 	extbf{F}oundational 	extbf{O}ptimization 	extbf{R}epresentations from 	extbf{G}raph 	extbf{E}mbeddings, a framework that addresses these limitations through unsupervised pre-training on diverse mixed-integer programming (MIP) instances. Unlike existing methods, 	extsc{Forge} does not require optimization solvers or optimal solutions during training.  
oindent	extbf{Core Innovation}: 	extsc{Forge} employs a vector-quantized graph autoencoder that learns structural representations at the instance level. Drawing inspiration from foundational models in NLP and computer vision, we introduce two key concepts to optimization: (1) a discrete vocabulary for representing MIP instances through vector quantization, and (2) preservation of global information that traditional GNN approaches fail to capture due to their inherent locality bias.  
oindent	extbf{Evaluation}: We demonstrate 	extsc{Forge}'s versatility in both unsupervised and supervised settings:  egin{itemize}     \item 	extbf{Unsupervised}: 	extsc{Forge} embeddings effectively cluster unseen instances across diverse problem domains and sizes without any task-specific training.     \item 	extbf{Supervised}: A single pre-trained 	extsc{Forge} model can be fine-tuned for multiple downstream tasks using minimal additional data. We evaluate on two distinct tasks: (1) predicting integrality gaps for cut generation, and (2) predicting variable hints for search guidance. Critically, the same pre-trained model with $\approx 3.25$M parameters generalizes across varied problem types and instance sizes. \end{itemize}  
oindent	extbf{Results}: Integrating 	extsc{Forge} predictions into Gurobi, a state-of-the-art commercial solver, consistently reduces primal gaps across both tasks and multiple problem domains. 	extsc{Forge} can also augment existing learning-based methods, improving their performance on instances these methods were specifically trained on but unseen by 	extsc{Forge}.  
oindent	extbf{Contributions}: We provide: (1) a general-purpose foundational model producing multi-level embeddings (instance, variable, and constraint), (2) unsupervised generalization across problem types, (3) supervised adaptability with minimal fine-tuning, (4) improved solver performance through integration, and (5) open-source code, pre-trained weights, and embeddings to foster further research.

nodes.title: Uncovering excitatory and inhibitory associations across nodes via stochastic generalization of Markov chains
nodes.authors: Okamoto, Hiroshi*; Yamano, Hiroko; Sakata, Ichiro
nodes.abstract: Markov chains are widely recognized as a fundamental framework for analysing complex networks. The most representative example is Google’s PageRank algorithm, which evaluates node importance using the stationary distribution of a Markov chain. Similarly, the circulation of goods, services and money in economic networks—such as supply chains, input–output tables and international trade—can be represented as probability flows in Markov chains. Conventional Markov chains converge to a stationary state over time. However, actual circulations, for instance monetary flows in economic networks, are dynamic rather than static. To capture the dynamism of real-world flows, we propose a stochastic generalization of Markov chain (SGMC). We show that SGMC uncovers hidden patterns of associations across nodes at any distance, which are intrinsically related to community structure.

nodes.title: Discovering Semi-Private Social Networks: Structural Bias from Private--Private Edge Censoring
nodes.abstract: Network data are often incomplete; methodological literature shows that how edges go missing can bias structural inference as much as, or more than, the number of missing edges. Some online social networks now exhibit a specific, privacy-driven observation regime when users ``lock'' their accounts: they remain reachable via public neighbors, but the edges among ``private'' users may be unobservable, creating a correlated pattern of missingness that differs from classic random-edge loss and from full actor nonresponse. Related work on mixed public/private profiles emphasizes that privacy can still permit substantial inference from visible structure, motivating a discoverability-oriented perspective on partial observation.  We operationalize network discoverability by comparing two edge-loss mechanisms on a large real-world social network (~10,000 nodes, ~95,000 edges), holding the total number of removed edges fixed at each sparsification level. In a baseline condition, edges are removed uniformly at random. In a privacy-realistic condition, a random subset of nodes is designated private, and only private--private edges are censored, modeling platforms in which private users can still be encountered via public edges, but their mutual edges are hidden. We track local cohesion (global clustering/transitivity), global reachability (giant component size and diameter), degree-distribution shape (power-law exponent), fragmentation (number of connected components), and mesoscale structure (Louvain community count and Newman modularity).  Despite identical edge-loss budgets, the two mechanisms produce sharply different trajectories: random edge removal preserves the degree-shape parameter and delays fragmentation, but rapidly inflates distances within a still-large giant component; privacy-driven censoring accelerates the loss of clustering, increases fragmentation, shrinks the giant component more quickly, and substantially shifts the fitted degree exponent. These results align with prior findings that measurement error and non-random missingness can systematically distort topology and inference. We conclude that discoverability in partially private social networks depends crucially on privacy semantics, not merely missingness rates, and we discuss implications for robust measurement and reconstruction under correlated edge censoring.

nodes.title: Gene Co-expression Network Rewiring Explains Variation in Immune-Mediated Fibrosis in wild Stickleback
nodes.abstract: Fibrosis is a complex, multigenic phenotype driven by sustained immune activation and tissue remodeling, making it well suited to network-based and machine-learning analyses that capture non-linear gene interactions. Rather than focusing on individual genes, understanding fibrosis requires identifying coordinated transcriptional programs and network structures that differentiate mild and severe outcomes across populations. We integrate head–kidney and liver transcriptomic data with fibrosis severity, parasite infection status, and ecological metadata from wild threespine stickleback (Gasterosteus aculeatus). Fibrosis was framed as a binary classification problem, enabling systematic comparison of linear and non-linear supervised models, including logistic regression with L1 and L2 regularization, random forests, and gradient-boosted decision trees (XGBoost). Models were evaluated with and without parasite load to assess robustness to infection-related confounding. XGBoost achieved the highest predictive performance (AUC = 0.748 with parasite load; 0.727 without), demonstrating that gene expression alone captures substantial fibrosis-related signal. Using XGBoost feature importance, we selected the top 50 genes for downstream analysis and resolved gene identities via BLAST alignment and Ensembl orthology mapping. To characterize higher-order organization, we constructed phenotype-specific gene co-expression networks and per- formed differential network analysis, identifying 1,748 statistically significant edges distinguishing high- and low-fibrosis states. Community detection revealed 13 densely connected gene modules, highlighting coordinated network rewiring associated with fibrosis severity. These results show that fibrosis variation in natural populations is governed by structured, gene network dynamics. Newly discovered genes from this analysis can uncover novel gene regulatory dynamics underlying immune responses to inflammatory diseases for various species.

nodes.title: The Diffusion of Causal Language in Social Networks
nodes.abstract: Causal reasoning plays a central role in human cognition. It facilitates the ability to infer, predict, and manipulate outcomes within the environment, which in turn lays the foundation for a uniquely adaptive decision-making framework that is crucial in navigating complex problem-solving contexts. With the pervasive influence of social media platforms, these online social networks have become critical for disseminating information, shaping public beliefs, and influencing daily life. However, no study has examined the propagation of causal language within social networks. In this work, we analyze the dispersion of messages containing causal language against those without, within the milieu of a large online social network. With the entirety of messages over one complete day on Twitter along with two additional days for validation, and with our validated ensemble method for identifying causal language, our findings reveal that messages with causal language exhibit a more extensive reach than those without. Furthermore, our counterfactual analysis demonstrates that the effect of causal language on information diffusion is truly \textit{causal}. Moreover, our findings indicate that messages incorporating causal language manifest a higher ability to spread to out-groups compared to those without.
