These novel insights reveal the unique diffusion pattern of causal language within social networks, and suggest a potential to mitigate the echo chamber effect, while causal language could serve as a bridge for diverse perspectives.

nodes.title: Cascade Processes in Directed Hypergraphs and the Architecture of the Chemical Space
nodes.authors: Medina, Sofia*; LaRock, Timothy; Lambiotte, Renaud
nodes.abstract: Directed hypergraphs allow us to capture multi-way interactions, and the development of methodologies on directed hypergraphs is an active area of investigation within network science. In this work, we develop a methodology for the analysis of connectivity in directed hypergraphs with an emphasis on the propagation of local connectivity and influence. This methodology is motivated by the logic of chemical reactions and captures how reactivity amongst nodes and hyperedges flows and accumulates. We note that this methodology may also be applied in general to hypergraphs beyond chemically-derived hypergraphs, in particular those that model system interactions which require specific prerequisite configurations or orderings before influence can propagate. We further introduce graph-theoretic metrics on the nodes and hyperedges of the hypergraph based on the application of our methodology, which unveil key drivers of the hypergraph system. Our metrics develop the reactive capital of nodes and hyperedges in relation to their capacity for production, the ease with which they can be produced, and their general association to other nodes and hyperedges in the directed hypergraph.  As a playground, we extend our investigation to the application of modelling of chemical reactions, capturing the properties of organisation within the chemical space. We compare the results of our methodology, and in particular the metrics derived for nodes and hyperedges, between a chemical reaction dataset and a randomised null model of this data. This analysis illustrates how our method can be applied and also allows us to outline properties of the chemical space and its connectivity.   The method we develop introduces an approach to modelling connectivity in directed hypergraphs, as well as offers a perspective of the organisation and traversal of directed hypergraph systems. Our methodology is a system-wide, exploratory method that captures the growth of possibilities in a system. Our method further quantifies how richly a system can be expanded in its inherent connectivity via selected subsets of nodes or hyperedges and in what depth, breadth, and ordering this can be done.

nodes.title: Mapping Innovation: Geometrics of the Adjacent Possible
nodes.abstract: The adjacent possible captures how new configurations arise from existing components [1], yet its underlying geometry has remained implicit. We argue that this implicit geometry is central to understanding innovation in sociotechnical systems, where recombination occurs within institutions, conventions, and infrastructures accumulated through past collective action, distorting the space of possibilities into regions of differing feasibility and value. Here, we develop a network-based framework that makes this geometry explicit by formalizing the adjacent possible as a recombination network, building on prior views of innovation as recombination search [2]. Within this framework, we conceptualize technological innovation as a network of technology codes, where historically typical assemblies–frequently co-used components–can form dense clusters that compress effective distances, distort shortest paths, and may give rise to curvature in the surrounding possibility space. We operationalize curvature by comparing least-resistance paths between newly combined components, weighted by past co-use, to their expected distances in the absence of typicality (Fig. 1a), capturing sociotechnical inertia as a measurable mesoscopic network property. Using U.S. patent data, we illustrate curvature with Edison’s 1881 electric lighting system, where accumulated precedents compress the adjacent possible and lengthen least-resistance paths relative to expected paths (Fig. 1b); at the inventor level, iconic cases reveal systematic differences in structural contexts, with Edison’s inventions more often observed in high-curvature regions and Tesla’s in flatter regions of the possibility space (Fig. 1c–d). At scale, curvature is consistently associated with innovation outcomes: inventions observed in higher-curvature regions are more likely to be high-impact “hit” patents, defined as those in the top 5% of ten-year forward citations (Fig. 1e), and to exhibit greater market value (Fig. 1f). Together, these findings highlight curvature as a unifying network concept linking typicality, search, and value, offering a network-science perspective on where successful innovations are more likely to be observed, at curved peripheries of institutionalized knowledge.  [1] Kauffman, S. A. (1993). The Origins of Order. Oxford University Press. [2] Fleming, L. (2001). Recombinant uncertainty in technological search. Management Science, 47(1), 117–132.

nodes.title: Identifying disturbances in power grids using reservoir computing
nodes.abstract: SEE PDF ATTACHED  Power grids are large, complex networks in which generators must remain synchronized to maintain stable energy flow. Disturbances such as generator failures or power surges can quickly escalate into widespread outages if not promptly addressed, as demonstrated by the blackout across Spain and Portugal in April 2025. This project focuses on the identification and characterization of such disturbances using only prior observations of system dynamics. Although various methods exist for detecting these events, accurately identifying and responding to them remains a significant challenge due to the grid's size, speed, complexity, and inherently dynamic behavior.  We model the grid using the second-order Kuramoto model, which captures the coupled dynamics of synchronous generators. To simulate disturbances, we apply sinusoidal functions to the frequency of selected generators, altering the behavior of the network. The Western Electricity Coordinating Council (WECC) 240-bus dataset, provided by NREL, serves as our test system, containing 146 generators and realistic network connectivity. Using reservoir computing, we can identify which generators are disturbed as well as the nature of the disturbances. Reservoir computing is a recurrent neural network based framework consisting of three layers: an input layer, a high-dimensional nonlinear “reservoir,” and a trainable readout layer. In our application, the input is the phase and frequency data from a system of N synchronous generators. The reservoir then transforms this data into a higher-dimensional space, and finally the readout layer is trained to learn a mapping from the reservoir states to the desired output, the underlying disturbances applied to each generator.   Preliminary results demonstrate that our method can successfully identify disturbances in the WECC power grid. Using a delayed control scheme, we can not only identify but also suppress the disturbances. Current efforts focus on testing robustness under limited observability and reflecting realistic operating conditions. This project addresses a pressing challenge in contemporary energy systems through the integration of rigorous modeling, data-driven methods, and realistic test cases. The results not only confirm the feasibility of this approach but also point to numerous avenues for future research, underscoring its potential to make a substantial and enduring impact on the field.

nodes.title: Measuring Gendered Access Costs to the Philosophical Canon via Steiner Trees and Path Analysis
nodes.authors: Suda, Towa*; Takedomi, Yuka; Hashimoto, Susumu
nodes.abstract: Background. The history of philosophy has often been narrated as an intellectual network centered on “Great Men” [1]. Feminist philosophy argues that core values such as reason and universality have been historically constructed around implicitly male subjects, topologically marginalizing female philosophers [2,3]. While recent quantitative studies show that articles about females generally exhibit lower centrality in hyperlink networks [4], philosophy’s intellectual system is uniquely shaped by a privileged core: the “canon.” In this system, what matters is not overall network centrality, but rather connectivity to the privileged core—access to authenticity. This study analyzes gender bias in access to the canon by simulating access through graph structures representing connectivity, using Steiner trees and shortest paths.  Methodology. We constructed a cross-lingual intersection graph using Wikipedia and Wikidata [5], capturing the 1-hop neighborhood of 130 canonical 20th-century philosophers and the lateral connections among them. We ensured cross-lingual comparability by retaining only entities present in four target languages (EN, FR, DE, JA). We model the network as a directed graph and compute two substructures: (1) approximate minimum directed Steiner trees rooted at philosopher nodes to the canonical set, representing the minimal knowledge set required to reach the canon; and (2) unweighted shortest paths. We define a centrality score C(v, l) for a node v regarding a gender label l ∈ {f,m} based on the proportion of shortest paths from gendered sources s ∈ Vl to canonical targets t ∈ T that pass through v.  Results. Contrary to the hypothesis that female philosophers incur higher access costs, our Steiner Tree analysis reveals that connectivity costs are nearly identical between genders across all datasets. Females do not suffer from lower network efficiency; they achieve canonical access through equally efficient but separate subnetworks. However, path composition differs drastically. Shortest paths originating from female philosophers contain other female entities at a rate 3.4 to 4.4 times higher than paths from males (Fig. 1). Permutation tests confirm this homophily is statistically significant (p < 0.01). Ranking analysis further identifies that female engagement is routed through specific hubs (e.g., “Feminism”, “Simone de Beauvoir”) that are peripheral in male paths.  Contribution. By focusing on connectivity to the canon, we elucidate the structural dimension of gender bias. Bias in the history of philosophy manifests not as a disparity in efficiency, but as a structural segregation of pathways. Female philosophers rely on a distinct female genealogy to bridge the topological gap to the canon.  [1] R. Collins. The sociology of philosophies. Belknap Press (1998). [2] M. Le Doeuff. The philosophical imaginary. Stanford Univ. Press (1989). [3] M. E. Waithe. In Methodological reflections on women’s contribution..., Springer (2020). [4] C. Wagner et al. ICWSM ’15, 454–463 (2015). [5] T. Suda, S. Hashimoto, Y. Takedomi. IPSJ Symp. Ser. Jinmoncom, 117–122 (2025).

nodes.title: Rising Dispersion in Country-Level Academic Mobility Rankings from ORCID-Derived Flow Networks
nodes.abstract: We study whether the global academic mobility system has become more stratified in recent years by constructing country-to-country mobility flow networks from longitudinal affiliation episodes in the ORCID Public Data File (2025 release). From ORCID affiliation transitions, we build directed weighted networks where edge weights represent cross-border mobility volume between countries (country codes normalized via ISO 3166-1 mappings). To quantify structural position in these mobility networks, we compute country scores using SpringRank, a generative, flow-based ranking method for directed networks. We then perform a temporal analysis using centered sliding windows (e.g., 3-year windows centered at each year) to obtain yearly distributions of country SpringRank scores. To summarize system-level inequality, we track the weighted dispersion (weighted standard deviation) of country scores in each window, using total mobility volume as weights.  Across the observation period (2007--2025), we observe a marked increase in score dispersion beginning around 2020, indicating a widening separation between countries that consistently occupy high-ranked positions in global mobility flows and those that remain peripheral. Because the dispersion metric aggregates over all countries, it provides a compact view of system-level stratification beyond individual country trajectories. These findings are consistent with an increasingly uneven mobility landscape, where a smaller set of countries concentrates a growing share of high-rank positions and mobility influence. Our dataset release and end-to-end pipeline enable independent verification and extension of the analysis.

nodes.title: Universal Subtree-Size Scaling in Hyperbolic Minimum Spanning Trees
nodes.abstract:
Hyperbolic random graphs provide a geometric model for complex networks with hierarchical organization and scale-free degree statistics. A single parameter $\alpha$, $0.5 \leq \alpha \leq 1$, controls how strongly nodes deviate from a uniform distribution on the hyperbolic disk; varying $\alpha$ changes the degree exponent by reshaping how much mass concentrates near the origin [1]. From such graphs we extract the \emph{hyperbolic minimum spanning tree} (HMST): each node connects to its nearest neighbor with smaller radial coordinate (except the node of minimal radius). The HMST retains the scale-free signature of the ambient network: the CCDF of HMST degrees follows a power law proportional to $k^{-2\alpha}$, hence it is strongly $\alpha$-dependent [2,3].  In contrast, we observe a universality phenomenon: the HMST { f subtree size} distribution is remarkably insensitive to $\alpha$. Across a broad range of $\alpha$ values, the decay exponent stays confined to a narrow interval, approximately $	au\in[1,1.09]$, despite substantial changes in degree heterogeneity. This suggests that while $\alpha$ tunes local connectivity, the hierarchical decomposition induced by the HMST rule is governed by a robust geometric mechanism.  A stochastic geometric description yields an equation for the expected subtree size. Let $S(r)$ denote the expected size of the entire subtree rooted at a node of radius $r$, including the node itself. Each direct child at radius $s>r$ contributes its own expected subtree $S(s)$, giving  egin{equation} S(r)=1+\int_{u=r}^{R} K(r,u)\,S(u)\,du,\qquad K(r,u)=\kappa_{\alpha}\,e^{-r/2}e^{u/2}, \label{eq:Volterra_subtree} \end{equation} which is a linear Volterra equation of the second kind with a separable kernel. Its solution implies an asymptotic power-law tail for the \emph{subtree size distribution}: if $\mathcal{S}$ denotes the (random) subtree size of a uniformly sampled node in the HMST, then $\Pr\{\mathcal{S}>s\}\propto s^{-	au(\alpha)}$ for large $s$, where $	au(\alpha) = \alpha/(\alpha^2-0.5 \alpha+0.5)$ .  Numerical simulations for $\alpha=0.6,0.7,0.8,$ and $0.9$ confirm the theoretical prediction, showing close agreement between measured subtree size distributions and the analytical exponents $	au(\alpha)$. These findings suggest that HMST hierarchies obey a robust, nearly parameter-invariant scaling law, and highlight geometry-derived trees as promising backbones for navigation and robustness studies [3].   igskip {\small 
oindent[1] D.Krioukov et al., "Hyperbolic Geometry of Complex Networks", \href{https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.82.036106}{	extit{Physical Review E}}, 2010.\ 
oindent[2] D. Ficzere et al., ''Structural Vulnerability of Greedy Routing in Hyperbolic Embedded Networks'', \href{https://dl.ifip.org/db/conf/cnsm/cnsm2025/1571195911.pdf}{In Proc. of 	extit{CNSM 2025}} \ 
oindent[3] A. Majd'{a}n, ''Scaffolds in Large Information Transmission Networks'', PhD dissertation, Chapter 2. \href{https://drive.google.com/file/d/1WfAhGTMQvLDjAumAbmiay4BZFGOAvFGY/view?usp=sharing}{In preparation.} }

nodes.title: Signed percolation in neuronal-network models
nodes.authors: Wang, Annie*; Litwin-Kumar, Ashok; Porter, Mason
nodes.abstract: Percolation, which was first introduced by Broadbent and Hammersley [5], has been studied widely in diverse applications [3, 4]. One can examine a percolation process to determine when a system is connected in a macroscopic way. Percolation has been generalized to many scenarios, including epidemic spreading on networks [6], urban traffic and transportation [7], and cascading failures in power grids [8]. However, in many physical systems, interactions between sites can either promote or inhibit spread. Signed percolation processes that capture such phenomena are understudied. Due to the existence of negative edge weights, the growth of the giant-cluster size is not monotonic in such processes.  Based on the Watts threshold model [1] and the work of Gleeson et al. [2], we study a two-state model with 0 and 1 as the states of nodes, where 0 stands for being inactive and 1 stands for being active. We suppose that all nodes have the same threshold, and we update each node to be active if the input signal from its neighbors is at least the threshold. If not, we set the state of the node to be inactive. We first study possible phase transitions of our model on a configuration model with a Poisson degree distribution. We choose the edge weights uniformly at random from 1 and −1. We then update the states of nodes asynchronously and observe a very sharp phase transition in the giant-cluster size as we vary the threshold, the Poisson parameter, and the initial fraction of active nodes. To then consider a more realistic model, we examine a stochastic block model with different Gaussian weight distributions within and between its blocks. However, we do not observe any phase transitions.  To gain further insights, we examine our threshold model on tree networks in which the top layer is the root and only the bottom layer has edges with negative weights. We observe oscillations in the giant-cluster size for synchronous state updates but not for asynchronous updates. Our theoretical analysis and numerical simulations reveal that these oscillations depend on the number of active nodes in the bottom few layers, rather than on the number of active layers, which are layers with at least one active node. We also observe these oscillations in the stochastic block model with synchronous updates but not in our configuration model (for both synchronous and asynchronous updates). We never observe such oscillations when we consider asynchronous updates.  With our simulations as a guide, we are working on a theoretical explanation of our observations and how activity propagates from one part of a neuronal network to another.  [1] Watts, D.J., A simple model of global cascades on random networks, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 99(9) 5766–5771 (2002) [2] Gleeson, J.P. and Cahalane, D.J., Seed size strongly affects cascades on random networks, Phys. Rev. E. 75(5) 056103 (2007) [3] Newman, M., Networks (2nd ed.), Oxford University Press (2018) [4] Diaz-Diaz, F. et al., Signed Networks: Theory, methods, and applications, arXiv:2511.17247 (2025) [5] Broadbent, S.R. and Hammersley, J.M., Percolation processes: I. Crystals and mazes. Math. Proc. Camb. Philos. Soc. 53(3) 629–641 (1957) [6] Cardy, J.L. and Grassberger, P., Epidemic models and percolation, J. Phys. A: Math. Gen. 18(6) L267–L271 (1985) [7] Li, D. et al., Percolation transition in dynamical traffic network with evolving critical bottlenecks, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 112(3) 669–672 (2015) [8] Schafer, B. et al., Dynamically induced cascading failures in power grids. Nat. Commun. 9(1):1975 (2018)

nodes.title: Benchmarking hyperbolic network embedders for link prediction
nodes.authors: Jankowski, Robert*; Celińska-Kopczyńska, Dorota; Kitsak, Maksim
nodes.abstract: Link prediction is a fundamental task in network analysis, aiming to infer missing or future edges from incomplete data. Hyperbolic geometry provides a natural latent space for this problem, as it captures both hierarchical organization and degree heterogeneity typical of real networks. We present a unified benchmark of hyperbolic network embedding methods for link prediction, comparing approaches from network science, machine learning, and algorithmic graph theory. Using a common evaluation protocol, we test all methods on synthetic networks from the S1/H2 model and real-world graphs. Our results reveal regime-dependent performance, with different embedders excelling under different levels of clustering, sparsity, and degree heterogeneity.

nodes.title: Robustness of interdependent scele-free onion networks
nodes.abstract: We present the theoretical analysis of the robustness of interdependent scale-free onion networks, which is the most robust network structure against both random failure and targeted attacks with extremely assortative degree correlation between nearby node connections. The functioning fraction of nodes of interdependent networks at each step of the cascading failure and the final giant component fraction are calculated by numerically solving the exact analytical equations governing the behavior of the system.  Various important quantities such as the critical values of the initial remaining node fraction at which the first-order collapse of the giant components occurs, the critical values of interdependency between the networks at which the first-order (abrupt) collapse of the giant component becomes the second-order (continuous), and the values of the critical exponents are also calculated.

nodes.title: Using Spatial Networks to Encode the Geometry of Deep Learning-based Black Hole Accretion Simulations
nodes.authors: Tan, Chester*; Bös, Cedric; Fromm, Christian; Scholtes, Ingo
nodes.abstract:
General Relativistic Magnetohydrodynamics (GRMHD) simulations are fundamental for interpreting horizon-scale images of supermassive black holes, such as those captured by the Event Horizon Telescope [0]. However, these simulations, performed by codes like the Black Hole Accretion Code (BHAC) [1], are computationally intensive, often requiring millions of CPU hours to resolve the complex dynamics of the accreted plasma and magnetic fields. A significant barrier to developing fast neural surrogates for these systems is the complex geometry of the data: BHAC employs block-based Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) [2] to dynamically concentrate resolution where needed, resulting in highly irregular, non-Euclidean data structures that defy standard grid-based deep learning approaches.  We describe ongoing work to bridge this gap by fundamentally treating the simulation domain as a spatial network, which represents the irregular AMR mesh points as nodes in a network, This network representation allows us to use the Geometry-Informed Neural Operator (GINO) [3], an architecture that utilizes Graph Neural Operators (GNO) to perform kernel integration via message passing within local neighborhoods (Figure 1). This network-based encoding allows the model to process BHAC's native adaptive grids directly, maintaining discretization invariance -- the ability to learn the underlying solution operator independent of the specific mesh resolution. The GNO encoder transforms the physical network into a latent regular grid where efficient Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) compute global interactions, before a graph-based decoder projects the solution back onto the target geometry. We demonstrate the suitability of this network-centric approach for learning the temporal evolution of black hole accretion flows, offering a path toward accelerating parameter space exploration by orders of magnitude compared to traditional numerical solvers.   igskip {\small   
oindent[0] EHTC et al., The persistent shadow of the supermassive black hole of M 87. I. Observations, calibration, imaging, and analysis, 	extit{Astronomy \& Astrophysics} 	extbf{681}, A79 (2024).\   
oindent[1] O. Porth et al., The black hole accretion code (BHAC). 	extit{Computational Astrophysics and Cosmology} 	extbf{4}, 1 (2017).\   
oindent[2] H. Olivares et al., Constrained transport and adaptive mesh refinement in the Black Hole Accretion Code. 	extit{Astronomy \& Astrophysics} 	extbf{629}, A61 (2019).\   
oindent[3] Z. Li et al., Geometry-Informed Neural Operator for Large-Scale 3D PDEs. 	extit{Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems} 	extbf{36} (2023). }

nodes.title: Absence-aware surprisal for interpretable anomaly detection in temporal networked systems
nodes.abstract: A central challenge in networked complex systems lies in analyzing high-dimensional temporal data, where the number of measured variables (nodes, features, or layers) vastly exceeds available samples. This is common in temporal and multilayer networks~\cite{multi}, where the ambient space grows exponentially while observations grow only polynomially, leading to sparsity and the breakdown of classical asymptotic guarantees~\cite{wainwright2019high}.  Quantitative analysis of complex and networked systems typically assumes that meaningful patterns arise from correlations among present features alone. This overlooks a critical insight: structure emerges not only from what is present, but also from patterns of absence. When expected patterns are missing in a specific datum, this carries diagnostic information that most methods ignore. We term these absent population-prevalent elements \textit{missing commonalities (MCs)}.   \textbf{Learning via Surprisability (LvS)} is an absence-aware surprisal method designed to identify MCs~\cite{mokryn2025interpretable}. LvS operates on data where each time bin or datum is a discrete probability distribution over features. In temporal networks, each bin can be viewed as a distribution over node, edge, or motif features, so LvS defines an interpretable transformation of dynamics on/of the network into surprisal-based profiles. First, LvS constructs a population-wide reference model (a ``timeline center’’) over all features. Jensen–Shannon divergence (JSD) then quantifies how much each time bin’s state deviates from this expected network state and remains well defined even under extreme sparsity. Crucially, JSD decomposes per feature, allowing LvS to attribute divergence to specific overrepresented or missing elements and to construct a compact surprisal signature for each time bin.   \noindent\textbf{Empirical results on temporal networked systems.} On the SWaT industrial control system, a water‑treatment sensor–actuator network with 450{,}000 time‑stamped records, LvS clearly outperforms standard unsupervised detectors such as Isolation Forest, LOF, LSTM, and an autoencoder in AUC while offering competitive $F_1$ (Fig.~\ref{fig:swat}). Beyond performance, LvS provides interpretable, feature‑level explanations of attacks on the network by highlighting unusual activations and missing commonalities in the evolving system state. Additional experiments on global mortality and historical text, viewed as temporal population and communication networks, indicate that absence‑aware surprisal profiles generalize across domains, enabling interpretable anomaly detection and change‑point discovery in high‑dimensional temporal networks.

nodes.title: From Hub Amplification to Periphery Dominance: Regime Shifts Under Trust–Uncertainty Gated Networks
nodes.authors: Masoumi, Razieh*; Lin, Yuru; Biswas, Ahana
nodes.abstract: Authors/Affiliation Razieh Masoumi, Ahana Biswas, and Yuru Lin School of Computing and Information, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA, USA  Abstract In this work, we model collective belief evolution under simulated radicalization in social networks where trust and distrust toward the same source can coexist. Going beyond influence mechanisms based solely on reinforcing trust, we formalize this ambivalence—trust in information/competence alongside distrust in motives—which reveals a pathway to polarization and extremism that standard opinion-dynamics models miss, since distrust is not simply a lack of trust (Zhang et al., 2022).  We introduce Gated Network Credence, a network-dynamics framework for belief evolution. Each directed tie maps coexisting trust and distrust to net trust and uncertainty, capturing willingness to rely on a source and tolerance for contradictions between trust and distrust as distinct drivers of social influence. Dual thresholds on net trust and uncertainty gate social influence, yielding a directed, nonsymmetric influence graph whose belief evolution follows continuous-time Laplacian consensus. We show that multiple zero eigenvalues indicate fragmentation, and that the left nullspace determines which nodes dominate the final belief, linking network filtering to long-run outcomes via spectral structure.  To test belief evolution across radicalization scenarios, we vary four empirically grounded mechanisms: trust–distrust coupling (rho; co-variation of trust and distrust on ties), prestige bias (p_s; trust toward highly connected nodes), promoter prevalence (x; fraction of high-degree nodes that promote a belief), and polarization degree (psi; initial belief gap between communities). We study these mechanisms across Erdős–Rényi, Barabási–Albert, and modular networks, and across three trust datasets: Bitcoin-OTC reputation, Wikipedia voting, and a political actor network derived from U.S. legislators’ ideology and interactions on X.  Exploring the trust–uncertainty threshold space (T_T, U_T) identifies three credence regimes characterized by trust propensity (Open vs. Guarded) and uncertainty screening strictness (Rigid vs. Fluid). Regimes F (Guarded–Fluid) and G (Open–Rigid) yield sharply different radicalization outcomes. Under regime F, promoter prevalence and prestige bias push influence toward the promoted belief values; stronger hub structure amplifies radicalization, weaker coupling further increases hub dominance, and modularity limits but does not reverse this effect. In contrast, under regime G, strict uncertainty screening prunes hub-supporting ties and shifts dominance to the periphery; modular networks are more resistant than other topologies. Results for Bitcoin-OTC and Wikipedia match these patterns. The political actor network is richer: ideology scores and interactions yield net trust, uncertainty, and polarization. Party-varying prestige bias and promoter prevalence reproduce the regime contrast: under regime F, hubs dominate; under regime G, influence shifts to non-hubs in the targeted community, even when the promoted belief comes from the opposing party.  Overall, our results show that belief formation depends not only on extremists, prestige, or topology, but also on how influence is gated. Trust and uncertainty thresholds induce distinct credence regimes with sharply different outcomes, and their spectral structure reveals how topology amplifies or constrains polarization and radicalization.

nodes.title: Beyond Retweets: Contrasting User–User and User–Content in Bow-Tie Decomposition
nodes.authors: Guarino, Stefano; Mounin, Ayoub; Caldarelli, Guido; Saracco, Fabio*
nodes.abstract: Most research on online social media focuses on interactions among users, with limited attention on the interactions between users and content. Indeed, ignoring content popularity when studying user interactions may lead to misleading conclusions: viral posts are far less informative than “standard” ones, since their diffusion does not necessarily reflect users’ preferences but rather the dynamics of virality itself. In this paper, we compare the insights coming when online debates on X/Twitter are described either considering only user–user interactions or including user–content interactions. Our main focus is the statistical significance of the bow-tie decomposition of the retweet network. The bow-tie decompositionof directed networks was first introduced by Broder et al. to describe the structure of the early WWW [1].  In Ref. [2], it was shown that in nearly all discursive communities across online debates in different languages, bow-tie structures are both informative (i.e. involving almost all accounts) and statistically significant (i.e. not simply due to random noise). We analyse three Italian political debates on X/Twitter collected in 2022. We compare results obtained studying the retweet network, i.e. a directed network of reposting where nodes represent accounts, and the user–content bipartite network. Using these two frameworks, we evaluate the statistical significance of quantities involved in the bow-tie decomposition. As a benchmark, we rely on maximum-entropy null models for complex networks, which have proven versatile in many contexts [3]. Our results show that in several relevant cases, significance patterns are reversed: quantities that are significantly larger than expected in one representation are significantly smaller in the other. This finding has several non-trivial implications. Among the most relevant ones, the statistical significance of bow-tie components depends on the chosen representation, meaning that interpretation hinges on the interaction type under consideration. Since social platforms inherently involve both user–user and user–content dynamics, neglecting either dimension risks producing a distorted view.  References [1] Andrei Broder et al., Computer Networks (2000). [2] Mattia Mattei et al., Scientific Reports 2022 12:1 (2022). [3] Giulio Cimini et al., Nature Reviews Physics (2019).

nodes.title: Chromatin structure-function relationships revealed by network approaches to 4D epigenome organisation
nodes.authors: Aliaga, Benoit; Raynal, Flavien; Pancaldi, Vera*
nodes.abstract: Recent technological advances have allowed us to map chromatin conformation and uncover the spatial organization of the genome inside the nucleus. These experiments have revealed the complexities of genome folding, characterized by the presence of loops and domains at different scales which can change across development and in different cell types. There is strong evidence for a relationship between the topological properties of chromatin contacts and cellular phenotype. Chromatin can be represented as a network, in which genomic fragments are the nodes and connections represent experimentally observed spatial proximity of two genomically distant regions in a specific cell type or biological condition. With this approach we can consider a variety of chromatin features in association with the 3D structure, investigating how nuclear chromatin organization can be related to gene regulation, replication, malignancy, phenotypic variability and plasticity.  We briefly review the results obtained on genome architecture through network theoretic approaches. As previously observed in protein-protein interaction networks and many types of non-biological networks, external conditions could shape network topology through a yet unidentified structure-function relationship.   One of the applications we have worked on involved studying the organisation of genes in the 3D nucleus according to their evolutionary age. Cancer cells are highly plastic, allowing them to adapt to various environmental conditions. Genes related to basic cellular processes evolved in ancient species, while more specialized genes appeared later with multicellularity (metazoan genes) or even after mammals evolved. Transcriptomic analyses have shown that ancient genes are typically up-regulated in cancer, while metazoan-origin genes are often inactivated. Oncogenesis is characterized by alterations in chromatin organization and the reactivation of unicellular phenotypes at both metabolic and transcriptional levels.   We have studied the assortativity of genes of different ages in the 3D chromatin networks of different cell types assessed by promoter-centred chromatin conformation capture.  Our findings reveal significant topological changes in chromatin organization during cell differentiation, with specific patterns involving Polycomb repression and RNA Polymerase II pausing, being reversed during oncogenesis. Going beyond the non-random organization of genes and chromatin features in the 3D epigenome, we suggest that these patterns lead to preferential interactions among ancient, intermediate, and recent genes, mediated by RNA Polymerase II, Polycomb complexes, and nuclear lamina, respectively.  We will present our perspective on the broad and insightful application of network theory in 4D genomics capturing 3D organisation across time.

nodes.title: Dynamics of Defensive and Malicious Worm Co-Propagation across Networked Systems
nodes.abstract: The rapid expansion of the Internet of Things (IoT) has vastly increased the attack surface of networked systems. In response to the persistent threat of self-propagating malware (``black worms''), it has been proposed the use of ``white worms'' as a countermeasure. These worms would be benign, self-replicating programs that traverse networks to notify owners, facilitate patching, and secure devices autonomously. This idea raises a central tension for cyberdefense: the more a defensive agent respects ethical constraints on autonomy and system modification, the less effective it may become at suppressing malicious activity. This work formalizes that trade-off and quantifies its consequences.  We develop a co-propagation model for a malicious worm and an ethically designed white worm within a network epidemiology framework. Under the homogeneous mixing approximation, we derive closed-form expressions for basic reproduction numbers and obtain analytical thresholds for botnet emergence. We then study spreading on networks with various topologies, showing that structure decisively shapes outcomes. In heterogeneous networks, the malicious worm’s advantage on high-degree nodes increases the burden on the defender. For the same ethical and kinetic parameters, botnets are larger and longer-lived than in homogeneous topologies. Conversely, when white worms can reach hubs early and activate promptly, they substantially reduce botnet prevalence and shorten the time the botnet remains above operationally relevant size thresholds.  Taken together, the results reveal a fundamental design dilemma as effective autonomous defense requires degrees of initiative and willingness to modify systems that challenge the ethical premise of minimally invasive agents. Our framework makes this tension explicit by tying macroscopic security outcomes to interpretable behavioral parameters (owner responsiveness and defender autonomy) and to measurable properties of network structure.

nodes.title: Spatial regionalization of multivariate data based on optimal information compression
nodes.abstract: Regionalization seeks to delineate the spatial areas into contiguous and homogeneous regimes based on shared characteristics. However, existing methods often depend on user-defined parameters or are restricted to single attributes, failing to capture the complex interdependence among socioeconomic variables. To address this, we propose a nonparametric, information-based framework that extends regionalization to multiple attributes using the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. Instead of treating each attribute independently (decoupled covariates), our method incorporates microdata of coupled covariates (e.g., joint counts of race and income) to exploit the correlation between attributes for improved data compression. Synthetic tests indicate that the coupled method robustly recovers ground-truth partitions in regimes where decoupled approaches fail. To further demonstrate the practical utility, we also apply our method to the U.S. Northeast megalopolis, results show that the coupled attributes reveal more granular and meaningful spatial regimes than the decoupled method while also achieves better data compression.

nodes.title: Nonparametric regionalization of spatial time series data
nodes.abstract: Regionalization aims to partition a spatial domain into contiguous regions that share similar characteristics. It helps us understand and manage large areas, for example in environmental planning, climate zoning, and defining socio-economic or ecological regions. Existing approaches typically rely on static spatial snapshots rather than evolving time series. Meanwhile, most time series clustering methods ignore spatial structure or only enforce spatial continuity through ad hoc regularization, and often require the number of regions to be chosen a priori. In this work we propose a nonparametric framework for regionalizing spatial time series based on the minimum description length (MDL) principle. Spatial locations are modeled as nodes in an adjacency network, where edges represent geographic proximity. The method performs a fast greedy agglomerative merge process, with runtime complexity on the order of O(N logN) for N nodes, to jointly identify a spatial partition and a set of representative driver patterns that minimize the total code length required to encode all time series. We demonstrate on noisy synthetic data that the method accurately recovers the planted regional structure and driver series, and can extract meaningful structure from real environmental and geographical time series, including discrete air-quality indices and continuous vegetation index records. Overall, the method provides a principled and efficient framework that yields spatially contiguous regions with interpretable temporal patterns, allowing natural regions to emerge directly from the data itself.

nodes.title: How Degree-Dependent Interactions Reshape Hub Survival in Ecological Networks
nodes.authors: Lee, Hae Seong*; Lee, Deok-Sun; Lee, Sang Hoon; Suweis, Samir; Park, Hye Jin
nodes.abstract: The population dynamics of ecological systems with complex structures are often studied using the generalized random Lotka-Volterra model. Previous studies have highlighted the crucial role of hubs in biodiversity. However, this strong influence may primarily result from their large number of connections, as these studies often assume that interaction strengths are uniform across the network.  In real ecological systems, however, interaction strengths often correlate with a species' degree (the number of species it interacts with). In this study, we introduce degree-dependent interaction strengths into the generalized random Lotka-Volterra model and investigate how this variation shapes biodiversity.   In our model, we assume that the interaction strengths are independent random variables that follow a Gaussian distribution whose mean and variance depend on the degrees of interaction species. Specifically, the mean interaction strength that species i exerts on species j is proportional to (k_i^\alpha) times (k_j^\beta), where k_i and k_j are the degrees of species i and j. The exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$ regulate the influence of hubs in the system. We find that according to the values of alpha, the \alpha-\beta plane is divided into the hub-dominating phase and the hub-suppressed phase.  In the hub-dominating phase, hub species show a higher survival probability than low-degree species. On the other hand, hub species survive less than low-degree species in the hub-suppressed phase. Our findings indicate that the correlation between interaction strengths and species degrees significantly impacts species persistence, leading to distinct phases where hubs either thrive or decline.

nodes.title: A Class of Optimal Directed Graphs for Network Synchronization
nodes.authors: Lu, Susie; Urschel, John; Liu, Ji*
nodes.abstract: In a paper by Nishikawa and Motter, a quantity called the normalized spread of the Laplacian eigenvalues is used to measure the synchronizability of certain network dynamics. Through simulations, and without theoretical validation, it is conjectured that among all simple directed graphs with a fixed number of vertices and arcs, the optimal value of this quantity is achieved if the Laplacian spectrum satisfies a specific pattern. This work proves this conjecture and further shows that the conjectured spectral condition is not only sufficient but also necessary. Moreover, the work proves that the optimal Laplacian spectrum is always achievable by a class of almost regular directed graphs, which can be constructed through an inductive algorithm.

nodes.title: Network-Based Visualization of Intra-Cluster Variability in High-Dimensional Data
nodes.authors: Plesníková, Kristýna*; Kudělka, Miloš; Ochodková, Eliška; Kriegová, Eva
nodes.abstract: Understanding intra-cluster variability in high-dimensional data remains challenging with standard distribution plots. We introduce two network-based representations—Confidence Interval (CI) networks and Interquartile Range (IQR) networks—that encode attribute variability as structural features of a similarity network. Starting from an LRNet-constructed sample similarity graph, clusters are identified using the Louvain method. For each cluster, a centroid vector (mean or median) is computed and augmented with attribute-specific deviation vectors defined by confidence interval or interquartile range bounds, isolating the contribution of individual attributes. These vectors are embedded into a network using cosine similarity and a k-nearest neighbor rule, yielding stable and interpretable topologies. Application to the IRIS dataset shows that IQR networks robustly recover species-level structure, while CI networks reflect uncertainty in attribute means through connectivity patterns. Compared to conventional boxplots, the proposed networks provide an integrated view of variability and attribute interactions, offering a scalable and interpretable tool for network-based analysis of high-dimensional and biomedical data.

nodes.title: Brain-Activity Flow on Brain Networks Uncovered by Topological Signal Processing
nodes.authors: Petruso, Flavia*; Preti, Maria Giulia; Van De Ville, Dimitri
nodes.abstract: The brain is a complex system where regions do not operate in isolation, but interact continuously through a network of connections, coordinating activity and enabling the emergence of high-level cognitive processes. Traditional node-centric analyses, including approaches based on graph signal processing (GSP), have provided first insights into these interactions. However, they often model connections as static links, thereby failing to fully capture the dynamic nature of information flow between brain regions.  In this work, we introduce topological signal processing (TSP), a recent framework designed to deal with higher-order structures and signals defined on complex topological domains such as graphs. We demonstrate the applicability of this approach to study brain communication by applying fundamental TSP operators to resting-state blood oxygenation-level–dependent (BOLD) functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data from the Human Connectome Project (HCP). We construct a 2-dimensional simplicial complex as the clique complex of a brain graph obtained by retaining the top 10% strongest structural connections across subjects (Fig. 1a). From the original fMRI time series, we then derive edge-level brain signals that capture lagged dynamics between pairs of brain regions, enabling the investigation of directed patterns that underlie inter-regional interactions. Domain-informed analysis of these signals reveals that, at rest, certain brain regions systematically precede or follow others over time, following both irrotational and circulant patterns. In particular, the discrete divergence operator highlights heterogeneous regional involvement within dorsal attention and visual networks, exhibiting strong positive and negative divergence patterns that suggest asymmetric roles in activity flow and integration (Fig. 1b-c).  Overall, our work represents one of the first applications of TSP to brain imaging data and paves the way for a communication-oriented analysis of brain networks that can explicitly account for higher-order interactions beyond node-centric representations.

nodes.title: Identifying high impact genes using single-cell RNA seq data through deep learning
nodes.authors: Levy, Orr*; He, Yongjian; Klein, Vered; Wang, Xu-Wen
nodes.abstract: Gene expression data is essential for understanding how genes are regulated and interact within biological systems, providing insights into disease pathways and potential therapeutic targets. Gene knockout has proven to be a fundamental technique in molecular biology, allowing the investigation of the function of specific genes in an organism, as well as in specific cell types. However, gene expression patterns are quite heterogeneous in single-cell transcriptional data from a uniform environment, representing different cell states, which produce cell-type and cell-specific gene knockout impacts. A computational method that can predict the single-cell resolution knockout impact is still lacking. Identifying high impact genes and their knockout outcome in scRNA-seq data is challenging due to the extreme complexity and cell-type specificity of gene regulatory networks (GRNs), the inherent stochasticity of gene expression, and the difficulty of predicting transcriptional outcomes following random damage. These challenges are further compounded by technical noise, data sparsity, and clustering artifacts intrinsic to scRNA-seq data. Here, we present a data-driven framework that overcome the need to extract the entire map of interactions in the gene regulatory network (GRN) for learning the mapping between gene expression profiles derived from gene assemblages, enabling the accurate prediction of perturbed expression profiles following knockout (KO) for any cell, without relying on prior perturbed data. We systematically validated our framework using synthetic data generated from gene regulatory dynamics models, two mouse knockout single-cell datasets, and high throughput in vitro CRISPRi Perturb-seq data. Our results demonstrate that the framework can accurately predict both expression profiles and KO effects at the single-cell level. Our approach provides a generalizable tool for inferring gene function at single-cell resolution, offering new opportunities to study genetic perturbations in contexts where large-scale experimental screens are infeasible.

nodes.title: How stubborn anticonformists drive social hysteresis - quenched anticonformity in the multistate q-voter model on networks
nodes.abstract: Discontinuous phase transitions have, for a long time, captured the interest of physicists, economists, and social scientists alike, because they are often associated with empirically verified phenomena such as tipping points, critical mass, and social hysteresis. For this reason, opinion-dynamics models that exhibit rich phase behavior are especially relevant in computational social science. Among them, the q-voter model is particularly interesting due to its inherent group-based influence corresponding to complex contagion, which has been shown to play a significant role in various social scenarios. Its multistate variant is thus an attractive basis for an agent-based approach to simulate discrete decision-making. Previous studies of the multistate q-voter model with anticonformity on complete graphs uncovered a counterintuitive result. Quenching the type of social response of agents, i.e., changing homogeneous agents with varying behavior into heterogeneous agents with fixed behavior, produces discontinuous phase transitions. This is contrary to the common expectation that quenched heterogeneity smooths transitions. However, the previous analysis was limited to the mean-field regime; hence, we extend it to random graphs to verify whether the effect is robust to changes in the underlying social network on which the dynamics occur. Our contribution is two-fold: (1) we apply the pair approximation method to the multi-state q-voter model with anticonformity, providing an analytical approach to both annealed and quenched systems with an arbitrary number of states; (2) using pair approximation and Monte Carlo simulations, we show that the phenomenon of quench-driven discontinuity persists beyond the complete graph, specifically on random graphs and Barabási-Albert scale-free networks.

nodes.title: When is Information Seeking Counterproductive?: The Impact of Vaccination Status and Source Trust on Beliefs in Mixed-Quality Information Networks
nodes.authors: Acierno, Jane*; Butler, Lucy; Davis, Jessica; Constantino, Sara; Swire-Thompson, Briony
nodes.abstract: Misinformation poses a serious threat to public health and scientific trust [1], motivating efforts to promote media literacy [2]. Many such interventions assume that encouraging people to seek information from trusted sources will guide them towards higher quality information, thus improving belief accuracy [3]. Yet individuals differ in which sources they trust [4], raising concerns that interventions prompting open-ended search may not function as intended in mixed-quality information environments [5]. The present study examines how a source-based media literacy intervention shapes information seeking and belief updating within an information environment containing both high- and low-quality sources. Beyond the intervention, we also examine how information seeking behavior impacts belief updating, and whether this differs between vaccinated and unvaccinated participants.   Participants (N = 668) were randomized to a control condition or a graphical media literacy intervention condition adapted from Guess et al. [2], which emphasized investigating source credibility and consulting multiple trusted sources (Fig. 1a). Next, participants rated their belief in six vaccine-related claims (three true, three false), then completed an open-ended information-seeking task (Fig. 1b). On each trial, participants could sample up to 100 health provider avatars representing either trustworthy (e.g., physicians) or untrustworthy (e.g., homeopaths) sources, which predominantly endorsed either accurate or inaccurate information, respectively. Participants then re-rated belief in each claim.  The media literacy intervention did not impact the amount or quality of information seeking, or enhance belief accuracy. However, seeking high-quality information served as a corrective mechanism: sampling more trustworthy sources increased belief accuracy (Fig. 1c). Conversely, sampling untrustworthy sources reduced accuracy, especially among unvaccinated participants. Although vaccinated and unvaccinated participants expressed similarly low trust in low-quality sources, vaccinated participants exhibited substantially higher trust in high-quality sources, producing a sharper perceived distinction between source types. This distinction predicted sampling behavior, and helps explain why vaccinated participants were far less likely to sample untrustworthy sources. Finally, we developed a Bayesian belief-updating model to assess whether each population integrated sampled evidence rationally. Overall, our findings suggest that media literacy interventions encouraging information seeking may not function as intended within heterogeneous information networks.  [1] S. Lewandowsky, U. K. H. Ecker, J. Cook, J. Appl. Res. Mem. Cogn. 6, 353–369 (2017).  [2] A. M. Guess et al., Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 117, 15536–15545 (2020).  [3] G. Pennycook et al., Psychol. Sci. 31, 770–780 (2020).  [4] A. M. Jones et al., Adv. Prev. Med. 2012, 932741 (2012).  [5] K. Aslett et al., Nature 625, 548–556 (2024).

nodes.title: Comparing War Narratives Through Cross-Lingual Narrative Networks
nodes.abstract: Understanding how different languages portray the same historical events is crucial for identifying and addressing cultural and geopolitical biases in collaborative knowledge production. In this study, we examine how different language editions of Wikipedia describe the same wars, focusing on the portrayal of participating entities across various languages. Our research questions are: Do Wikipedia language editions exhibit distinct and recurring entity-level narrative patterns in their portrayal of wars? Which languages show greater similarity in these narrative patterns?  All data were collected through Wikipedia's official MediaWiki API. Starting from the English Wikipedia category ``List of wars by date,'' we identified wars that started after 1900 and their belligerents. Using these wars as seed articles, we expanded the dataset by sampling related wars and battles through internal links within each article and through Wikipedia categories of the form ``Battles of [War Name].'' For each battle, we then collect the corresponding articles from 20 language editions of Wikipedia, when available. We translated all non-English articles into English using the Google Translation API and applied a connotation frame analysis to the translated text. After deduplication and filtering, the final dataset consists of 2,630 battles from 976 wars.   Using the RIVETER framework [1], we extracted verb-centric agent–verb–theme tuples and constructed an entity-level narrative network for each language. In each network, nodes correspond to entities appearing in the text, and an edge is established between two entities whenever they co-occur within the same agent–verb–theme relation. Each entity is associated with three attributes---power, agency, and sentiment---computed by averaging the verb-level connotation scores provided by the RIVETER corpus over all verbs in which the entity participates (Figure 1). These attributes capture how entities are systematically portrayed in terms of dominance, intentionality, and affect within narrative contexts. For each language, we select the three most frequently mentioned entities across all wars and compute their average power, agency, and sentiment scores by first averaging within each war and then across wars.  Our preliminary results illustrate cross-linguistic narrative patterns. Table 1 reports these scores for English, German, and Chinese as illustrative examples, showing that languages portray their respective countries as less powerful and less agentic. Our future directions include constructing war-level narrative networks for each language by retaining entities associated with the two belligerents of each war. Pairwise similarities between language-specific networks will be computed using the Propagation Kernel method [2] and normalized within each war. For each language pair, we will average the normalized similarities across all shared wars to emphasize narrative similarity rather than coverage differences.   [1] Antoniak, M., Field, A., Mun, J., Walsh, M., Klein, L., \& Sap, M. (2023, July). Riveter: Measuring power and social dynamics between entities. In Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics [2] Neumann, M., Garnett, R., Bauckhage, C., \& Kersting, K. (2016). Propagation kernels: efficient graph kernels from propagated information. Machine learning, 102(2), 209-245

nodes.title: Ethical Visibility: Bridging Network Science, Ethics, and Information Design for Reimagining Global Art representation and inclusiveness
nodes.abstract: This talk explores how Information Design can promote equitable decision-making in representing and including Global South artists in exhibitions. Using an interdisciplinary approach that combines Network Science, Information Design, and philosophical inquiry, it demonstrates how quantitative analysis, social-justice-oriented interpretation, and visual communication can empower artist selection. Focusing on the Venice Biennale 2024 edition, Foreigners Everywhere, as a case study, the talk traces the process from data extraction to visualization, uncovering patterns of cultural bias and systemic exclusion. By integrating scientific and humanistic perspectives, the study proposes innovative pathways for ethical, inclusive, and critically reflective curatorial practices.

nodes.title: Visualizing Genetic Diversity and Scale in the Million Veteran Program
nodes.abstract: This project, a collaboration between the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and the Barabási Lab, explores large-scale genetic associations within the Million Veteran Program. We analyze over 26 million SNPs linked to 1,572 phenotypes across 2,068 genetic traits, representing complex relationships between diseases and shared genetic markers in an interactive network dashboard.  The dashboard integrates network science, information design, and interactive visualization, enabling users to explore genetic diversity through filtering, search, clustering, and degree-based selection. Nodes represent diseases, and edges denote shared SNPs, uncovering underlying patterns and systemic trends. A static version, recognized with the Information is Beautiful Longlist Award (2024), also secured additional funding to expand and advance the project.  By combining large-scale data analysis with human-centered design, this work addresses underrepresentation in genomic research and demonstrates how interactive visualization makes complex biological networks accessible, interpretable, and actionable.

nodes.title: Inferring Network Interactions from Short and Noisy Signals with Deep Learning
nodes.authors: Pavaux, Marion*; De Vico Fallani, Fabrizio; La Rocca, Daria
nodes.abstract: Graph-based representations provide a powerful framework for modeling and analyzing a wide range of complex systems. But in many cases, the connections between the nodes of the network are unknown, and have to be reconstructed from the individual node dynamics. In practice, networks of interest are large and complex, while available observations are limited and noisy, which substantially impairs the robustness of classical connection-inference methods, like Bayesian methods, which struggle in sparse data contexts [1]. For example, in network neuroscience, computational methods of connectivity estimators often fail to reliably infer brain connectivity from short, noisy and non-stationary signals. In this paper, we address the question of network inference  by developing a deep learning-based framework to learn and infer interactions between multivariate time series. Applying this model to simulated electroencephalography (EEG) brain signals, we show that it can more robustly reconstruct spectral connectivity as compared to standard Welch's modified periodogram method. The proposed architecture combines convolutional feature extraction with an attention mechanism and is trained to estimate imaginary coherence, a connectivity measure designed to mitigate volume-conduction effects [2]. We evaluated our framework on short and noisy simulated signals, demonstrating improved connectivity reconstruction in conditions close to real experimental conditions. Moreover, domain-adaptation techniques enable efficient transfer of the model to real electroencephalography data. Overall, our results illustrate how deep learning architectures can be trained to overcome fundamental limitations of classical network-inference techniques when applied to sparse and noisy data. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using deep learning for network inference and suggests that the approach can be extended to other connectivity estimators and to non-neural networked systems.  [1] Young, J.-G., Cantwell, G. T. \& Newman, M. E. J. Journal of Complex Networks (2021). [2] Nolte, G. et al. Clinical Neurophysiology (2004).

nodes.title: Can We Identify Breakthroughs Before They Happen? Predicting Disruptive Science from Manuscript Text and Citation Network Features
nodes.abstract: Disruptive scientific works create knowledge that challenges the status quo, resulting in new innovation trajectories [1, 2]. A disruptive paper does not necessarily get many citations. Instead, it redirects future edges away from its predecessors. The disruption score (D-score) quantifies this redirection, measuring the degree of disruption of a scientific work [1]. However, computing D-scores requires long citation windows, which limits the early identification of disruptive research. This study investigates whether disruption can be predicted using (i) a paper’s text embeddings alone, (ii) a combination of text embeddings and semantic alignment within its citation network, and (iii) limited post-publication citation data.  We analyze ego-centered citation networks for 80,000 focal papers published in journals or conferences between 1980 and 2019 across the fields of Computer Science, Medicine, Biology, and Economics. Paper metadata and disruption scores were obtained from SciSciNet and abstracts from Microsoft Academic Graph. The D-score, developed by Funk and Owen-Smith [1], ranges from -1 to 1, where higher values indicate greater disruption. We apply SPECTER 2.0 [3], a scientific text embedding model trained on large-scale scholarly corpora, to map research articles into a 768-dimensional vector space (Fig. 1). Citation topology is combined with semantic alignment by averaging the individual cosine similarities between the focal paper and its references, the focal paper and its citing papers, and between references and citations. Logistic regression models are employed for classification.   Across fields, integrating both the text embeddings of the focal paper and the semantic alignment of its citation topology consistently yields the strongest performance across the evaluation metrics, outperforming those using either component alone (Fig. 2). Notably, restricting citation data to the first five years post-publication results in comparable performance, suggesting that disruptive works can be identified early without waiting for their full citation network to unfold.  Together, these results suggest that integrating semantic embeddings with citation topology provides a richer characterization of disruptive research than retrospective citation patterns alone. A key direction for future work is to investigate the robustness of these results across other disciplines, larger datasets, higher-order network structures, and shorter temporal windows.  [1] Funk, R. J. & Owen-Smith, J. A Dynamic Network Measure of Technological Change. Management Science (2017). [2] Wu, L., Wang, D. & Evans, J. A. Large teams develop and small teams disrupt science and technology. Nature (2019). [3] Singh, A., D’Arcy, M., Cohan, A., Downey, D. & Feldman, S. SciRepEval: A Multi-Format Benchmark for Scientific Document Representations. in Proceedings of the 2023 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 5548–5566 Figure 1: Each paper in the citation network is a vector embed- ding using SPECTER 2.0. Figure 2: Model performance comparison using Macro F1 scores.

nodes.title: Data-driven contagion models of information diffusion on Bluesky
nodes.abstract: Information online is known to diffuse among people differently depending on the topic. Sensitive or risky topics often diffuse according to a so-called complex contagion process, in which individuals often require multiple, reinforcing exposures before they are likely to contribute to the diffusion. On the other hand, many topics are not risky, and their diffusion is de facto modeled as a simple contagion process. Simple contagions, however, come with additional assumptions that may not match reality. Here, we propose an alternate perspective on online information diffusion in which the likelihood that a user is infected (i.e., joined a discussion) is a particular function of the number of their follows that are infected. Improving over a simple contagion model, our model allows for a nonzero probability of infection with no infected neighbors as well as a saturating probability of infection less than 1. We validate our model against more than 10 million posts on the online social media platform Bluesky. Our model suggests an interim paradigm of information diffusion that captures behavior not well-described by current state-of-the-art simple or complex contagion modeling.

nodes.title: Topological Higher-Order Features for Brain Network State Classification
nodes.authors: Skhiri, Wafa*; De Vico Fallani, Fabrizio
nodes.abstract: Characterizing functional brain networks is crucial to model and analyze brain states in basic and clinical applications. Most current approaches rely on pairwise functional connectivity or linear spectral features, which capture only a fraction of the complexity of brain dynamics [1]. Neuroscience evidence increasingly suggests that neural computation involves higher- order interactions (HOI)-dependencies arising from the simultaneous coordination of multiple neural populations rather than isolated pairwise connections [2]. These HOI have been shown to reveal organizational features that remain hidden in classical graph-based analyses of brain networks and may therefore provide a richer substrate for brain state decoding. In this work, we investigate whether HOI-inspired network structure can improve the characterization of EEG activity during a typical brain-computer interface task based on motor imagery (MI). Instead of focusing on power modulation, we estimate instantaneous phase differences between EEG channels and treat these as a multivariate dynamical interaction pattern. Using these phase-based similarity networks, we construct a higher-order topological representation of neural interactions through spectral graph embedding followed by a topological data analysis (TDA) pipeline (Fig. 1a). We evaluate whether these topological features reliably distinguish MI tasks from resting states across several EEG recording sessions. Preliminary results indicate that simplicial-complex–based analysis applied to phase-interaction structure capture discriminative information that complement standard functional connectivity metrics (Fig. 1b,c). This suggests that topological characterizations—inspired by the notion of HOI as simultaneous multi-node coordination—may capture aspects of neural organization that are behaviorally relevant and potentially useful for many applications in cognitive and clinical neuroscience. [1] De Vico Fallani F, Richiardi J, Chavez M, Achard S. Graph analysis of functional brain networks. Philos Trans R Soc Lond B Biol Sci. 2014;369:20130521. [2] Santoro A, Battiston F, Lucas M, Petri G, Amico E. Higher-order connectomics of human brain function. Nat Commun. 2024;15:10244 .

nodes.title: Beyond Partisan Echo Chambers: Age and Gender Segregation in  Information Ecosystem
nodes.abstract: A central concern for democratic deliberation is whether citizens share a common informational foundation or instead sort into separate information environments. The echo chamber thesis argues that online platforms can intensify this sorting by segregating publics into ideologically homogeneous groups [1, 2]. Yet empirical investigations have focused primarily on partisan segregation [3, 4], overlooking other demographic dimensions that may also structure information consumption.  We analyze URL-sharing behavior among 1.6 million Twitter users matched to U.S. voter files via unique name-location combinations, providing validated demographic attributes (age, gender, party affiliation) rather than inferred characteristics. Our dataset comprises 43 million unique URLs shared between January 2017 and December 2022, enabling longitudinal analysis at both the domain level (information sources) and the URL level (specific stories). Using the multigroup Theil H index [5], a decomposable measure of segregation, we quantify segregation along partisan, age, and gender dimensions and establish statistical significance through permutation tests that randomly shuffle demographic labels while preserving the observed sharing structure.  Three findings emerge. First, URL-level segregation (H ≈ 0.32–0.40) substantially exceeds domain-level segregation (H ≈ 0.10–0.17), indicating that demographic groups rely on similar sources but select systematically different content within them, a pattern that domain-level analyses miss. Second, age-based segregation rivals or exceeds partisan segregation throughout the observation period, reaching H ≈ 0.39 by late 2022, consistent with emerging work on demographic homophily online [6]. Third, we identify a structural break in January 2021, after which age and gender segregation increase sharply while partisan segregation plateaus, suggesting a shift from a primarily partisan-organized information ecosystem to one segregated along multiple demographic dimensions.

nodes.title: Disruption and Adaptation of Social Connectedness at Third Places after Disasters
nodes.abstract: Disaster induced population dynamics trigger changes in socio-spatial patterns in affected communities leading to fragmentation and weakening of existing social ties, that have been considered an important factor for resilience in various studies. However, the dynamics and transformations of social networks resulting from disaster-induced displacement remain largely unexplored in the literature. To bridge this gap, using large scale mobile phone GPS data, we measure how the level of connectivity of social networks (measured through co-location in third places) changes, in communities affected by the 2021 Marshall Fires in Colorado. By comparing network metrics such as the clustering coefficient and mean degree between pre- and post-disaster social networks, we find that post-disaster social networks significantly lose connectivity. To investigate the mechanisms of why social networks lose connectively after disasters, we build several counterfactual network models.  First, we find that randomly removing individuals (nodes) with rates proportional to the actual displacement leads to underestimation in network connectivity. This suggests that some form of behavioral adaptation leads to preservation of connectivity in the social networks. Second, we find that using heterogeneous reduction rates in visitation patterns to points-of-interest (POIs) based on three factors: 1) distance from home to POIs, 2) distance between the fire and POIs, and 3) POI category, enables the replication of the post-disaster network characteristics. This suggests that POI visitation behavior significantly determines the dynamics of social networks after disasters.

nodes.title: Characterizing agglomeration economies by blockmodeling spatial proximity graphs
nodes.authors: Wu, Jianrui*; He, Baiyue; Kirkley, Alec
nodes.abstract: Agglomeration economies are a spatial phenomenon in which industries of the same type concentrate for the advantage it provides for sharing labor, resources, infrastructure, and knowledge. Urban amenities such as restaurants, shops, and services often exhibit pronounced spatial clustering to reap these benefits of agglomeration. A good deal of existing research in urban planning and geography has focused on agglomeration economies, with various measures developed to characterize the the extent to which industries cluster [1]. However, these existing measures either require the tuning of manual input parameters or expensive computational simulations for determining statistical significance of a given observed level of spatial clustering. This reduces their effectiveness for understanding how spatial agglomeration tendency varies across industries and spatial scales, both of which are critical for future city planning [2]. Furthermore, it is important for measures of spatial agglomeration to account for both homophily \emph{and} heterophily on a consistent scale.   Here we propose principled, computationally efficient, fully nonparametric measures of spatial homophily and heterophily using Bayesian stochastic blockmodeling. Given $N$ geo-located points (e.g. amenities, points of interest) with categorical labels, we construct a proximity graph $\bm{A}(\epsilon)$ connecting all point pairs within distance $\epsilon$ and characterize the level of homophily among these amenities using the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. Specifically, we compare three competing models for $\bm{A}(\epsilon)$ using their corresponding description lengths $-\log P(\bm{A}|\text{model})$: a \emph{neutral} Erd\H{o}s-R\'{e}nyi model; a \emph{homophilic} block model in which the edge probability $\rho_{\text{in}}$ among identical amenity types exceeds the cross-type probability $\rho_{\text{out}}$; and a \emph{heterophilic} block model with the opposite constraint. For fair comparison, the latter two models also encode the categorical labels $\bm{b}$ under a non-informative prior jointly with the graph. These three models can be compared scalably with careful parallelization and analytical marginalization over the blockmodel priors.  Applying this framework to an open source Foursquare amenity dataset spanning 779 cities worldwide, we discovered \emph{consistent spatial scales} of urban organization: homophilic clustering peaks at a walkable neighborhood scale, while heterophilic mixing peaks at district-level scales. These patterns are common across city sizes and geographic regions. Our results reveal a shared hierarchical urban organization in which similar amenities agglomerate locally, while diverse amenities mix at larger scales to form functionally integrated areas. The Bayesian framework provides a principled, parameter-free approach for detecting these patterns, and offers new tools for urban science and planning.

nodes.title: Characterizing the scholarly influence of three canonical community detection algorithms through citation networks
nodes.abstract: Girvan-Newman, label propagation, and Louvain are three canonical community detection algorithms. In order to understand their influence to the broader scientific community, we studied the 2-hop neighborhood of the citation networks of the three papers reporting those algorithms using a snapshot of the COCI, the OpenCitations Index of Crossref open DOI-to-DOI references. We discovered different temporal transitive closure behaviors in the 2-hop neighborhood. We defined three types of citations: 1st-gen citations (1-hop neighbors) are those cites a focal paper; 2nd-gen citations (2-hop neighbors) are those cites a paper that cites a focal paper; transitive closure (TC) citations are those that are both 1st-gen and 2nd-gen, meaning they cite the focal paper and cite (at least) one paper that cites the focal paper. We constructed two types of time series: P1 and P2. P1(x) is the proportion of the number of TC citations over the number of 1st-gen citations, both limited to those published in year x. P2(x) is the proportion of the number of TC citations over the number of 2nd-gen citations, both limited to those published in year x. While the P2(x) traces are similar, the P1(x) trace of Blondel et al., 2008, which reported the Louvain algorithm, is markedly different from the other two. Blondel et al., 2008 has a higher proportion of 1st-gen citations that do not cite any other citations of it. A possible explanation is that Louvain has stronger trans-disciplinary influences than Girvan-Newman and label propagation, which causes more papers citing Blondel et al., 2008 do not cite any other citations of it. Further investigation is underway.

nodes.title: Finding Patient Zero at Scale: How Training Data Size Impacts Detection Performance
nodes.abstract: Identifying the source of an epidemic (the "patient zero") is crucial for containing outbreaks. Several source detection methods use simulated outbreaks as training data.
