Theory and practice of phase transitions tell us [3] that at the onset of a systemic event, system behavior is characterized by a small number of “order parameters” describing system on the mesoscopic scale. In a case of continuous systemic events, which are analogous to phase transitions of the 2nd kind, order parameters evolve much slower than the individual system components and thus can serve as global parameters to be used for systemic risk management. Some trusted entity, e.g., government agency, tracks “slow” evolution of a “small number” of the order parameters and communicates the “essential” tracking data to the strategic system components. Then individual components combine these data with the available local information to evaluate and manage their individual systemic risks. Note that while distributed systemic risk mitigation in a network with strategic components can be modelled as a non-cooperative game of the strategic components, the proposed “signaling” transforms the corresponding competitive, i.e., Nash, equilibrium to the correlated equilibrium. Our conjecture is that this transformation allows for a significant reduction in the Price of Anarchy (PoA) which quantifies inefficiency of selfish risk mitigation vs. centralized socially optimal mitigation. We confirm this conjecture on an example of a Susceptible-Infected-Susceptible (SIS) contagion.

nodes.title: Coupling Social-Context and Contagion Dynamics: A Generic Framework
nodes.abstract: A growing body of evidence shows that contagion dynamics are shaped not only by contact structure or biological processes, but also by contagious social dynamics that alter behavior, risk perception, and social context. Classic examples include needle-exchange programs that reduced HIV transmission without directly targeting infection, and widespread mask adoption during the COVID-19 pandemic, which lowered effective transmission by modifying susceptibility and exposure rather than curing disease. Across such settings, social context acts as a latent regulatory layer that modulates contagion spread.  Motivated by these observations, we propose a generic modeling framework that couples social-context dynamics with contagion processes. The framework consists of two interacting layers defined over the same population: a social-context layer that captures the evolution of individual context through weak-tie interactions and threshold effects, and a contagion layer that models transmission along the contact network. Contagion probabilities are endogenously modulated by social context, creating feedback between internal social states and observable spreading dynamics. The framework generalizes classical contagion and threshold models and provides a unified structure for analyzing intervention-driven and socially regulated spreading phenomena across biological, behavioral, and informational domains.

nodes.title: Homophily without locality: Information topology shapes segregation in adaptive networks
nodes.abstract: Homophily is a pervasive organizing force in networks [1], yet it does not always take the same structural form. In some settings, homophily appears as proximity segregation, with dense local clustering and insulation between groups. Elsewhere, homophily appears as groups differentially occupying central, high-leverage roles within a shared network. Both patterns are widely observed, but it remains unclear why similar homophilous tendencies lead to such different outcomes. Existing network models typically explain homophily through local attachment rules, spatial constraints, or fixed preferences [2]. These approaches naturally generate clustering, but they provide limited insight into hierarchical or positional segregation without introducing additional asymmetries [3]. Coevolutionary network models show how competition for centrality can generate hierarchy, but they fail to explain how homophilous preferences interact with positional games [4]. As a result, the literature lacks a unifying explanation for how the same homophilous rewiring process can produce either local clustering or global hierarchy.  We introduce a coevolutionary network model in which agents rewire edges to improve the value of information encountered through network propagation. Following the adaptive rewiring framework of Holme and Ghoshal [4], nodes occupy an undirected graph and update edges through local, degree-preserving swaps. Homophily enters in two ways. First, information transmitted across unlike nodes is down-weighted by a friction parameter (η < 1), inducing a row-stochastic transition kernel that penalizes cross-group paths. Second, agents evaluate information exposure relative to their own group identity, valuing encounters with in-group nodes more than encounters with out-group nodes. Information propagation is modeled as a discounted random walk with factor α. An agent’s utility is the expected discounted value of nodes encountered along walks originating from that agent. Increasing α expands the effective information horizon, shifting rewiring incentives from local neighborhood structure toward global network position.  We show that the depth of the information horizon determines the structural resolution of homophilous constraints. When information is local (low α), agents minimize immediate friction through proximity segregation, forming dense, assortative clusters. As the horizon expands (high α), local clustering becomes insufficient to overcome the compounding transmission costs of η over long paths. In this global regime, agents break structural symmetry to form a high-capacity core, yielding positional segregation and hierarchical concentration. These results demonstrate that the form of segregation is not determined by homophily alone, but depends mechanistically on the topology of information that mediates competitive interactions. This provides a unifying explanation for how segregation can arise either as spatial separation or as control of network positions.  References  [1] McPherson, M., Smith-Lovin, L., and Cook, J. M. “Birds of a Feather: Homophily in Social Networks.” Annual Review of Sociology, 27, 415–444 (2001).  [2] Gross, T., and Blasius, B. “Adaptive coevolutionary networks: a review.” Journal of the Royal Society Interface, 5(20), 259–271 (2008).  [3] Holme, P., and Newman, M. E. J. “Nonequilibrium phase transition in the coevolution of networks and opinions.” Physical Review E, 74, 056108 (2006).  [4] Holme, P., and Ghoshal, G. “Dynamics of Networking Agents Competing for High Centrality and Low Degree.” Physical Review Letters, 96, 098701 (2006).

nodes.title: Two synchronization processes in the second-order Kuramoto model with inertia
nodes.abstract: Synchronization is a fundamental organizing principle across biological, neuronal, and engineered systems---from firefly flashing and cardiac pacemakers to power grid stability and Josephson junction arrays [1,2]. While the first-order Kuramoto model has provided deep insights into spontaneous synchronization, many physical systems involve inertia that fundamentally reshapes collective dynamics. This is particularly relevant for electric power grids, where the swing equation that governs generator dynamics is mathematically equivalent to the second-order Kuramoto model with inertia.  A critical yet underexplored question concerns how the shape of the natural frequency distribution $g(\omega)$ influences the synchronization pathways. In first-order systems, Gaussian distributions yield continuous transitions, while uniform distributions produce mixed-order transitions with abrupt jumps [3]. As modern power grids integrate increasingly diverse renewable sources, power injection profiles become more heterogeneous, approaching broad or uniform-like distributions. Understanding how such distributional changes affect synchronization and stability is, therefore, of both theoretical and practical importance.  We investigate this question in the second-order Kuramoto model, $m\ddot{\theta}_i + \gamma\dot{\theta}_i = \omega_i + (K/N)\sum_j \sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$, comparing Gaussian and uniform frequency distributions. Our analysis reveals two fundamentally distinct synchronization pathways. Gaussian distributions exhibit \textit{density-driven} dynamics: a dominant central cluster emerges early and gradually absorbs neighboring oscillators through quasi-static growth, producing a single abrupt transition in the order parameter. In contrast, uniform distributions follow \textit{velocity-driven} dynamics: multiple clusters form simultaneously across the frequency spectrum, with high-velocity clusters acting as organizing centers that partition the system into rotational domains.  This mechanistic difference manifests in Devil's Staircase patterns---hierarchical arrangements of synchronized clusters with rational frequency ratios. Although both systems develop such structures, their origins differ: density-driven absorption in Gaussian systems versus velocity-mediated partitioning in uniform systems. Crucially, the velocity-driven mechanism renders uniform systems more fragile: perturbations targeting high-velocity nodes trigger cascading desynchronization, whereas Gaussian systems remain robust under similar disturbances.  These findings carry direct implications for decentralized power grids, where frequency stability increasingly depends on high-power nodes with large angular velocities---precisely the nodes whose failure can precipitate system-wide collapse.

nodes.title: Network Data Profile: disease progression network in Korea
nodes.authors: Yoo, Ki Bong*; Lee, Kyung-Min ; Nam, Ji-Woong ; Liu, Yixuan; Jung, Ye-Won ; Oh, Jeong-Min
nodes.abstract: Disease networks are powerful tools for identifying disease characteristics and capturing progression trajectories. However, existing disease networks were developed based on network medicine, which can limit their applicability to real-world public health purposes. To address this gap, we developed a comprehensive, unbiased disease progression network and will introduce this dataset at the conference. We used national health insurance claims data of approximately 50 million total Korean population, collected from 2011 to 2022. We used only the primary ICD-10 diagnosis codes and reclassified into 655 disease categories including death. To minimize bias, disease associations were calculated within age and sex strata. Then, Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) analyses were performed with adjusting confounding and mediating diseases according to the DAG, income, region, BMI, alcohol consumption, smoking, exercise, and disability. Relative risk, attributable risk, and transition probabilities were estimated by IPW. We identified statistically significant disease pairs (RR > 1.5, q-value < 0.00001) and conducted two iterative rounds of IPW analysis to refine the network. The final disease progression network, established after the second round of IPW analysis, consists of 74,745 significant disease pairs. This includes an overall network as well as specific subnetworks stratified by age and sex. At the presentation, we will share how we developed the disease progression network, and node features and edge features. We believe this dataset will serve as a valuable resource for advancing research in network science and network medicine.

nodes.title: A Multiscale Metric of Effective Distance for Predicting Epidemic Arrival Times
nodes.authors: Moghaddas, Nima*; Ueda, Minami; Boudreau, Mariah; Chinazzi, Matteo; St-Onge, Guillaume
nodes.abstract: While historical epidemics were driven primarily by local transmission, modern outbreaks unfold through complex human mobility patterns spanning multiple scales, from daily commuting within metropolitan regions to intercontinental air travel. Previous work has defined effective distance metrics on the international aviation network using logarithmic functions of passenger flows along edges, initially based on shortest paths [1] and later extended to incorporate multiple paths and random walks [2]. However, existing implementations neglect the role of short-range mobility that shapes local transmission dynamics, especially in remote areas.  We build on this work by developing a multiscale effective distance metric that incorporates short-range commuting fluxes, allowing local mobility patterns to influence network flows alongside long-range air travel. We also replace the assumption of a uniform global mobility rate with heterogeneous node-specific mobility rates derived from combined long- and short-range flows to better capture differences in connectivity across populations. We validate our metric using results from the Global Epidemic and Mobility (GLEAM) metapopulation model, whose mobility layers combine flight data with short-range commuting patterns collected from the Offices of Statistics of countries across five continents [3]. Visualizations reveal a sharper wavefront when nodes are arranged radially according to our multiscale metric (Fig. 1d-f) compared to the random-walk based aviation-only effective distance (Fig. 1a-c), reducing the width of this wavefront (5th to 95th percentiles) by approximately 27%. Our approach shows improved correlation between effective distance and derived arrival times [3], increasing R2 from 0.918 to 0.992 (Fig. 1g-h). These results show that integrating multiple scales enhances the ability of effective distances to reflect realistic spreading patterns. This work can be used to reconstruct outbreak sources and spatial transmission routes from empirical data, and dramatically reduce the computational burden of these inferences compared to simulation-based approaches.

nodes.title: A Spectral Framework for Coupled Thermal and Reactive Transport in Pore Network Models
nodes.abstract: We develop a mathematical framework for \textbf{dynamics on pore-scale networks} that couple fluid flow, species transport, and heat transfer, motivated by fractured aquifer systems [1,3]. The network consists of edges representing pores, along which species and heat undergo advection, diffusion, reaction, and conduction, coupled through nonlinear pressure-driven flow and temperature-dependent wall reactions. These edge dynamics interact through \textbf{vertex-based continuity and Kirchhoff-type flux balance conditions}, yielding a fully coupled multi-physics dynamical system on the network.  To enable analytical insight into the resulting \textbf{spreading and interaction of transport processes on the network}, we linearize the governing equations about chemical equilibrium and show that the coupled thermal–species subsystem admits a \textbf{vector-valued generalized eigenvalue problem} arising from linear stability analysis. The associated eigenmodes define a natural spectral basis for representing interacting transport dynamics across the network. Projecting the linearized system onto this basis yields a reduced-order model for modal amplitudes, coupled through pressure, temperature, and concentration variables at network vertices.  We validate the spectral reduction against full PDE simulations on pore networks and analyze convergence and mode interactions as functions of key nondimensional parameters. The results demonstrate how complex, nonlinear multi-physics \textbf{dynamics on networks} can be systematically decomposed into interacting transport modes, providing a tractable framework for reduced-order modeling with applications to subsurface energy storage and reactive flow in geological formations.

nodes.title: Effective criminal network disruption via the friendship paradox
nodes.abstract: Organised criminal groups are increasingly understood to be flexible, decentralised networks, rather than rigid hierarchies [1]. Such groups tend to be resistant to law enforcement attempts to disrupt their activities, and can quickly adapt quickly to such interventions. Moreover, law enforcement strategies to disrupt and dismantle such networks can be extremely resource intensive and differentially effective. In particular, approaches typically require \emph{complete knowledge} of the criminal network structure in order for disruptions to be effective (Fig.~1 (a)-(b)), which is almost never the case for covert networks. Here we introduce \emph{Friendship Paradox Disruption} (FPD), which leverages the well-known friendship paradox [2-3] to disrupt criminal networks (Fig.~1 (c)-(d)). Importantly, rather than requiring complete network knowlege, FPD relies only on \emph{local knowledge} of network structure, for example provided by informants or partial surveillance by law enforcement authorities.  In this talk we discuss challenges of collecting data on and studying covert criminal networks, and introduce an approach based on exponential random graph models (ERGMs) [4] to generate synthetic criminal networks from limited data. We review typical disruption strategies and metrics for assessing their effectiveness, as well as presenting a novel disruption metric based on paths between nodes. We compare and contrast the effectiveness of FPD with other disruption approaches (Fig.~1 (e)) on synthetic and real networks [5], and discuss the implications of our findings for law enforcement.

nodes.title: A Diagnostic Framework for Sampling-Induced Distortion in Network Metrics
nodes.abstract: Empirical network data are almost always sampled, whether due to API limits, partial crawls, or missing data. The chosen sampling method and sampling fraction can significantly distort measured network statistics, so metrics computed on a sampled graph may misrepresent the underlying network. Prior work on graph sampling has mainly focused on designing and comparing algorithms that preserve individual statistics such as degree distributions, clustering, path lengths, or modular structure. Existing studies analyze how node-based, edge-based, and traversal-based samplers affect these properties, but they do not provide a single, interpretable distortion score that is comparable across metrics, sampling schemes, and network types.  In this work I propose a simple diagnostic, the normalized distortion (ND), that quantifies how unreliable a given network metric becomes under a chosen sampling scheme and sampling fraction. Given a full “ground-truth” network with a true metric value (for example, the global clustering coefficient or assortativity), I repeatedly sample subgraphs using a fixed sampler and fraction and compute the same metric on each sampled subgraph. From these values I compute the sample mean and sample standard deviation, and then rescale both the bias (the difference between the mean and the true value) and the variability (the standard deviation) by a characteristic scale based on the magnitude of the true value plus a small constant to avoid division by zero. The normalized distortion is defined as the Euclidean combination (square root of the sum of squares) of this relative bias and relative variability, yielding a single dimensionless number that captures how far, on average, the sampled metric deviates from the truth and how unstable it is across samples, all on a comparable relative scale.  I demonstrate this framework on four canonical random graph models: Erdős–Rényi (ER), Watts–Strogatz small-world (WS), Barabási–Albert scale-free (BA), and a stochastic block model (SBM). For each model, I consider four samplers (random node, snowball, degree-weighted node, and uniform edges with induction) and sampling fractions between five and thirty percent of the nodes, focusing on two metrics: the global clustering coefficient and the average shortest-path length. The results show that normalized distortion for clustering is often large at low sampling fractions in BA and ER graphs, especially under degree-weighted and uniform-edge sampling, while random node sampling is consistently less distorting; in WS networks, clustering is comparatively robust, with normalized distortion remaining below about 0.4 even at five percent sampling. Average shortest-path length is generally more robust (normalized distortion mostly below about 0.7), but its behavior still depends on sampler and topology: snowball sampling preserves path length particularly well in ER, SBM, and WS, whereas random node sampling can substantially distort distances in BA and WS, and degree-weighted or uniform-edge sampling show marked distortion at intermediate fractions.  Overall, the normalized distortion framework turns sampling from a hidden nuisance into a measurable design choice, quantifying how much bias and variability different samplers introduce for each metric and providing a practical, topology-aware calibration tool for choosing metrics, sampling strategies, and sample sizes in empirical network studies.

nodes.title: Growing a Sympoietic Art Organism: An Experimental Framework for Tracking the Micro-Dynamics of Cultural Evolution
nodes.authors: Dazeley-Gaist, Phileas*; Camacho Cabrera, Gabriel; Thompson, Kyle; Arango Franco, Sara; Bulla Casas, Lina; Abd El Razek, Nadine
nodes.abstract: Empirical data recording how cultural artefacts, motifs, and lineages form in real time are of great benefit and interest in the study of cultural evolution. In this work, we introduce an experimental design (nicknamed “Sympoietic Art Organism” in a nod to Haraway) that attempts to richly capture the bottom-up emergence of a shared cultural heritage in a small, controlled setting, as well as the influences at play throughout its evolution. Our approach calls for a small community of artists to engage in active collaborative artmaking over several months to build up a shared creative commons. After an initial “seeding phase” round of art contributions to the commons data repository, participants are asked to contribute at least five new artefacts to the commons every two weeks, over a series of “co-creative cycles”, and with the requirements that each shared piece engage with one or more pre-existing artworks (that is, artworks from earlier cycles) and that we record these influences between works to a shared ledger.   We, the ten co-authors of this study, took part in our own experiment over three months, growing a dataset of 411 original artworks, and a network of 725 verified relationships between them. Our experiment yielded a complete history of the creation of cultural artefacts (artworks) by each participant, and of all acknowledged influences from other pieces, allowing us to reconstruct the citation network of contributed artworks. By enforcing synchronous updates within each co-creative cycle such that participants only engaged with artworks from earlier stages, we were able to model the network’s growth as a time series.  Our results are well suited for multi-modal analysis. Quantitatively, we observed the structural evolution of the citation network from disconnected nodes into a consolidated core as the system matured. We found the network’s structural dynamics were consistent rich-get-richer feedback, showing that preferential attachment emerged endogenously around specific hub artworks. Qualitatively, the artworks provide a basis for tracing a kind of semantic phylogeny through visual inspection and comparison of contributed artworks, to see how participants adapted, remixed, or subverted specific aesthetic motifs across generations.   We propose this experiment as a proof of concept for a scalable ethnographic tool in network science. This approach provides a transparent environment for investigating the mechanisms of cultural inheritance.  This work was initiated at the 2025 Complexity Global School, organised by the Santa Fe Institute and TREES group at the Universidad de Los Andes.

nodes.title: Human vs. Generative AI: A Comparison of Creative Strategies
nodes.authors: Yu, Yulin*; Shen ,  Anthony; Wang, David; Uzzi, Brian
nodes.abstract: The rapid advancement of generative AI has renewed optimism that creativity—central to innovation across domains from science to art—can be substantially expanded through human–machine collaboration. While AI systems can recombine vast bodies of existing knowledge in novel ways, prior work suggests that human creativity retains distinctive advantages, particularly in flexibility and contextual adaptation. To enable effective coordination between human and machine creativity, we ask a fundamental question: \textbf{How do machines (Gen-AI) achieve creativity compared to humans, and what processes underlie these differences?}  We investigate this question using the Divergent Association Task (DAT) [1], a widely used and replicable measure of creative ideation. Analyzing over 5{,}000 human and machine DAT responses worldwide, we apply text and network analyses to compare creative strategies at both individual and collective levels. First, following theories of cognitive simplicity, we examine step-by-step vocabulary selection, operationalizing word simplicity using five metrics drawn from prior literature: (1) age of acquisition, (2) word frequency, (3) word length, (4) spoken-language tendency, and (5) written-language tendency. We track the first through seventh words produced in each response. Second, we examine categorical jumps between successive words, as larger semantic shifts are thought to signal greater creative potential. To quantify word categories and distances, we use WordNet [2], a tree-structured lexical network (Figure~1A), and compute per-response category counts as well as average, variance, and maximum jump distances. Finally, we examine collective behavior by comparing the total number of categories covered by humans versus machines.  Our findings reveal three key insights. First, at the individual level, humans tend to use simpler and more common words than machines, yet collectively generate far greater lexical diversity. Humans use higher-frequency and lower-complexity words (1.62 vs.\ 1.98), but produce substantially more unique words overall (4048 vs.\ 749). Both humans and machines progress from simpler to more complex words over time; however, machines exhibit a steeper increase in complexity in later stages (Figure~1A). Second, at the categorical level (Figure~1B), humans use fewer broad categories per response but explore more fine-grained categories, and collectively cover a much wider semantic space than machines. Finally, while human word-to-word semantic distances are smaller on average (11.19 vs.\ 12.59), they show higher variance (6.22 vs.\ 8.97), consistent with Lévy-flight–like search patterns. Together, these results suggest that the advantage of human creativity lies less in individual output and more in collective variation, highlighting a key complementarity between human and machine creative processes.

nodes.title: Genotype networks drive oscillating endemicity and epidemic trajectories in viral evolution
nodes.authors: Lamata Otín, Santiago*; Rotita Ion, Octavian C. ; Arenas, Alex; Soriano Paños, David; Gómez Gardeñes, Jesús
nodes.abstract: Rapidly evolving viruses use antigenic drift as a key mechanism to evade host immunity and persist in real populations. While traditional models of antigenic drift and epidemic spread rely on low-dimensional antigenic spaces, genomic surveillance data reveal that viral evolution produces complex antigenic genotype networks with hierarchical modular structures. In this study, we present an eco-evolutionary framework in which viral evolution and population immunity dynamics are shaped by the structure of antigenic genotype networks. Using synthetic networks, we demonstrate that network topology alone can drive transitions between stable endemic states and recurrent seasonal epidemics. Furthermore, our results show how the integration of the genotype network of the H3N2 influenza in our model allows for estimating the emergence times of various haplotypes resulting from its evolution. Our findings underscore the critical role of the topology of genotype networks in shaping epidemic behavior and, besides, provide a robust framework for integrating real-world genomic data into predictive epidemic models.

nodes.title: Investment Flows in Venture Capital
nodes.abstract: The distribution of private investment across sectors has attracted sustained scholarly attention, in part because sectoral allocation is a primary determinant of aggregate financial returns and long-run growth. Yet, despite extensive empirical work, there is still no consensus on how such distributions emerge, nor on how profitable investment windows shift over time from one industry to another. A key reason is that industries are typically treated as discrete, well-defined silos, implicitly assuming stable boundaries and limited cross-sectoral spillovers. This representation obscures the underlying technological, functional, and informational continuities that shape capital reallocation and innovation dynamics.  To address this limitation, we adopt a bottom-up approach to sector formation. Rather than imposing ex-ante industry classifications, we construct sectors endogenously by clustering firms along three complementary dimensions: similarity in tasks and production activities, following recent advances in task-based representations of firms (Acemoglu, 2024); similarity in products and capabilities, grounded in the economic complexity framework (Tacchella et al., 2012, 2018); and textual similarity in firm descriptions, capturing latent technological and strategic proximity (Gordon & Phillips, 2009). Integrating these dimensions, we embed firms in a high-dimensional network space in which distance reflects economic and technological relatedness rather than administrative classification.  Within this network, private investment flows can be interpreted as a diffusion process operating over an irregular, evolving structure. Using three decades of U.S. private investment data, we show that the spatial propagation of investment is not well described by smooth reallocation or sector-by-sector rotation. Instead, it exhibits scaling properties and clustering patterns consistent with fractal geometry, closely resembling growth dynamics in disordered media. In particular, investment expansion follows dynamics analogous to correlated percolation, whereby capital propagates through locally dense clusters of related firms and technologies, occasionally triggering rapid phase transitions when previously disconnected regions of the network become accessible.  This perspective reframes sectoral investment dynamics as an emergent phenomenon arising from micro-level firm relatedness, rather than as a sequence of independent sectoral cycles. Profitable investment windows do not simply “move” across predefined industries; they percolate through a continuously reconfiguring economic space shaped by task overlap, product complementarities, and shared narratives. As a result, sectoral boundaries appear porous, endogenous, and time-varying, offering a unifying framework to explain both the persistence of investment clusters and the sudden reorientation of capital toward new technological domains.

nodes.title: Information exchange processes leads to ultra-small world networks
nodes.abstract: A substantial body of evidence indicates that real-world networks exhibit the small-world property, meaning that the maximal distance between any two nodes grows logarithmically rather than linearly with network size. In 2023, we proposed a game-theoretic approach based on a weighted analogue of betweenness centrality as a utility function, improving upon existing models at that time - most notably the Watts–Strogatz model. Our framework provided an explanation for why, in real social networks, this principle can be stated even more strongly: most social networks are organized such that no individual is more than six connections away from any other, an empirical regularity known as the six degrees of separation.  The proposed game-theoretic approach showed that the “six degrees of separation” property naturally arises as an equilibrium outcome in networks where individuals balance their aspiration to improve their centrality,captured by the standard notion of betweenness centrality, against the costs of forming and maintaining connections. However, two important aspects remained unaddressed: first, the motivation for forming directed connections beyond pure intermediation roles (which are clearly relevant in real-world settings), and second, the absence of an explicit payoff formulation.  In the present work, we adopt a different perspective. Each agent is now modeled as both a sender and a receiver of information. During information transmission, agents prefer to use shortest paths, as information becomes progressively distorted with each transmission step, akin to the “telephone game.” A node derives utility when a sufficiently large amount of high-quality, minimally distorted information passes through it—both as a destination and as an intermediary.  The proposed framework not only reproduces the tendency toward ultra-small-world structures (with only technical modifications relative to our original model), but also naturally accommodates the utility of directed links and allows for agent heterogeneity. Moreover, it provides a promising foundation for analyzing the emerging agentic web: a paradigm of the Internet that, according to current forecasts, may replace the classical web architecture by 2030th.

nodes.title: The fragile nature of rewarded cooperation in social networks: a mixed game model.
nodes.abstract: Issues around sustainable development frequently spills down to the tension between self-interest and altruism or in other terms the willingness to take cooperative action at the cost of individual payoffs. This paper investigates some aspects of sustainable cooperation through an extended Prisoner’s Dilemma (PD) game, which is extended along four dimensions.  First, the model distinguishes between individualist and conformist attitudes [1]. Indivdualist players follow the standard PD game, while conformists play a coordination game and align their strategy with that of the majority of their social connections. Unlike existing models, this mixed game setup assigns different payoff functions to conformists. Second, adjusted replicator dynamics [2] is applied, where attitudes evolve based on their relative payoffs, recognizing that social attitudes change more slowly than strategy choices. Third, equilibrium outcomes are analyzed within complex social network structures [3]. Fourth, the effect of monetary incentives for cooperation is examined in interaction with attitude dynamics and network structure [4].  Equilibrium results show that cooperation only persists if both cooperation and conformism are in the majority at the game outset, otherwise individualism and defection prevail. If this condition holds, then a stable yet heterogeneous equilibrium can emerge where conformists cooperate and individualists defect.   Rewarding promotes a shift toward cooperation, but its effectiveness depends on the composition of the population. If all players are conformists, the reward will be ineffective as the decision rule of conformists only indirectly depend on the reward. The reward encourages individualism, potentially altering the direction of attitude dynamics. While the reward is active, individualism spreads, even if individualists cooperate. In a complete network, this often leads to complete cooperation, but once the reward is withdrawn, the system collapses into full defection. These patterns persist in incomplete networks. The reward boosts cooperation but also promotes individualism, causing cooperation to drop below pre-reward levels once. Network structure plays a crucial role in shaping these dynamics. Sparse social networks help sustain cooperation, but degree asymmetry has an even stronger impact on equilibrium outcomes. Equilibrium cooperation is more likely to persist and the effect of the reward is greater in scale free networks compared to symmetric (Erdős-Rényi type) random or complete networks. Moreover, the dispersion across independent simulation runs is smaller and outcomes are typically closer to full cooperation, reflecting the role of highly connected nodes that bridge local neighborhoods. Scale free network structures also lead to sharper declines in cooperation once the reward is withdrawn. These results are driven by the stronger influence of degree asymmetry on the spread of individualism.  [1] Pena, J., Volken, H., Pestelacci, E., Tomassini, M. (2009): Conformity hinders the evolution of cooperation on scale-free networks. Physical Review E 80. DOI:10.1103/PhysRevE.80.016110.  [2] Nowak, M.A., May, R.M. (1992): Evolutionary games and spatial chaos. Nature 359, 826–829. DOI:10.1038/359826a0. [3] Szabó, Gy., Fáth, G. (2007): Evolutionary games on graphs. Physics Report 446, 97–216. DOI:10.1016/j.physrep.2007.04.004.  [4] Jimenez, R., Lugo, H., Cuesta, J.A., Sanchez, A. (2008): Emergence and resilience of cooperation in the spatial prisoner’s dilemma via a reward mechanism. Journal of Theoretical Biology 250, 475–483. DOI:10.1016/j.jtbi.2007.10.010.

nodes.title: Adaptive Cannistraci Hebb Network Automata Modelling of Complex Networks for Path based Link Prediction
nodes.authors: Zhao, Jialin; Muscoloni, Alessandro; Michieli, Umberto; Zhang, Yingtao; Cannistraci, Carlo*
nodes.abstract: Many complex networks have partially observed or evolving connectivity, making link prediction a fundamental task. Topological link prediction infers missing links using only network topology, with applications in social, biological, and technological systems. T he Cannistraci Hebb (CH) theory provides a topological formulation of Hebbian learning, grounded on two pillars: (1) the minimization of external links within local communities, and (2) the path based definition of local communities that capture homophilic (similarity driven) interactions via paths of length 2 and synergetic (diversity driven) interactions via paths of length 3. Building on this, we introduce the Cannistraci Hebb Adaptive (CHA) network automata, an adaptive learning machine that automatically selects the optimal CH rule and path length to model each network. CHA unifies theoretical interpretability and data driven adaptivity, bridging physics inspired network science and machine intelligence. Across 1,269 networks from 14 domains, CHA consistently surpasses state of the art methods including Structural Perturbation Method (SPM) SPM), Stochastic Block Models SBM )), graph embedding methods, and message passing graph neural networks while revealing the mechanistic principles governing link formation. Our code is available at https://github.com/biomedical cybernetics/Cannistraci_Hebb_network_automata

nodes.title: A graph generation algorithm for network meta-analysis
nodes.abstract: Network meta-analysis (NMA) is a statistical method to synthesize evidence from multiple clinical trials that compare treatment options for the same condition. The method derives its name from a graphical representation of the data where nodes are treatments, and edges represent comparisons between treatments in trials [1]. Because of the higher-order structures introduced by trials that compare more than two treatments, these networks are more accurately described by bipartite graphs [2]. Here, trials define a second type of node, and edges connect trial nodes to the treatment nodes they compare.   NMA is widely used in healthcare decision-making and is an active area of methods development. Model validation, methods comparisons, and investigation of model behaviour require comprehensive simulation studies. However, simulating NMA datasets typically involves manually specifying the network structure, which becomes increasingly difficult for large datasets. Simulated datasets have therefore been limited to small networks (<= 10 treatments) with a limited range of geometries, and trials that involve only two treatments. As a result, simulation studies of NMA often fail to represent the full range of network structures encountered in practice. To overcome these challenges, we develop an algorithm to automatically generate NMA networks with realistic properties.  To inform our algorithm, we investigate the typical structural properties of an ensemble of 300 NMA networks drawn from analyses published in 2023. We begin with a bipartite version of the configuration model [3] which samples the degrees of the treatment nodes (the number of trials involving that treatment) and degrees of the trial nodes (the number of treatments compared in that trial) from the observed degree distributions of the ensemble. As shown in Fig. 1, this algorithm generates ensembles of random graphs whose degree distributions closely resemble those of the real world NMAs, both in their bipartite and unipartite representations. To more accurately reproduce the high proportion of star networks observed in the real world ensemble, we adapt the algorithm by sampling the degrees from a mixture model informed by the different distributions associated with star and non-star networks.   To the best of our knowledge, this is the first algorithm designed to generate realistic NMA networks. This will facilitate efficient and flexible simulation studies of NMA methods. It also provides a starting point for generating more complex datasets that are becoming increasingly common in this area.

nodes.title: How Overlapping Input Structures Amplify Systemic Risk in Global Supply Chains?
nodes.authors: Gyimesi, András*; Braun, Erik; Longauer, Dóra; Sebestyén, Tamás
nodes.abstract: Disrupting global supply chains (GSCs), recent global events drew attention to their fragility and the systemic risks they pose to the global economy. This paper examines the underlying structural characteristics of GSC networks that exacerbate their vulnerability. Using a general equilibrium model with production networks, similar to [1, 2], we develop an indicator called Structural Vulnerability (SV) based on input-side structural factors, such as supplier concentration and overlapping input linkages. The example in Figure 1 shows that if the input sources of the S1 sector become more concentrated towards itself (Model C) or become correlated with another sector (S3 in Model D), then SV is amplified. We also measure each sector's contribution to vulnerability (denoted by SVC), which shows that S1 has a larger SVC in Model D than in Model B, despite having an identical input structure.  Empirical analysis shows that increased economic specialization and interconnectedness have led to a significant rise in systemic risks between 1995 and 2019, especially up until 2012. This is evident in Figure 2, where the SVC of almost all countries and sectors increased between the two dates. We identify that the manufacturing sectors of developing countries were the main contributors to the growing vulnerability. The input structures of developing countries and manufacturing sectors changed in a way that their SVC values got inflated, as shown in Figure 2. The results suggest that the reason behind this increased vulnerability is the increasing homogeneity across sectors’ input sourcing structures, rather than by more concentration within individual sectors. This finding calls for coordination of supply chain and risk management decisions, as micro-level decisions can build up systemic risk by homogenizing input sourcing structures across GSC networks.

nodes.title: A network of cycles from random minimum cycle bases for material networks
nodes.abstract: Many material networks are well-described by their cycles. Graphene, carbon nanotubes, and diamond are periodic tilings of hexagonal carbon rings in the plane, on a cylinder, and in 3D space, respectively. The formalism of this is complex. One application we are particularly intereseted in is predicting molecular structures and giant (macroscale) molecules formed in hydrocarbon pyrolysis under extreme temperatures and pressures. We model the ensemble of carbon skeletons as a random graph [1,2]. Our most recent “disjoint loop model” [1] yielded excellent predictions for giant molecules with H/C ratios ≥ 2.5, as in butane (C4H10). In carbon-rich systems the accuracy of our disjoint loop model’s prediction of the size of the largest molecule deteriorates as carbon rings form clusters, thereby violating the “disjoint loop” assumption. This led us to building theory and designing algorithms for extracting cycles to characterize large, complex graphs, as in Fig. 1(a) [6]. We propose a method to sample the minimum cycle bases of a graph uniformly at random. From this, we define a dual graph, a network whose nodes represent cycles in the random minimum cycle basis, and whose edges represent their intersection paths, Fig. 1(d).  The set of simple cycles cannot be used because it grows exponentially with graph size. Instead, the conventional approach is to treat the set of cycles as a vector space, where addition is the symmetric difference, and scalars are 0 and 1. Then, cycles can be sampled from a minimum cycle basis (MCB), a basis of the cycle space that minimizes the sum of lengths. Unfortunately, an MCB of a graph is not unique. Because of this, several authors extend MCBs to a unique set of cycles, e.g., Vismara [3] defines the relevant cycles as the union of all the MCBs of a graph. The size of the relevant cycles, however, may still grow exponentially with graph size.  Because of the size limitations of the relevant cycles – and other extensions of MCBs – Gleiss et al. [4] and Kolodzik et al. [5] construct equivalence relations for the relevant cycles. These partition the relevant cycles into a smaller number of equivalence classes. We modify this approach and define polyhedron-interchangeability (pi) and short loop-interchangeability (sli) classes. pi and sli classes identify 3D structures and nested rings, respectively. See Fig. 1(b). We sample the MCBs of a graph uniformly at random from the pi and sli classes using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo approach. We post-process this MCB so that pairs of cycles intersect over a single path as in Fig. 1(c). The final MCB permits a notion of dual graph as shown in Fig. 1(d).  We sampled MCBs and visualized loop clusters from a large collection of molecular dynamics simulations of hydrocarbon pyrolysis of large systems. We observed a phase transition in the loop cluster size distribution and a counterintuitive statistics of the largest molecule size.

nodes.title: Phase Transitions in Heider Balance with Heterogeneous Social Temperatures
nodes.abstract:
Heider’s balance theory provides a fundamental framework for understanding polarization and alliance formation in social networks through triadic interactions [1]. Most existing stochastic formulations assume a homogeneous social temperature, implicitly treating all interpersonal relations as equally volatile [2]. Empirical social systems, however, are highly heterogeneous: social ties differ widely in stability, susceptibility to change, and exposure to noise [3].  We introduce a generalized Heider balance model in which each link is assigned its own social temperature to capture heterogeneity of fluctuations. By mapping the dynamics onto a higher-order Ising-type system with heterogeneous effective couplings, we derive a distribution-dependent mean-field theory that governs the average opinion $x$:  egin{equation}     x=\int_0^{+\infty}	anh\left( eta x^2ight)ho( eta)d eta, \end{equation} where $ eta$ is the heterogeneous inverse temperature and $ho$ is the distribution for $ eta$. The self consistence equation shows that collective behavior depends not only on the average inverse temperature but on the entire distribution of heterogeneity.  From the self consistence equation, we establish universal bounds controlled by the third order momentum $\gamma^3$ for the critical transition between polarized and non-polarized states, $C_* \leq \mu_{m C} <  rac{5}{4}\left( rac{4}{3}\gamma^3ight)^{ rac{1}{5}}$, and prove that the homogeneous limit provides a universal lower bound $C_* \approx 1.716$ for the critical average inverse temperature $\mu_{m C}$. Crucially, we theoretically show that light-tailed and heavy-tailed distributions lead to qualitatively distinct phase diagrams as Fig. 1 shows: while increasing heterogeneity such as standard deviation $\sigma$ destabilizes polarization in light-tailed cases, heavy-tailed distributions can sustain polarized states even under arbitrarily large fluctuations.     igskip {\small 
oindent[1] F. Heider, J. Psychol. 	extbf{21}, 107 (1946).\ 
oindent[2] K. Malarz and J. A. Holyst, Phys. Rev. E 	extbf{106}, 064139 (2022).\ 
oindent[3] M. Bagherikalhor, A. Kargaran, A. Shirazi, and G. Jafari, Phys. Rev. E 	extbf{103}, 032305 (2021). }

nodes.title: Emergence of Covid-19 Variants via Dominance in a Multi-Variant Epidemic Model
nodes.authors: Ivry, Aviel; Cohen, Reuven; Patron, Amikam*
nodes.abstract: We investigate the emergence and spreading of dominant Covid-19 variants – Alpha, Beta, Delta and Omicron – throughout the Covid-19 pandemic, based on a network multi-mutation epidemic spreading model that we developed. We characterize properties of a pathogen’s mutation that lead it to reach dominance over other mutations that spread simultaneously with it. Based on this model we explain the following pandemic related facts: (i) Three pandemic waves refer to three variants that reached dominance over other coexisting variants during three periods : Alpha wave in 2020, Delta wave in 2021, and the Omicron wave in 2022 - mid-2023. We explain the successive replacements of Alpha by Delta and Delta by Omicron. (ii) The importance and severity ascribed to the Beta variant. In our epidemic spreading model, a pathogen can mutate continuously during the epidemic spreading. We consider pathogens whose mutating parameter is the mortality mean-time and study the evolution of this parameter over the spreading process.  Based on this, we compute the probability of an individual (node) carrying a pathogen variant to infect other individuals (nodes), depending on the mutating parameter of the specific variant. We show that less aggressive mutations with a higher mortality meantime have a higher probability of infecting their neighbors and a larger proportion relative to other mutations. Using Epidemiological metrics of the Covid-19 variants, we calculated the parameters of our model for these variants. Based on this, we deduce that the replacements of Alpha by Delta and Delta by Omicron throughout the pandemic, were not due to the emergence of a variant with a much higher infection probability, but rather to the emergence of a variant with a shorter infection mean time and a low mortality probability. We also show that the sufficiently high infection probability of the Beta variant combined with it relatively high mortality probability, makes it a significant variant.

nodes.title: Urban Multilayer Network Framework from the Space-Society Perspective: Modeling, Characterizing and Understanding on the Dynamics of Urban Space
nodes.abstract: Applying network-based methods to urban issues is increasingly important, yet most studies still separate visible physical networks like road networks from invisible social networks. Research that treats the built environment as a container of human activities and uses complex network analysis methods to reveal characteristics and laws of urban spatial structures remains limited.     We propose a modeling strategy that integrates urban physical space with social associations patterns by representing resident activity-related buildings as nodes and social communication as edges (ties). Spatial functional differences among buildings are further used to construct attribute-specific layers, forming a spatial multilayer network of urban area.     Preliminarily and empirically, we have finished the analysis on the central urban area of metropolitan Shanghai. We built a single-layer residential space network for 6731 gated communities, using community centroids as nodes and opportunities of inter-community social interaction in public space as edges (ties). On the basis of network measurement, like robustness and degree distribution, the identified “network community” has been proved to be in line with the geographical sense of “regional identity”. Furthermore, the multi-layer network of urban space is an extension of the single attribute network. Because the urban residents’ activities—such as working, trading (including consuming) and leisure—occur in specific buildings, architectural complexes or blocks, the spatial scope of different activities can be defined accurately. Meanwhile, residents move among these functional urban spaces with varying modes, frequencies, and purposes. These spatial and behavioral definitions with the connotation of planning theory are very suitable for all the basic conditions of constructing multi-layer network of space. And, residents’ movements define intra-layer ties and cross-layer couplings, satisfying the theoretical conditions for constructing a multilayer spatial network analogous to a diverse ecological system.     This spatial multilayer network offers a promising analytical paradigm for capturing urban complexity, encompassing major physical spaces and generalized social linkages. With richer functional details and behavioral data, this integrated spatial–social network approach may yield deeper insights and support predictions of urban evolution.     In sum, applying complex network analysis to the spatial–social structure of cities provides new methods and perspectives for urban research and contributes to theoretical development in network science.  Keywords: Multilayer Network of Urban Space; Physical Space; Social Interaction; Dynamic Mechanism; Spatial Pattern Research Paradigm

nodes.title: Reconstructing Historical Social Networks as Complex Systems: Analyzing Diaries from the Reform War (1858-1860) in Mexico
nodes.authors: Motilla, Jose Antonio*; Espitia , Diego ; Ugalde , Edgardo; Galán-Vásquez, Edgardo
nodes.abstract: Social systems are inherently complex, shaped by dynamic interactions, power structures, and historical contingencies. Understanding how these networks evolve over time is crucial for reconstructing past societies and identifying underlying structural patterns. This study applies network science, text mining, and Natural Language Processing (NLP) [1] to reconstruct historical social networks from personal diaries written during the Reform War (1858-1860) in Mexico, one of the most violent conflicts in mexican history. In previous research [2, 3], we reconstructed networks from Juan de Vildosola’s Diary and developed growth models that capture the social network as seen from the observer’s perspective, as a subgraph of a larger system exhibiting a Barabási-Albert structure, thus revealing key actors and ideological affiliations. Expanding this approach, we analyze four additional diaries from the same period, written by individuals with diverse political perspectives, as well as texts from traditional, non-academic historical sources. Using NLP and named entity recognition (NER), we extract and normalize historical figures, using them as building blocks to construct interaction networks. We hypothesize that these reconstructed networks allow us to examine structural similarities and resilience in wartime societies. This comparative analysis provides new insights into how official historical narratives may oversimplify the complexity of past societies during civil wars. Our findings contribute to modeling social systems as evolving networks, offering an interdisciplinary perspective that addresses key challenges in historical data analysis, such as fragmentation, ambiguity, and bias in primary sources. Furthermore, we demonstrate how computational approaches and exceptional historical documents can serve as valuable methodologies for complex systems research, particularly in the study of civil conflicts and long-term social dynamics.  [1] Motilla-Ch ́avez, J.A., Espitia, D., Gal ́an-V ́asquez, E., et al. A Methodological Framework for the Use of AI Tools in Automated Workflows for Generating Validated and Structured Historical Datasets, 17 November 2025, PREPRINT (Version 1) available at Research Square [https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-7943432/v1] [2] Motilla-Ch ́avez, J. A., Ugalde, E. & Gal ́an-V ́asquez, E. Social networks from dailies: the observer’s point of view. Appl Netw Sci 9, 67 (2024). https://doi.org/10.1007/s41109-024-00681-9. [3] Motilla-Chavez J, Ugalde E, Gal ́an-V ́asquez E. An ́alisis de un diario de la Guerra de Reforma (1858-1860) en San Luis Potos ́ı, M ́exico, por medio de la teor ́ıa de redes: una propuesta metodolo ́gica para el an ́alisis de textos histo ́ricos. In: Violencia, repre- sentaciones y estrategias. La guerra y sus efectos en M ́exico, Colombia y Guatemala, siglos XVI-XX. COLMEX (2021)

nodes.title: Multiplicity without Switching in Evolving Correlation Networks: A Coupling–Localization Atlas of Market Organization (US vs EU)
nodes.abstract: Complex networks are often summarized by a single number, implicitly treating organization as one-dimensional. We report “multiplicity without switching” in financial correlation networks: at comparable overall coupling, the market’s dominant mode supports multiple, distinct organizations, while stress mainly shifts where observations sit in coupling space rather than causing frequent within-band state flips.  Using daily sector returns (2006–present) and rolling correlation matrices (90-day windows), we build a coupling–localization atlas and test for bimodality within a fixed-coverage, rank-matched coupling band. Both U.S. and European sector networks show strong bimodality, confirmed by a nonparametric multimodality test with overlap-aware block bootstrap. A declustered high-coupling stress gate reveals occupancy migration in event windows: coupling rises, localization falls, and inflow into the highest-coupling bin increases sharply, while within-band switching remains rare. Results persist under coarser sampling strides and under shrinkage-based correlation estimation.

nodes.title: Evaluating the Ability of General-purpose AI Agents to Solve Network Science Problems
nodes.authors: Li, Xuanchi*; Kojaku, Sadamori; Yang, Kaicheng
nodes.abstract: Please see the attached pdf file.

nodes.title: Mixture models uncover polycentric urban structure from mobility networks
nodes.abstract: The modern city is often characterized by a tension between centralizing forces and decentralizing forces. Human mobility patterns offer a powerful lens to understand a city’s dynamic structure, often revealing complexities hidden in static maps of population or land use. Tracking where and when people travel – their daily moves across the city – yields a map of functional urban connectivity, effectively the "revealed preference" of residents using and experiencing the city. Here we exploit GPS mobility data for urban residents in cities across Germany, France and the United Kingdom to develop a method for understanding how cities balance the forces of centralization and decentralization. In doing so, we find regularities in how people distribute activities in space with implications social mixing, productivity, and sustainability. We analyze GPS traces for 50 million residents across 226 functional urban areas in France, Germany, and the United Kingdom. We use home- and stop-detection to define each trip of length d, the distance between home and stop. Each pooled home hexagon (H3 level 9) is assigned a vector of trip distances. Because these length distributions are comprised of trips generated from different processes, which include leisure activities and commutes, we employ finite mixture models to separate shorter-range routine mobility from longer-range travel within a single, interpretable framework.  For each home hexagon we model the empirical distribution of trip lengths with a Gamma mixture representing proximal, medial, and distal mobility scales. Mixture parameters are obtained by maximum-likelihood expectation-maximization and the components are ordered by increasing mean distance. The resulting means represent the typical trip distance within each component, or scale, while the weights quantify the share of all trips undertaken in that scale for the neighborhood in question. To understand how these values vary with respect to urban spatial structure, we estimate an urban nucleus for each city by taking the centroid of the busiest location by GPS pings and computing each neighborhood's distance to that centroid.   Fitting mixture models to ~20,000 neighborhoods across cities in Europe, we see a number of important regularities. The distal mean grows with distance to the nucleus: peripheral households pay a systematic distance premium. The proximal distances do not systematically vary by distance to the nucleus, and they are invariant to city size, suggesting that local activity operates at a similar scale no matter than neighborhood; medial distances scale but less so than distal. We show the slope of the best fit line for each city, with brigher colors corresponding to larger cities; we find stratification where larger cities have both lower slopes and lower intercepts: smaller cities are more dependent on their urban centers while larger cities fragment into subcenters. An advantage of this method is that it lets us assign individual trips to components and examine the composition of them. We divide each neighbourhood into socio-economic quantiles to show that 50% and 30% of intergroup interaction across cities comes from distal and medial components, respectively, with implications for social contact given remote work trends; if centralized work declines, intergroup exposure may decrease. Cities face an efficiency–mixing trade‑off, and strengthening medial hubs raises medial weights and flattens medial means, reducing peripheral burdens while preserving access to the core. An ``efficient polycentric'' configuration therefore retains a strong, accessible nucleus while distributing everyday amenities to the medial scale.

nodes.title: Emergence and Evolution of Semantic Complexity in Urban Digital Twins
nodes.abstract: Contemporary urban dynamics are increasingly driven by pervasive digitalization, producing complex interdependencies between online platforms and offline behaviors. To adequately capture and analyze these intertwined processes, such dynamics need to be framed comprehensively through Urban Digital Twins (UDTs), which provide an integrated representation of both digital (e.g.: smart water city traffic solution) and physical urban systems (e.g.: transportation network)[1]. The complexity brought by UDTs raises the need for an systemic approach to trace its emergence and evolution across the evolving spectrum through science of science perspective[2]. While bibliometric indicators and science mapping techniques trace the emergence and evolution of concepts, they often rely on fragmented workflows across multiple tools and custom scripts. This limitation often arises when tracking conceptual development over time. Conceptual evolution is highly sensitive to choices in query formulation, database selection, filtering, and modeling, even minor differences in search strategies, pre-processing, or network construction can yield divergent narratives. Without an integrated workflow, distinguishing robust patterns from analytical artifacts is difficult. This study makes a twofold contribution. First, it develops an integrated pipeline linking literature retrieval (OpenAlex API), pre-processing, network construction and embeddings (Word2Vec), analytical metrics (centrality, edge scaling, community detection, temporal expansion), and interactive visualization (Dash), providing a fully transparent workflow. Second, a front-end interface allows end-to-end investigation of any topic’s conceptual emergence and evolution. These contributions underscore the importance of transparency and reproducibility in bibliometric reviews, illustrated here with literature on ‘Urban Digital Twins’ in titles or abstracts without date restrictions (Figure 1).  The network can be specified as a co-occurrence network where for every unordered pair (k1, k2) of distinct keywords that appear together in a publication, a co-occurrence count is increased by one. Jaccard similarity of keyword sets between years is stored as a graph-level attribute and provides a simple indicator of how much the set of active keywords changes from year to year where in year t and t − 1, with keyword sets Kt and Kt−1 and denoted as J(Kt,Kt−1) = |Kt∩Kt−1| |Kt∪Kt−1| . Furthermore, concept-method relations are modeled with a bipartite network from the same dataset. In this representation, one set of nodes corresponds to keywords interpreted as concepts and the other set corresponds to keywords interpreted as methods or technologies. An edge between a concept and a method indicates that they co-occur in at least one publication. To construct this network, the pipeline first classifies keywords into three categories: concept, method, or other. Each unique keyword from the corpus is presented to five open-source language models (Llama 3, Mistral, Gemma 2, Phi-3, and Qwen 2.5). Each model is prompted to return exactly one of the three labels, framed in the context of Urban Digital Twins research to reduce ambiguity. In the end, this study provides a coherent understanding of the conceptual development of Urban Digital Twins and offers a generic, transparent, and reproducible bibliometric method and tool, supplemented by a front-end interactive interface that enables spatiotemporal exploration of the conceptual network.

nodes.title: Mapping the Portuguese Business-to-Business Production Network: Structure, Dynamics, and Sectoral Heterogeneity
nodes.abstract: This paper analyzes Portugal's business-to-business production network using for the first time administrative transaction data from 2016–2024. Drawing on over 45 million firm-to-firm relationships among approximately 770.000 firms, the study documents structural asymmetries and sectoral differences that illuminate the foundations of production networks and their implications for shock propagation and aggregate output volatility in Portugal.  The analysis reveals a highly concentrated network: the top 1% of firms by connectivity account for 79% of all firm-to-firm links, while the top 10% capture 96% of observed relationships and 87% of aggregate B2B sales. The network exhibits a pronounced supplier-customer asymmetry that varies systematically by sector, reflecting how production technology and market structure jointly shape network structure.  Several network metrics are strongly heavy-tailed (approximately power-law in the upper tail), with important implications for aggregate fluctuations. Shocks to large firms may not average out, potentially generating macroeconomic volatility through granular mechanisms. The distribution of customer relationships shows a fatter tail than that of supplier relationships, a pattern consistent with stronger downstream concentration and potentially more intense propagation of demand-side shocks than supply-side shocks.  The analysis also reveals strong regularities: measures of systemic importance scale with firm size, firms face trade-offs between customer counts and sales per customer, and production chain position measures capture distinct and complementary dimensions of value chain structure.  Temporal analysis demonstrates network resilience: despite a 12–13% contraction in network activity during the 2020 pandemic, the network recovered rapidly by 2024. These findings advance understanding of production network formation, sectoral vulnerabilities to supply chain disruptions, and the potential for firm-level shocks to generate aggregate volatility through network amplification.

nodes.title: Subsumption Rules in Higher-Order Contagion
nodes.abstract: Conventional models of higher-order contagion, such as those defined on simplicial complexes, typically assume that higher-order interactions act as additive reinforcements to pairwise spreading. This synergy is widely recognized as the primary driver of discontinuous phase transitions and multi-stability. In this work, we challenge this additive paradigm by introducing a "subsumption rule": when a higher-order structure (e.g., a $2$-simplex) is activated, its constituent lower-order components (edges) are effectively covered and cease to function as independent transmission channels.  By the mean-field approach, we derive that the effective transmission rate in our model follows a binomial inversion. This analytical framework reveals that higher-order structures can counterintuitively act as inhibitors of spreading under certain conditions. By assuming a power-law scaling of the form $\beta_m = \beta_1 \cdot m^\alpha$, we show that our model recovers the classic network spreading behavior if and only of $\alpha=1$. Crucially, we identify a "Structural Braking" regime in the sub-linear case ($\alpha < 1$), where the subsumption of lower-order links outweighs the higher-order promotion, leading to a suppression of the steady-state prevalence.  Focusing on $2$-simplices, we systematically map the transition landscape across different scaling regimes: For $\alpha < 1$ (Regime I, suppression): Increasing simplicial density $k_{\Delta}$ leads to a consistent decrease in the steady-state prevalence $\rho^*$, confirming the suppression effect. For $\alpha > 1$ but relatively small (Regime II, weak promotion), the higher-order reinforcement is insufficient to trigger bi-stability. The system remains constrained by the topological saturation limit ($k_{\Delta} \leq k_1/2$), exhibiting a continuous phase transition. At sufficiently large $\alpha$ (Regime III, strong promotion), the model recovers the subcritical transcritical bifurcations and prominent hysteresis loops characteristic of explosive contagion, aligning with classical results but now understood as a competition between subsumption and scaling.  Our results provide a unified perspective on how local interaction rules—beyond mere topology—dictate the global phase transitions of complex systems. By bridging the gap between additive synergy and resource-constrained spreading, this work offers a theoretical foundation for more realistic modeling of information and disease diffusion.

nodes.title: Resilience of China’s Domestic Inter-Firm Transaction Network via Bow-Tie Architecture and LLM-Optimized OSINT
nodes.abstract: China’s domestic inter-firm transaction network—often described as the backbone of the world’s factory—has attracted growing strategic attention, yet remains highly opaque. This interest has intensified following the December 2025 revelations surrounding China’s semiconductor self-sufficiency initiatives and the rapid rise of domestic firms such as BYD in the electric vehicle sector. Conventional analyses of China’s domestic supply chain networks, however, are severely constrained by information asymmetry: in government-disclosed corporate filings, approximately 83\% of transaction partners are anonymized [1]. As a result, strategic inter-firm transaction networks are largely invisible in official records, creating a “black box” that prevents systemic resilience assessment. In this study, we focus explicitly on China’s domestic inter-firm transaction network, representing supplier–customer relationships among firms operating within China, rather than international trade flows.  To overcome the opacity of official disclosures, we develop a proprietary OSINT pipeline to aggregate fragmented evidence of inter-firm transaction relationships scattered across the web. We target 4,331 listed Chinese firms and 20,000 large Chinese enterprises with operating income exceeding \$130M. Even when focal firms do not disclose their transaction partners, we reconstruct domestic inter-firm links by triangulating partial disclosures from counterpart firms’ investor relations materials and reports from major media outlets. Using the Google Search API, we retrieve the top five sources per firm and process the resulting unstructured text using Qwen 3 with DSPy-optimized prompting [2]. Data reliability is further supported through randomized consistency checks using an LLM-as-a-Judge framework based on Gemini 3 Pro as an auxiliary validation tool.  The reconstructed domestic inter-firm transaction network provides an unprecedented view of China’s internal supply chain structure, capturing 26$\times$ more unique firms and 55$\times$ more transaction relationships than official disclosure-based datasets. Crucially, as shown in Figure 1(a), the resulting network restores plausible global topology: whereas official records exhibit an artificial truncation in degree beyond ten partners due to disclosure limits, fragmenting the network and undermining inference, the OSINT-derived network reproduces power-law-like degree patterns commonly observed in real-world inter-firm transaction networks [3]. Bow-tie decomposition reveals a giant strongly connected core (SCC) comprising 68,775 firms (56.2\%), accompanied by an upstream IN component comprising 11.2\% of firms and a downstream OUT component comprising 15.6\%.  Simulated targeted attacks further expose structural vulnerabilities concentrated in high-degree hubs, as shown in Figure 1(b). Degree-based hub attacks most effectively degrade global connectivity, yielding a robustness index of $R = 0.0267$, which is slightly lower (i.e., more destructive) than SCC-targeted attacks ($R = 0.0268$), while random failures are substantially less damaging ($R = 0.2496$). These findings indicate that the resilience of China’s domestic supply chain is governed not by the scale of individual firms, but by its structural dependence on a small set of central hub nodes within the domestic inter-firm transaction network.

nodes.title: Assessing the risk in statistically reconstructed firm-level critical raw material supply networks
nodes.authors: Baum, Sophia*; Heß, Maximilian; Klimek, Peter
nodes.abstract: Critical raw materials (CRMs) are essential for producing goods for everyday life and modern technologies. They are therefore of strategic importance to the EU economy but are also associated with a high risk of supply disruption. For example, over 80% of the global tungsten supply is mined in China and is primarily used to produce extremely hard and durable alloys for high-performance industrial tools, as well as in catalysts, pigments, lighting, and electronic applications. Dependencies on raw materials are often assessed only at the level of mined or minimally processed forms. Vulnerabilities that emerge further downstream are more challenging to identify and require a comprehensive, detailed representation of industrial value chains, which has so far proved difficult to achieve. The assessment is typically performed at the country level, only taking trade into account, not at the firm level, and therefore does not account for domestic production or company ownership. This study introduces (a) a novel approach to map value chain networks for critical raw materials (CRMs), combining AI-based generation, trade data, and statistical validation, and (b) a statistical reconstruction of the underlying firm network. The resulting networks can be used to evaluate and quantify concentration effects and vulnerabilities for individual regions and the global value chains in which they are embedded.  To automatically construct a value chain originating from a raw material at the sixth-digit level of the Harmonized System (HS), we use a two-step process. First, we identify a broad set of products related to the extracted or minimally processed material using a previously proposed production network [2]. These product pairs serve as provisional inputs to an LLM, which generates granular production paths from input to output using HS-like product descriptions. The embeddings of these descriptions are then matched to real HS descriptions using cosine similarity. Second, we post-process the preliminary network using four hyperparameters: an embedding threshold, a frequency threshold (how often a link is generated in step 1), an evaluation threshold (LLM-based pairwise validation), and a trade significance threshold (the correlation between input imports and output exports under a permutation test). The hyperparameters are calibrated using a manually researched phosphorus rock network. For the core components, basic phosphorus compounds, phosphates, and phosphatic fertilizers, accounting for about 90% of global phosphate rock use, we recover 100% of actual nodes and links with no false positives. To map the underlying firm network, we identify companies from the ORBIS database involved in the extraction, processing, or manufacturing of the focus critical raw material or products derived from it. This is done by first filtering ORBIS firm descriptions for keywords related to the critical raw material and its broad applications. To assign a firm to specific HS code, we first map the HS-based value chain network to its NACE4 equivalent. To evaluate whether a firm produces a certain HS code, the firm must be assigned to the mapped NACE4 sector or one of the first-order neighbors on the NACE4-based value chain network. This relaxes the strict requirement of an exact NACE4 sector match, as such assignments are often imprecise. We then task an LLM to evaluate whether a specific firm is involved in the production of a given product. If the evaluation is positive, we consider the firm a producer of that product. Thus, for each product in the HS-based value chain network, we obtain a list of producing firms. We then connect the firms using a gravity model of trade, incorporating the underlying input-output structure and trade data [3] as constraints. Figure 1 shows one example of such a network for tungsten, including companies that extract and process tungsten or manufacture intermediate products part of the tungsten value chain. Companies are scored

nodes.title: Product-level value chains from firm data: mapping trophic levels into economic growth
nodes.authors: Fessina, Massimiliano; Tacchella, Andrea ; Zaccaria, Andrea*
nodes.abstract: Global value chains shape everything from the price of coffee to the roll-out of clean technology, yet policy still relies on sector-level input–output maps that gloss over the differences among the single products. Our study reconstructs a detailed product-level value chain by identifying statistically significant input-output relationships between products derived from firm-level trade data. The result is a network of about 5000 products, which provides a tool for analyzing the structure and function of global value chains at an unprecedented level of detail. Leveraging concepts from ecological network theory, we assign a trophic level to each product, providing a topology-based measure of its production stage and allowing us to assess the overall hierarchical structure of the network through the trophic coherence. This enables the computation of the average trophic level of imports and exports for firms and countries, which provides information about industrial specialization patterns and economic growth.
