We show that the trophic structure of value chains is only detectable at a fine product resolution, and that firms tend to operate with a positive trophic jump - i.e., they export more downstream products than they import. At the country level, we observe a strong negative correlation between export diversification and the average trophic level of imports: more diversified economies import more upstream products, while countries with low diversification are constrained to import downstream goods — a pattern we define trophic barrier. This relationship reflects differences in industrial specialization, with manufacturing-intensive countries like China and Italy importing more basic inputs than service-oriented economies like the US and France. Similarly, we find that trophic jump correlates positively with diversification, indicating that more advanced countries add greater value by transforming basic inputs into complex outputs. Crucially, these trophic metrics are not just descriptive: we demonstrate that both the average trophic level of a country’s imports and its trophic jump are strong predictors of future economic growth. Our detailed, totally data-driven input-output network of products provides a practical reference for trade strategists, development planners, and researchers navigating supply-chain reconfiguration and geopolitical fragmentation.

nodes.title: Distance Dependent Bayesian Community Detection
nodes.authors: Beaudry, Isabelle*; Mendoza, Marcelo; Bro, Naim
nodes.abstract: Community detection in networks seeks to identify to which cluster the nodes belong. Various traditional methods consider only the connections among the members of the population to make this classification. The rationale behind those methods is that highly connected nodes are more likely to belong to the same cluster than poorly connected ones. Although structure-based community detection algorithms may perform well under some circumstances, these methods ignore information included in many real-world networks, such as the characteristics of their members. For instance, nodal attributes may represent a person's characteristics, such as age, gender, and interests. The node attributes may affect the formation of clusters in a population. This phenomenon is well documented in the social science literature and is known as network homophily. Consequently, models leveraging link density and attribute homogeneity may help improve community detection. The main contribution of this work is to propose a community detection method that guarantees a lower link density across clusters than within clusters for any community pairs and also considers the similarity of the nodes with respect to their attributes. The inference is then performed under a Bayesian nonparametric framework. We assess the performance of the method through an extensive simulation study. Furthermore, we compare our results in real data with state-of-the-art community detection methodology for network data.

nodes.title: From Case Counts to Contact Networks: Inferring Epidemic Coupling in Space and Time
Beyond Euclidean Distance-Based Clustering: A Network Approach to Unsupervised Classification
nodes.abstract: Unsupervised classification is a fundamental tool for organizing complex datasets into groups of similar elements, enabling the identification of shared characteristics and underlying processes, while averaging out individual data point variability. Traditional unsupervised learning approaches, such as k-means and hierarchical clustering, typically operate in a vector space and rely on Euclidean distance to compute class centroids and relationships among the data. This setup is often poorly suited to empirical data, where features are correlated, information is redundant, and meaningful relationships may not be adequately captured by Euclidean metrics. Although dimensionality reduction is commonly employed to address feature correlations, Euclidean distances remain susceptible to degradation in moderately to high-dimensional spaces and may fail to encode non-linear or directional dependencies.  We introduce a network-based workflow for unsupervised classification that replaces vector-space representations with similarity networks. Pairwise relationships between data points, computed using arbitrary similarity or dependency measures (e.g., cosine similarity or mutual information), are encoded as weighted edges in a fully connected graph. This formulation decouples clustering from Euclidean geometry and enables the use of metrics tailored to the structure of the data and the scientific question of interest. To reveal the underlying informative network topology, we extract a statistically significant network backbone using the disparity filter. Community detection is then applied to the backbone network to identify classes of data points. By simultaneously constructing a network of data points and a network of features, the proposed framework enables the direct interpretability of clusters in terms of their dominant characteristics.  We demonstrate the approach on the Geospatial Attributes of Gages for Evaluating Streamflow (GAGES) dataset, comprising 9,067 watersheds in the continental United States described by over 300 environmental variables. The resulting watershed communities show strong spatial coherence and distinct signatures associated with natural and anthropogenic drivers, and correspond to markedly different hydrological behaviors. We show that tuning the workflow parameters controls edge density and community resolution, enabling investigation at different granularities. In addition, network-specific measures, such as degree centrality, can be used to select representative elements within each class, useful to prioritize instruments deployment. Due to its metric flexibility and reliance on network topology, the proposed method is domain-agnostic and applicable to a wide range of high-dimensional datasets.

nodes.title: Disparity-in-Differences: Extracting Hierarchical Backbones of Weighted Directed Networks
nodes.abstract: Empirical networks often consist of heterogeneous and asymmetrical relations. Existing network backbone extraction methods have successfully identified significant relations based on heterogeneity but are not designed to incorporate asymmetry that may encode hierarchical structures. To fill the gap, motivated by the concept of power-dependence relations [1], this paper proposes a new method named "disparity-in-differences" that measures a statistical significance of asymmetric dependence, which is defined as the difference of normalized edge weights between two nodes. The term "disparity" is adopted from the disparity filter [2], a widely used network backbone extraction method.   According to the concept of power-dependence relations [1], the dependence of i on j is equal to the power of j over i. In this framework, power imbalance between i and j can be represented as the difference in dependencies. For two nodes i and j, the normalized weight of an edge i -> j is considered as the extent that i depends on j and vice versa. The difference between the normalized weights of i -> j and j -> i would thus serve as a proxy for a hierarchical relation. To calculate its statistical significance, the proposed method samples values from a null model that assumes edge weights are uniformly distributed over neighbors as used in the disparity filter [2]. A sampled value for a node i (x_{ij}) follows a Beta distribution, Beta(1,k_{i}-1), where k_{i} is the number of out-neighbors of i. If the difference between two associated normalized weights is significantly higher or lower than x_{ij} - x_{ji} values, the proposed method assigns an unweighted edge i -> j or j -> i to indicate j or i is at a higher position in a hierarchy, respectively (Figure 1).   For evaluation, the proposed method is applied to a journal citation network, a U.S. airport network, the Enron email network, and a world trade network. Compared to existing network backbone extraction methods, it better captures hierarchical relations that align with journal quality ratings, airport hub categories by size, levels of management, and a core-periphery structure of countries, respectively. Backbones extracted by the disparity-in-differences method will provide insights into not only known hierarchies but also emerging, fading, or missing hierarchical relations.  [1] Emerson, R. M. (1962). Power-dependence relations. American Sociological Review, 27(1), 31-41.  [2] Serrano, M. Á., Boguná, M., & Vespignani, A. (2009). Extracting the multiscale backbone of complex weighted networks. Proceedings of the national academy of sciences, 106(16), 6483-6488.

nodes.title: Compressibility of Neuronal Activity Across Dynamical Regimes
nodes.abstract: Complex networks often exhibit rich collective dynamics that depend sensitively on patterns of interactions between nodes. In neuronal systems, these interactions can lead to distinct dynamical regimes—subcritical, critical, and supercritical— each characterized by different patterns of propagation, correlation, and information flow [1,2]. A central hypothesis in this field is that healthy brain activity operates near a critical point [1], enabling efficient communication and flexible computation. This motivates the central question in this work: to what extent is the apparent variability in brain activity driven by a relatively small set of informative correlations, and how does this depend on the underlying neural dynamics? In this work, we study neuronal systems through the lens of information compressibility [3], a measure that quantifies how much uncertainty in collective dynamics can be reduced by a subset of pairwise interactions. This measure is grounded in the minimax entropy principle [4], which seeks the least-biased probabilistic model consistent with a chosen set of constraints. Specifically, neural activity is modeled using a multivariate Gaussian maximum-entropy distribution constrained by selected pairwise covariances. As correlations are added, the model’s entropy decreases, indicating that more structure is being captured. The greedy minimax-entropy algorithm determines, at each step, which correlation, when included, results in the most substantial reduction in global entropy. This process creates an optimal compression curve that measures how effectively network interactions account for collective variability. We replicate the greedy minimax-entropy compression framework introduced in [3] and extend it to synthetic neuronal activity generated using networks of Izhikevich neurons [5]. Our initial results focus on activity simulated on random networks (Erd˝os-R´enyi graphs, p = 0.2) consisting of N neurons with excitatory (80%) and inhibitory (20%) populations [6]. Different dynamical regimes were produced by tuning the effective branching ratio or, equivalently, adjusting the balance of excitation and inhibition. Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the analysis pipeline. Fig. 1A shows an example of simulated neural time series generated in a near-critical regime (branching ratio, σ = 0.9), while Fig. 1B shows its corresponding correlation matrix. Fig. 1C shows the resulting greedy minimax-entropy compression curves across several branching ratios, showing systematic differences in compressibility based on the branching ratio of the dynamics. Fig. 1D summarizes these results by plotting the area above the compression curves in 1C as a function of branching ratio. We find that strongly subcritical dynamics are easy to compress because the activity is highly redundant and quickly dies out. In contrast, supercritical dynamics are harder to compress, as bursting and unstable activity produce noisy correlation patterns. Near-critical regimes fall between these extremes: their activity can be compressed efficiently but not trivially, indicating a balance between structure and variability. Together, these results show that information compressibility provides a clear, system-level indicator of the underlying neural dynamical regime.   1] Beggs & Plenz (2003). Neuronal avalanches in neocortical circuits. Journal of Neuroscience, 23(35), 11167-11177. [2] Shew, Yang, Yu, Roy, & Plenz (2011). Information capacity and transmission are maximized in balanced cortical networks with neuronal avalanches. Journal of Neuroscience, 31(1), 55-63. [3] Weaver, Faskowitz, Betzel, & Lynn (2025). Quantifying the compressibility of the human brain. arXiv:2510.16327. [4] Lynn, Yu, Pang, Palmer, & Bialek (2025). Exact minimax entropy models of large-scale neuronal activity. Physical Review E, 111(5). [5] Izhikevich (2003). Simple model of spiking neurons. IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks, 14(6), 1569-1572. [6] Braitenberg & Sch¨uz (2013). An

nodes.title: Effects of screen time and activities on mental health in children
nodes.abstract: Children and adolescents are engaging with digital technologies at increasingly younger ages, and recent years have seen a  rapid expansion of interactive tools such as AI chatbots and wellness applications. A growing body of literature links screen time and digital media use to mental health outcomes in youth, including depressive symptoms, sleep disruption, and suicidal ideation, suggesting a complex interplay between individual differences, contextual factors, and environmental  characteristics [1,2]. To investigate divergent associations between screen use (e.g., social media, video games, TV,  phone), physical activities, and mental health during childhood and early adolescence, we used data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, a large (N ≈ 12, 000) longitudinal (t = 4) dataset. We conducted an exploratory analysis at baseline (year 0), selecting participants with ≥ 80% valid responses across screen time, activity, mental health, and social measures (n = 3, 713). Heterogeneity in screen time and activity profiles was examined by  computing subject × subject similarity matrices and applying the Louvain algorithm for modularity maximization [3]. We  retained clusters with at least 10% of the subjects, resulting in six clusters covering 91% of the selected participants. Clusters were characterized by screen time and activity patterns, and their associations with mental health outcomes. In Figure 1 we can observe preliminary analysis with different clusters presenting distinct screen usage patterns. For example, in Figure 1B, Cluster 2 expresses higher screen activity reported both by the children and their parents and  correspondent higher scores in most problematic mental health outcomes. Further analysis is being developed. Future  work will also explore this approach longitudinally, investigating how patterns of screen use and categorized activities  relate to mental health trajectories and brain organization, with a focus on developing analytical frameworks that can capture the complex, bidirectional relationships between digital media use and youth mental health.

nodes.title: Complexity, Risk and Resilience in Policy Sub-Systems
nodes.abstract: In this paper we assess systemic resilience [1] by considering risk complexity and risk predictability in a simulation of a policy making sub-system. The aim is to determine whether a range of different risks have a differential impact on the production of consensus or the conditions that lead them to degenerate to polarisation and conflict. We account for different types of power for political agents, such as influence, authority, relational and information advantage. We further acknowledge complexity in that policy domains are interdependent and that external shocks can alter system properties (i.e. affect the terms of reference for the interaction between actors). We also account for policy issue salience among political agents; the different phases of the policy process, which determine the level of their engagement; and the level of agitation contingent to proximity to a decision/vote. We employ data from well documented cases of longitudinal policy making [2] and use t1 as baseline of networks of policy making comparing to t2 with Agent Based Model simulations which provide an opportunity to test for different network and complexity effects. The synthetic data simulate deliberations during a specific stage of the agenda setting and consultation stage in the policy process. The aim is to test for the impact of a range of political risks, including system polarization, sensitivity to conflict during negotiations, system disintegration, and variance from ex ante preferences of policy actors (i.e. risk in the predictability of outcomes ex ante). The model employed is a hybrid network model, combined with an agent-based model. Modeling includes variables for issue salience, clustering level, network properties (reciprocity, transitivity, etc), tie maintenance costs, and agent power among others. Extreme events are also modeled such as external shocks, while agents impact one another via a DeGroot and a bounded-confidence Friedkin-Johnsen influence process. Also modeled is the presence of exceptional agents, such as political entrepreneurs and leaders. Initial results indicate resilient political systems are affected by risks from policy shifts, and shocks from policy debates when they degenerate to echo-chamber exchanges [3]. Resilience also appears to have a negative relationship to entropy risks. Furthermore, the presence of political entrepreneurs significantly affects the probability of achieving policy consensus and reduces policy variance. This study confirms the sensitivity of political outcomes to the interaction between policy system resilience, risk level and risk type.

nodes.title: Comparative study of networks of writers in different historical periods obtained by Wikipedia-mining
nodes.authors: Cwilich, Gabriel*; Agapitos, Paschalis; Spivak, Yonatan; Schwartz, Gustavo A.
nodes.abstract: From the English version of Wikipedia, we extracted the pages corresponding to persons in the literary world, and we created six networks organized by birth date, from 1700 to 1999. The weighted links among them are not the standard hyperlinks but their more subtle relatedness which is  obtained calculating the Google distance[1,2] (GD)  between the pages of the most relevant authors in each period (as measured by the in-degree  of their Wikipedia pages); the relatedness is the negative exponential of their GD.  Many of the characteristics of these networks  (their size, average connectivity, size and order of the largest core) follow clear temporal patterns, presenting an exponential growth across time (with an exception for the last historical period which represents a "work in progress"), and that trend is consistent with the trends of Wikipedia as a whole;  in spite of that, the strengths of the weighted links remain almost identical  across the different historical periods, presenting in all cases a clear bimodal distribution of weak and strong links; the relative weight of weak links increases in time as the relations between writers diversify.   We implemented a spectral analysis of the different networks, using the eigenvalue distribution of the normalized Laplacian; while the average eigvenvalue remains constant in time, their  distribution narrows; but remarkable, the algebraic connectivity of all the networks remains constant in spite of the size of the networks changing by more than an order of magnitude (from 50 to 1100) and the increasing value of their (Shannon) spectral entropy[3].  Since the networks described above  are dense, we used some procedures of sparsification (triangular inequality backbone extraction, standard thresholding and disparity filtering, keeping equal levels of connectivity across networks) in order to further or analysis, and the different discussed lead to similar results.   The identity of the writers belonging to the highest cores provides insights on their  connections, not gleaned  apriori from the structure of Wikipedia itself. In some cases it reveals connections linked to national origin or language, in others to the thematic content of their writing, or some unexpected connections between two different national groups.  We studied the process of disintegration of each  network upon successive removal of nodes in decreasing order of their degree or closeness or edges in decreasing order of their betweenness, monitoring  the evolution of the largest components.  The removal of nodes based on closeness uncovered an interesting hierarchical structure, since across all networks and connectivity levels, at certain points the removal  of a node results in the splitting of the network in two pieces of similar size, revealing the existence of  communities, and sub-communities within them (see Figure 1) which had no links connecting them in the original network (and not  visible in the overall structure of the network). Normally these communities split along national or linguistic lines; their study might provide valuable insights to complement what a more "microscopic”, discipline specific,  approach can provide.  [1] G. Schwartz, Human. Soc. Sci. Comm. 8, 127 (2021) [2]. L. Miccio , C. Gamez-Perez, J.L. Suarez and G. Schwartz, Adv. Comp. Syst. 25, 2240010 [3] M. De Dominico and J. Biamonte., Phys Rev X, 6 ,040062 (2016)

nodes.title: Network science techniques applied to soft matter simulations
nodes.abstract: Soft matter provides a concrete instance of networks leading to complex physical properties. In this poster, we demonstrate the use of network science tools to study transitions in connectivity and mechanics of soft matter systems. These tools provide tangible evidence for the physical mechanisms that underlie material properties in two systems of interest: percolation robustness in ion exchange membranes, and graph coarsening in granular systems.  For material systems, the transition between a state of percolation to a state of disjointedness can have important ramifications on their properties. To computationally track a percolation process, we propose the use of k-stub decomposition (KSD) [1], which excludes particles with degree less than k from the network. In physical networks, increasing k has the effect of peeling back layers from the surface; hence, increasing k can remove weakly connected surface dendrites and bottlenecks. For example, in our study [2], anion exchange membranes undergo a sharp increase in ionic conductivity at specific relative humidities, presumably due to percolation of ion channels comprising water molecules. KSD analysis of molecular simulations reveals that this transition takes place when channels are robust at the k = 3 level, revealing that 3 water molecules are needed in the channel at a minimum to enable a vehicular transport of ions.   The second system we have studied deals with dense granular suspensions: under the right conditions, these systems undergo shear thickening due to frictional constraints between the particles. As these systems flow, the frictional force chain network (FFCN) evolves as frictional contacts are formed and broken. The properties of FFCN are dependent on the kind of constraints (frictional constants, rolling/sliding frictions, etc.) and conditions (density, shear rate). In this study [3], we used graph coarsening to identify independent force chains, hubs, and terminals. The force chain length distribution and the connectivity of the FFCN provide useful insight into the trends in rheology of these systems.   Beyond these two examples, we are currently applying these techniques to other soft matter systems, including complex polymeric mesophases, metamaterials, and nanoparticle self-assembly.  [1] Mohseni-Kabir, A., Pant, M., Towsley, D., Guha, S., & Swami, A. (2021). Percolation thresholds for robust network connec- tivity. Journal of Statistical Mechanics: Theory and Experiment, 2021 (1), 013212. [2] Wang, Z., Sun, G., Lewis, N. H., Mandal, M., Sharma, A., Kim, M., ... & de Pablo, J. J. (2025). Water-mediated ion transport in an anion exchange membrane. Nature Communications, 16 (1), 1099. [3] Sharma, S., Sharma, A., & Singh, A. (2025). Frictional contact network in dense suspension flow. arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.22747.

nodes.title: A Metapopulation Network Framework for Evaluating Travel-Based Intervention Strategies for Global Outbreak Control
nodes.authors: Bay, Clara*; Davis, Jessica; Chinazzi, Matteo; Vespignani, Alessandro
nodes.abstract: Early intervention at the source of an outbreak is widely recognized as the most effective way to curb spread in the initial phase. Travel restrictions and border-control measures are a common example: they are frequently among the first internationally deployed actions, despite mixed evidence on their effectiveness. In practice, however, policy responses are often reactive, introduced only after epidemiological thresholds are crossed. Here, we explicitly evaluate timing as the key policy lever by comparing proactive  versus reactive travel-based interventions during emerging outbreaks. We model global disease spread on a metapopulation mobility network, in which geographic areas (subpopulations) are connected by empirically travel flows. Within each subpopulation, transmission dynamics follow an SLIR compartmental model, while individuals move between subpopulations along network edges according to mobility data. We evaluate six travel and border-control intervention strategies spanning two policy approaches: reactive measures, in which controls are implemented only after an epidemiological trigger is reached and are applied to travel from the outbreak source, and proactive measures, in which controls are implemented early and reduce mobility more broadly across the network (Fig. 1A & B). Within each category, we consider three interventions: (i) border-control measures only (mobility reduction), (ii) border-control measures combined with targeted mitigation that reduces transmission at the source, and (iii) border-control measures combined with global mitigation that reduces transmission across all locations. Using stochastic numerical simulations on both theoretical random networks and the data-driven Global Epidemic and Mobility (GLEAM) metapopulation model, we systematically vary implementation timing and the intensity of mobility reduction.  Results (Fig. 1C) highlight the limitations of reactive, source-focused border measures, which are generally implemented too late to substantially alter international dissemination. In contrast, proactive, early border-control strategies, especially when paired with measures that reduce transmission (global mitigation), provide markedly stronger control of global spread across a wide range of timings and intensities, and can significantly delay or even successfully contain outbreaks. Overall, we offer a general framework to quantify the trade-offs between proactive versus reactive travel restrictions and border control in a globally connected system during the earliest stages of an emerging outbreak. These insights can help inform timely policy design and strengthen preparedness for future emerging infectious disease threats.

nodes.title: Party Time: A Temporal Network Dataset of a Social Celebration and its Bursty Dynamics
nodes.abstract: Human proximity networks from conferences, classrooms, hospitals, and workplaces have consistently exhibited bursty interaction dynamics [1]. However, many of these contexts involved scheduled interactions of different lengths and participants with different formal roles. Is burstiness an inherent feature of organic social interaction, or limited to settings with heterogeneous types of interactions? Do the temporal statistics of human interaction observed in professional, academic, and public gatherings extend to a private social celebration?  We collected high-resolution proximity data from a cocktail hour of a wedding in a Chicago suburb. Ninety-three consenting wedding guests wore Ultra-Wideband (UWB) proximity badges that detected when other badge-wearers were within 1.5m (± 0.1 m, 5s bins), supplemented by coarse location estimates and pre– and post–event surveys on personality and self-reported conversations. Participants self-identified their wedding party affiliation (among friend/family groups), enabling direct social group-level mixing analysis instead of demographic proxies relied upon for previous group-mixing studies (e.g., [2]). We are preparing a de-identified public release of the resulting temporal network, contributing a novel “private party” setting to the public corpus of face-to-face interaction temporal networks.  Preliminary comparisons to one-hour windows from SocioPatterns collections [3] (conferences, schools, office, hospital) show similarly robust bursty dynamics in the party context, though with systematic differences. As seen in Figure 1, contact rate (edge weights of the integrated network) distributions agree closely; contact duration distribution remains comparably broad but exhibit an earlier cut-off; the inter-arrival time distribution is steeper yet exhibits a stronger power-law trend than the SocioPatterns contexts. Using the self-identified groups, we are investigating if differences between within-group and cross-group interaction timing contributes to the overall burstiness. Once released, this data collection will enable more empirically grounded modeling of social events, whether for contact tracing simulations or cultural studies, in addition to refining the universality of face-to-face interaction burstiness.   [1] Barrat, A., Cattuto, C., “Temporal Networks of Face-to-Face Human Interactions” [2] M. Oliveira, et al., “Group mixing drives inequality in face-to-face gatherings” [3] https://www.sociopatterns.org/

nodes.title: When PageRank Feeds Back: Fixed Points of Personalized Centrality in Complex Networks
nodes.abstract: PageRank is one of the most influential centrality measures in network science, originally introduced for ranking web pages and later generalized to a wide range of applications in social, biological, economic, and technological networks. While classical PageRank theory focuses on the existence and uniqueness of a stationary distribution for a fixed personalization vector, less attention has been given to PageRank as a functional operator acting on the space of personalization vectors itself. This presentation studies PageRank from this functional perspective. The central aim of the poster is to provide a complete characterization of existence, uniqueness, and structure of fixed points of PageRank functional $PR_\lambda$ in terms of the network’s strongly connected components. Specifically, it is analytically demonstrated that if $\mathcal{G}$ is a strongly connected network, then $PR_\lambda$ admits a unique fixed point, which can be analytically determined. Conversely, when $\mathcal{G}$ is not strongly connected, existence of fixed points is still guaranteed, while uniqueness depends on the structure of the strongly connected components (sinks).   Beyond their theoretical significance, these results hold practical implications for dynamic and temporal networks, where the underlying topology evolves over time. In such contexts, the personalization vector itself may vary dynamically, raising the natural question of how to select teleportation distributions that reflect intrinsic structural properties of the network. For example, in web graphs, one could adopt a feedback mechanism in which teleportation probabilities at a given time step are determined by the PageRank distribution from the previous time step. The steady state of such a process, if it exists, corresponds precisely to the fixed point of the operator $PR_\lambda$. Hence, by adopting a functional operator perspective, this work establishes a rigorous analytical framework for PageRank in complex networks, elucidating both the mathematical structure of the operator and its potential for modeling adaptive and temporally evolving systems.

nodes.title: Bridging contacts as epidemic bottlenecks in a city-scale mobility network
nodes.authors: Zambrano, Marlli*; Thampi, Ashish; Jarynowski, Andrzej ; Rincon, Alejandra; Schulz, Steven ; Belik, Vitaly
nodes.abstract: Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) such as lockdowns and movement restrictions aim to reduce close-range en- counters that can transmit airborne infections. Yet contacts are not equally consequential, their epidemiological impact depends on where they sit in the larger network of human encounters. Here we ask whether a small set of structurally “bridging” contacts can act as transmission bottlenecks and therefore provide high-leverage targets for prevention. We analyze opt-in, anonymized geo-location data from 14 430 Berlin residents with persistent identifiers collected from June to August 2023. From individual trajectories, we reconstruct city-wide mobility and contact networks. A contact is defined by co-location of two individuals within 8 m for at least 2 min, yielding 12 048 observed contacts. Using Louvain community detection on the resulting encounter network, we identify loosely connected mesoscale units and quantify how community structure relates to urban boundaries. Visitation and mixing patterns still align with Berlin’s former east–west division and with current district borders (boundary alignment p << 0.01), indicating that historical and administrative structure continues to shape present-day encounter networks. Classifying edges by community membership shows a near- even split between within-community (6 379) and between-community (5 669) contacts. However, within-community contacts are about 5 x more likely to recur between the same partners, while between-community contacts are more tran- sient. To evaluate intervention leverage, we run SIR-type outbreak simulations and compare edge-removal strategies at a fixed “contact reduction budget.” Removing 30% of edges produces strongly strategy-dependent outcomes: selectively removing between-community edges reduces total infections far more than random deletion of the same size. Relative to the no-removal baseline, the attack rate drops to 0.33 ± 0.05 under targeted inter-community removal versus 0.58 ± 0.08 under random removal, corresponding to 43% fewer infections than random suppression for the same reduction in contacts. These results highlight bridging links between communities as disproportionate drivers of urban transmission and suggest that network-aware policies that selectively limit cross-community mixing could achieve substantial epidemic control with less disruption than uniform restrictions.

nodes.title: Percolation and Criticality in Hyperuniform Networks
nodes.authors: Wang, Yongyi*; Kim, Jaeuk; Jiao, Yang; Stuhl, Izabella; Torquato, Salvatore; Albert, Reka
nodes.abstract: Hyperuniform many-particle systems, which encompass crystals, quasicrystals and certain exotic disordered systems, exhibit an anomalous suppression of density fluctuations on macroscopic length scales relative to those of conventional disordered systems [1]. These systems are well studied in Euclidean space. We propose that networks derived from hyperuniform point configurations show distinctive critical behaviors. In this study, we construct Delaunay triangulation networks of 2D point configurations, in which the points become the vertices and the edges are weighted by Euclidean distance.   We investigate the percolation behaviors of networks derived from disordered stealthy hyperuniform systems, which are an important subclass that exhibits a tunable degree of local order via the parameter $\chi$ [2]. We compare them with those constructed from Poisson (uncorrelated) point configurations. We investigate a distance-based bond percolation process in which shorter edges in the triangulation network are assigned higher occupation probabilities according to a tuning parameter $z$.   We estimate the percolation thresholds $z_c$ and critical exponents of the networks via finite-size scaling and the Newman–Ziff Monte Carlo algorithm. We find that stealthy hyperuniform networks exhibit lower percolation thresholds than Poisson networks. The higher the value of $\chi$, the lower the percolation threshold, suggesting enhanced connectivity and transport efficiency in networks with hyperuniform spatial embeddings, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:configs}. Moreover, we show that stealthy hyperuniform systems with large $\chi$ belong to the lattice universality class, while the critical exponents of the Poisson and low-$\chi$ systems exhibit consistent shifts.   Recent attempts have illustrated the challenges of classifying statistically homogeneous disordered planar networks using classical network centrality measures [3]. Our distance-based percolation method is able to identify and distinguish these networks with suppressed density fluctuations through their emergent clustering properties.  [1] S. Torquato and F. H. Stillinger, “Local density fluctuations, hyperuniformity, and order metrics,” Phys. Rev. E, vol. 68, no. 4, p. 041113, 2003.  [2] S. Torquato, “Hyperuniform states of matter,” Phys. Rep., vol. 745, pp. 1–95, 2018.  [3] E. Newby, W. Shi, Y. Jiao, S. Torquato and R. Albert, "From point patterns to networks: to what extent does the Delaunay triangulation reproduce key spatial and density information?" Journal of Complex Networks, vol. 13, no. 6, 2025.

nodes.title: Time-varying differential network analysis from tumor-educated platelet transcriptome to capture mechanisms of tumor progression
nodes.authors: Vessi, Santiago; Rughetti, Aurelia; Filetti, Marco; Farina, Lorenzo; Petti, Manuela*
nodes.abstract: Differential network analysis has been designed to highlight network changes between conditions: in network biology, this approach can capture tissue-selective processes and identify critical signaling pathways altered during tumor transformation and progression [1]. In oncology, the most appropriate tumor surveillance strategy remains an unsolved issue. Indeed, the typical approach (based on solid biopsies and resource-demanding follow-up programs) has limitations in terms of invasiveness, time resolution and accessibility of molecular data. To date, blood-based liquid biopsies represent a promising source of biomarkers to detect and monitor cancer without invasive procedures. The latest developments in isolating and characterizing various elements in blood, including tumor-educated platelet (TEP) RNA, make it possible to identify cancer-specific genomic and transcriptomic abnormalities in blood. In particular, TEPs are circulating blood cells with a distinct tumor-driven phenotype and act as carriers and protectors of metastases. Some recent studies have shown that the TEPs transcriptome can be used for cancer diagnosis [2]. However, these works are only based on differential expression analysis.  The objective of this work is to propose Differential Network Analysis (DNA) to study longitudinal TEPs transcriptomic data. In particular, we applied the Time-varying DNA (tDNA, [3]) to investigate gene network rewiring during glioblastoma (GBM) progression.  We downloaded mRNA sequencing data of TEPs from the GEO database under accession number 156902 [4] and we selected GBM samples. Longitudinal data were available for 55 patients, although for a subset of 13 patients a complete set of measurements from time 0 to time 3 was available. Given the K=4 time points, the tDNA approach jointly estimated K-1=3 differential networks ∆, enabling the identification of both consistent and time-specific rewiring patterns driven by key genes. These genes were classified as either common (shared across time points) or time-specific critical nodes. Across the three obtained differential networks, we identified 14 common and 134, 80, and 72 time-specific key genes for ∆_(0-1), ∆_(1-2) and ∆_(2-3), respectively. KEGG enrichment analysis of the identified nodes revealed convergence on immune and inflammatory signaling pathways, including IL-17 signaling (adj-p=0.005), Toll-like receptor signaling (adj-p=0.008), and Herpes simplex virus 1 infection (adj-p=0.01), largely driven by time-specific genes from each differential network. These findings highlight processes associated with tumor progression and immune modulation in glioblastoma [5]. To validate the findings obtained with tDNA, we compared them with DiffCorr approach [6], developed to compare pairs of experimental conditions. This alternative method identified 101, 120, and 102 critical nodes in the three differential networks, selected by intersecting the top 10% of genes ranked by degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality. Seventy-one genes overlapped with those identified by tDNA, including 4 classified as common hubs. However, KEGG enrichment analysis did not highlight significant pathways. In conclusion, these results demonstrate that critical nodes identified in time-varying differential networks obtained from longitudinal TEPs transcriptomic data can detect molecular mechanisms of disease progression with potential clinical impact in tumor monitoring.  [1]	Yan W et al. Mol Ther Nucleic Acids. 2024. doi: 10.1016/j.omtn.2024.102260. [2]	Best MG et al, Cancer Cell. 2015. Doi: 10.1016/j.ccell.2015.09.018. [3]	Xu T et al. IEEE/ACM Trans Comput Biol Bioinform. 2021. doi: 10.1109/TCBB.2019.2949039. [4]	Sol N et al. Cell Rep Med. 2020. doi: 10.1016/j.xcrm.2020.100101. [5]	Parajuli P & Mittal S. J Spine Neurosurg. 2013. doi: 10.4172/2325-9701.S1-004. [6]	Fukushima A. Gene. 2013. doi: 10.1016/j.gene.2012.11.028.

nodes.title: Community-aware hyperbolic embeddings: methodology and application on the FlyWire FAFB dataset
nodes.abstract: Hyperbolic geometry offers a superior latent space for embedding hierarchical complex networks, yet existing methods frequently overlook the utility of efficient community detection methods. We present CLOVE (Cluster Level Optimized Vertex Embedding), a recently published approach that maps networks to the hyperbolic disk by using community structure to dictate angular sectors —optimized via a Traveling Salesman Problem heuristic. We apply this framework to the massive FlyWire Drosophila FAFB connectome, benchmarking the results against ground-truth physical coordinates. Our analysis demonstrates that incorporating community priors generates resource-efficient embeddings that successfully capture the topological organization of large-scale biological neural networks.

nodes.title: The Impact of Network Structure and Prior Assumptions on Network Reconstructability
nodes.authors: Glover, Cory*; Weis, Erik; Kumar, Sagar; Ehlert, Joey; Klein, Brennan
nodes.abstract: Many real-world systems from biology to financial markets are governed by complex networks of interactions where the network structures are either directly unobservable or must be inferred from data [1]. Often, researchers perform this network reconstruction task by using time series recordings of node-level dynamics in the system.   When reconstructing a network from N node-level time series, there are O(N^2) possible edges to infer. With so many possible edges, any practical network reconstruction method requires regularization to avoid overfitting and yield acceptable results [2]. Regularization, however, can impose structural preferences, producing biases in the network reconstruction. Despite the ubiquity of network reconstruction, these induced structural biases are rarely characterized [3,4].  While there are many techniques for reconstructing networks from time series data, we focus on a nonparametric Bayesian method based on the minimum description length [3]. We use this approach due to its: 1) clear and explicit statistical biases that can be readily interpreted, 2) lack of hyperparameters, the values of which are generally unknown in practical settings, 3) broad applications for a large class of networks, and 4) asymptotic recoverability of the true data-generating model, which is contained within the model class. This last condition ensures that if we record node states for an infinite number of time steps, we are guaranteed to recover the true network exactly, so long as the dynamical process satisfies certain properties [2]. On the contrary, in the case of limited data, our reconstruction will be more heavily influenced by the model’s prior assumptions.  In this work, we simulate Ising model dynamics on a range of synthetic networks, and we investigate the following: given a particular choice of statistical bias, how does reconstruction error vary with the class of network in question? While such questions have been considered in the case where network properties are directly conditioned on the prior [3,4], we instead focus on the case when no network statistics are known a priori, which is a realistic scenario in many practical applications with real data. We find that in the low data regime: 1) dense networks are systematically reconstructed as more sparse (Fig. 1a), 2) networks with high clustering are not reconstructed as such (Fig. 1b), and 3) networks with heterogeneous degree are harder to recover (Fig. 1c). This last finding contrasts with recent work [3], highlighting the utility (and perhaps fragility) of strong assumptions in improving reconstruction accuracy. Overall, our results suggest that, in scenarios with limited data, even well-motivated nonparametric priors may not provide sufficiently strong inductive biases to recover the corrected network.  [1] Oliver M Cliff et al. Unifying pairwise interactions in complex dynamics. Nature Computational Science (2023). [2] Charles Murphy et al. Duality between predictability and reconstructability in complex systems. Nature Communications (2024). [3] Tiago P Peixoto. Network reconstruction via the minimum description length principle. Physical Review X (2025). [4] Jia-Jie Qin and Gang Yan. High Reconstructability of Degree-Heterogeneous Networks. Physical Review Letters (2025).

nodes.title: The Hidden Organization of Online Communities: Reddit through the Lens of Stochastic Block Models and Neural Embeddings
nodes.abstract: Over the past two decades, online platforms have staged major and challenging events, including the spread of (mis)information, the amplification of unrepresentative voices, toxic public discourse, and coordinated collective action. The exposure and exchange networks they enable provide the structural conditions through which social friction and change emerge. Understanding these networks is therefore crucial for explaining how collective dynamics allow the propagation of news, behavior cascades, norms are enforced, and the coordination of collective action efforts. However, uncovering community structure from observed behavior depends critically on the computational tools employed, which can yield markedly different representations and thus constrain our theories of online collective dynamics. Here, we examine these differences by comparing higher-order communities identified using neural embeddings (NEs) and stochastic block models (SBMs) applied to 13 years of Reddit data from the Pushshift API, comprising approximately 5B comments and 3M users.  Our network-based approach reveals a more complex structure of political discussion on Reddit than that identified by previous work using NE representations. Using different SBM configurations, both fixing the number of communities to 30 and inferring it from the data, we find that the traditional “Political” cluster breaks down into 18 and 112 communities, respectively, while absorbing thousands of additional subreddits and thereby losing thematic coherence. Finally, applying a Hierarchical SBM (hSBM) uncovers the intrinsic nestedness of those communities of interest: it iteratively performs community inference and groups the communities present at lower levels into larger blocks as we move up in the hierarchy. This hierarchical approach uncovers self-organization across multiple resolution levels and identifies information corridors through which content moves as users cross-post across communities. Notably, it shows that some non-political subreddits occupy structural positions equivalent to political communities, highlighting organizational dynamics and generative mechanisms that are concealed in neural embedding representations.  Overall, we show that network-based methods reveal richer and more nuanced patterns of online political structure than previously identified, underscoring the importance of methodological choices in studies of social media networks.

nodes.title: A continuum limit for dense spatial networks
nodes.abstract: Many physical systems---such as optical waveguide lattices and dense neuronal or vascular networks---can be modeled by metric graphs, where slender ``wires'' (edges) support wave or diffusion equations subject to Kirchhoff boundary conditions at the nodes. We propose a continuum-limit framework which replaces edgewise differential equations with a coarse-grained partial differential equation (PDE) defined on the continuous space occupied by the network. The derivation naturally introduces an edge-conductivity tensor, an edge-capacity function, and a vertex number density to encode how each microscopic patch of the graph contributes to the macroscopic phenomena. These results have interesting similarities and differences with the Riemannian Laplace-Beltrami operator. We calculate all macroscopic parameters from first principles via a systematic discrete-to-continuous local homogenization, finding an anomalous effective embedding dimension resulting from a homogenized diffusivity. Numerical examples---including periodic lattices, random graphs, and quasiperiodic monotiles---demonstrate that each finite model converges to its corresponding PDE (posed on different manifolds like tori, disks, and spheres) in the limit of increasing vertex density. These high-density networks encode emergent material and functional properties. They reflect the ability of many real-world, space-filling networks to function simultaneously at multiple scales using the continuum as a feature.

nodes.title: Bridging Simple and Complex Contagions in Belief Dynamics: A Voter Model Extension
nodes.authors: Franco, João*; Robinette, Michael; Lipfert, Jackson; Lovato, Juniper; 	Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent
nodes.abstract: The standard voter model of belief dynamics [1] – how beliefs form and evolve – assumes that individuals consistently express their internal beliefs and that these beliefs can change from one period to another. However, social norms and conformity pressures often lead individuals to either withhold beliefs or express beliefs that are contrary to their own [2], while internally held beliefs may exhibit inertia due to psychological mechanisms such as social hysteresis.   Our extension of the voter model (Figure 1 (a)) distinguishes between expressed beliefs, following simple contagion dynamics, and internal beliefs, that update through complex contagion mechanisms. Individuals hold binary beliefs on a given topic (a = “Against,” f = “in Favour”) but may express the opposite (af = holding “Against” but expressing “In Favour”). Expressed beliefs update through simple contagion based on the beliefs expressed by neighbors in the previous period, with contagion strength mediated by alignment with one’s internal belief (c when aligned and c′ when misaligned), reflecting the potential burden of expressing beliefs contrary to those internally held. Internal beliefs update based on neighbors’ expressed beliefs but follow a q-voter model, capturing complex contagion dynamics and belief inertia.  Mean-field approximations reveal that when individuals face higher contagion pressure to express beliefs contrary to their internal ones (c′ > c), the symmetry typical of voter models breaks down, enabling minority beliefs to persist at higher proportions than their initial frequency in the long term (Figure 1 (b)). These effects are robust across a range of initial conditions. Future work will explore how network structure and belief withholding shape the interplay between simple and complex contagion in real-world contexts.

nodes.title: Spatial barriers reshape residents’ emotional experiences in urban China
nodes.abstract: Emotion is a fundamental human physiological process with well-documented associations across a spectrum of life outcomes—from economic success and career advancement to physical health and longevity. Urban spatial structures profoundly shape residents' emotional experiences in their daily movements. While behavioral barriers—visible obstacles like highways and rivers, as well as invisible ones rooted in socioeconomic segregation and uneven amenity access—have been documented[1], how they relate to emotional experiences remains under-explored. This study investigates whether behavioral barriers shape residents' emotional expressions and how economic conditions, infrastructure availability, and urban density moderate this relationship across cities of different scales and regions. We analyzed 75 million geo-tagged social media posts (Weibo, Chinese version of Twitter) from a cleaned dataset of over 2 million residents' check-ins and textual expressions at POI (Point of Interest) level across 60 Chinese cities. This integrated dataset uniquely enables examination of emotional patterns at both neighborhood and inter-regional scales. Our approach combines: (1) mobility neural embeddings to identify behavioral barriers from observed movement patterns, capturing both visible and invisible constraints that traditional geographic analysis misses[1]; (2) sentiment analysis to quantify emotional divergence; and (3) multivariate regression to examine barrier-emotion relationships across city types. Overall, this study integrates emotional wellbeing, place, and human mobility to reveal how spatial segregation shapes not just physical movement but also residents' psychological experiences[2] and wellbeing[3]. By providing fine-grained spatial evidence, we offer actionable insights for designing healthier urban environments.

nodes.title: Moral Signaling and Network Centrality in Online Opioid Recovery Communities
nodes.abstract: Online communities can play an important role in supporting recovery from opioid use disorder (OUD), particularly for people who lack access to supportive offline networks. While prior work has examined the content of recovery-related discourse, less is known about how moral language is structured within networks of interaction and how it relates to actor prominence and engagement. We study four large Reddit communities focused on OUD recovery and harm reduction, modeling each community as a directed interaction network in which nodes represent authors and edges represent reply relationships.  Using supervised large language models trained to detect ten moral foundations proposed in Moral Foundations Theory (Care, Harm, Fairness, Cheating, Authority, Subversion, Loyalty, Betrayal, Purity, and Degradation), we annotate over half a million posts and comments with moral attributes and enrich network edges with the moral content expressed in interactions. We analyze how moral signaling varies across communities with different recovery philosophies, how moral content relates to engagement, and how it is distributed among central versus peripheral actors.  Across all communities, moral language is common within interaction networks. Distinct moral profiles emerge at the community level, reflecting differing norms and treatment orientations. Central actors, those with both high indegree and outdegree, are are more likely to be morally expressive than less connected participants and engage not only in virtue-oriented discourse (e.g., Care), but also in vice-oriented moral language (Degradation, Cheating) often associated with stigma and shame. Rather than suppressing such discourse, central actors may express negative morality as part of community norm negotiation and as a way of engaging with shared experiences of stigmatizing and dehumanizing moral judgments.  These findings demonstrate how moral sentiment functions as a relational signal within recovery networks and highlight the value of integrating moral content with network analysis to understand community dynamics. More broadly, the work illustrates how moral signaling and network position jointly shape participation and engagement in online support communities.

nodes.title: GRNage: An epigenetic clock based on gene regulatory network features
nodes.authors: Shutta, Katherine*; Wu, Helena; DeMeo, Dawn; Quackenbush, John
nodes.abstract: Epigenetic clocks are models that measure biological aging. The divergence of biological age from chronological age is a hallmark of many complex diseases, including cancer. Epigenetic clocks are typically trained to select a set of DNA methylation loci (CpGs) that are predictive of chronological age or other aging phenotypes in a healthy cohort. A limitation of this approach is the lack of interpretability of the selected model components.  Sets of CpGs that are predictive of the training outcome may not directly participate in underlying biological mechanisms that are drivers of aging or disease progression.  Gene regulatory networks (GRNs) are bipartite graphs connecting transcription factors (TFs) to the genes that they regulate. GRNs encode functional regulatory processes within a network structure. We hypothesized that an aging clock based on GRN network features could provide interpretable functional insight into dysregulated biological aging in cancer. To this end, we introduce GRNage, the first gene regulatory network-based aging clock. GRNage models biological age as a linear combination of GRN properties (TF out-degree; target gene in-degree). The input to GRNage is a GRN estimated with the PANDA algorithm ; the output is an estimated biological age (see Figure).  GRNage is trained to select a set of TFs and genes whose degrees are predictive of epigenetic age acceleration as estimated by the Horvath epigenetic clock As gene regulatory processes have been demonstrated to be highly tissue-specific  , GRNage is also tissue-specific. Here, we present a skin-based GRNage clock trained in 451 subcutaneous melanoma (SKCM) samples from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA).  We tested the relevance of SKCM GRNage in biological context by calculating GRNage of four different tumor metastasis classifications.  In the training dataset, the mean GRNage was significantly higher in the regional metastases than the primary tumor (p=0.048 for regional cutaneous/subcutaneous metastases vs. primary, p=0.017 for lymph node metastases vs. primary), while no significant difference was observed between GRNage of the primary tumor and the distant metastasis (p=0.82 for distant vs. primary). In the testing dataset, this pattern was preserved between the primary tumor and regional cutaneous/subcutaneous metastases (p=0.046) GRNage represents a new network-based paradigm in aging research: patterns of epigenetic age can be captured by changes in gene regulatory networks. Importantly, GRNage does not require DNA methylation data, but can be built using only transcriptomic data, which are more widely available. Thus, GRNage opens potential for widespread analysis of epigenetic aging patterns in a range of previously published transcriptomic datasets. Future analyses of the biological context of the features driving GRNage may reveal novel changes in gene regulation associated with accelerated aging in cancer and complex diseases.

nodes.title: A Network of Warring States: Antagonism and the Emergence of Stability in Medieval Japan
nodes.authors: Ghasemian, Amir*; Shirado, Hirokazu; Christakis, Nicholas
nodes.abstract: Negative relationships significantly influence the structure and dynamics of social networks, even possibly serving a role in stabilizing an otherwise unstable system across time. These connections make a significant contribution to conflict management, facilitate the diffusion of information, and alleviate ideological polarization; however, their role in the stabilization of human social networks remains somewhat ambiguous. We explore this idea by analyzing unique historical data from the Warring States period of medieval Japan, where 2,222 clans engaged in 2,889 battles over 133 years. Our network analysis reveals a temporal shift in the topological location of battles and warring actors. Specifically, we delineate a fragmentation phase (1467–1566), characterized by instability and decentralized conflict, followed by a unification phase (1566–1600), distinguished by centralized conflict. By extending classical balance theory to state-like actors, these findings provide insights into the role of negative ties in long-term social destabilization and stabilization cycles.

nodes.title: The Triple Lock: Geometric Obstructions to Partial Information Decomposition
nodes.abstract: Synergy—information emergent in the whole but invisible to the parts—is a defining signature of complex networks. While Partial Information Decomposition (PID) [1] formalizes this structure via a redundancy lattice, the field remains deadlocked by the lack of a canonical measure. A promising recent inquiry attempts to resolve this ambiguity through Riemannian Information Geometry, defining redundancy as the orthogonal projection of a joint distribution onto a split-independence submanifold [2, 4].  In this work, we prove a No-Go Theorem for Riemannian PID. We demonstrate that the pure Riemannian geometry of the probability simplex is mathematically insufficient to support a consistent decomposition. We identify three irreducible structural obstructions, termed the "Triple Lock":  Rank-Width Mismatch: The tangent space spanned by marginal constraints scales linearly with system size n, whereas the PID lattice scales super-exponentially (Dedekind numbers). We deduce that for n ≥ 3, the tangent bundle lacks sufficient rank to linearly encode the full combinatorial complexity of higher-order redundancies, creating an irreducible bottleneck for purely vector-based decompositions.  The Work Obstruction (Holonomy): Information measures must be state functions (path-independent). However, the non-vanishing curvature of the Fisher metric implies non-trivial holonomy. We show that integrating local informational updates (gradients of redundancy) is path-dependent; the computed value varies with the trajectory through distribution space, rendering the decomposition ill-defined without an auxiliary flat connection.  Möbius Sign Failure: Even effectively freezing the path, geometric projections (m-projections) onto split-independence submanifolds do not guarantee order-preservation on the lattice. This geometric misalignment can structurally force the creation of negative atoms (negative synergy) upon Möbius inversion, putting the Riemannian formulation in direct conflict with the non-negativity axiom of information.  These findings compel a fundamental re-evaluation of how we measure informational decompositional structures. For network scientists seeking to disentangle synergy from redundancy. Whether in neural connectomes or social hypergraphs, our results demonstrate that standard metric-based approaches are structurally flawed. We argue that a rigorous calculus of interaction must abandon purely Riemannian methods in favor of the dually flat affine geometry of the exponential family [3], or pivot to purely combinatorial foundations to consistently capture the rich lattice structure of multivariate dependencies.  References: [1] P. L. Williams and R. D. Beer, Nonnegative decomposition of multivariate information, arXiv:1004.2515 (2010). [2] X. Niu and C. J. Quinn, A Measure of Synergy, Redundancy, and Unique Information Using Information Geometry, Proc. IEEE ISIT (2019). [3] S. Amari, Information Geometry and Its Applications, Springer (2016). [4] J. W. Kay, A Partial Information Decomposition for Multivariate Gaussian Systems, Entropy 26(7) (2024).

nodes.title: How Network Topology Links to Growth and Stagnation in Online Hate Communities
nodes.authors: Nefriana, Rr.*; Yan, Muheng; Hwa, Rebecca; Lin, Yu-Ru
nodes.abstract: While online hate speech is frequently analyzed through content lenses, the structural architecture of extremist networks remains under-researched. This study investigates how these network properties link to future engagement patterns across distinct hate ideologies related to the Israel-Palestine conflict. We analyzed a ten year longitudinal dataset of Facebook activity by hate groups. Monthly networks were constructed where nodes represent unique groups and edges represent shared users (those posting in multiple groups). We employed negative binomial regression modeling to determine how group degree (inter-group connectivity), homophily index (ideological similarity), and the proportion of multi-group users predict next month engagement (likes, comments, and shares). Regression results demonstrate that group degree significantly and positively predicts future engagement for Anti-Semitic and Other-Hate groups, but remains non-significant for Islamophobic groups. Homophily negatively predicts engagement in Anti-Semitic groups, suggesting that insularity leads to activity stagnation. This provides a potential explanation for the distinct structural profiles revealed in our baseline analysis: while Islamophobic groups are highly homophilic, Anti-Semitic groups maintain a balanced structure, a configuration that mitigates the engagement penalties associated with high insularity. A high proportion of posts from multi-group users is associated with lower subsequent engagement across all categories, with the pattern most pronounced in Islamophobic silos. These findings suggest hate groups exhibit distinct survival patterns, calling for tailored interventions. Islamophobic groups are self-sustaining yet fragile to internal dilution; disrupting homophilic echoes, such as via nudges toward alternative perspectives, may be effective. Anti-Semitic groups are more networked and less insular, with a risk of backfire effects from hostile interactions; targeting bridging nodes might help contain their spread, though future research is needed to confirm this.

nodes.title: Using a Heterogeneous Graph Transformer to repurpose drugs for the treatment of Nipah virus infectious disease
nodes.abstract: Nipah virus (NiV) is a bat-borne pathogen whose infection in humans causes disease with a very high observed mortality rate, with the NiV-M lineage (identified in Malaysia) having a case fatality of 39% while NiV-B lineage (identified in Bangladesh and India) reporting a much higher mortality range of 60% to 100% in different outbreaks. Since the identification of the first cases of NiV infection in humans in Malaysia in 1998, several outbreaks have occurred in South East Asia. Due to its high mortality rate and lack of effective therapies or vaccines, it is currently listed as a select biological agent by both the US Dept. of Health and Human Services and US Dept. of Agriculture. Therefore, it is crucial to develop treatments for care of infectious cases in order to reduce the health impacts and public health concerns. In this work we leverage a heterogeneous graph transformer model in order to repurpose drugs for the treatment of NiV infectious disease. By using the model in a heterogeneous Knowledge Graph (KG) we hope to harness different varied forms of interactions between different biological entities - such as interactions between drugs and gene/proteins, diseases and gene/proteins, diseases and effect/phenotype (symptoms), drugs and effect/phenotype, etc - in order to predict novel indications between already existing, approved drugs and the disease from NiV infection. This KG based drug repurposing capitalizes on the known heterogeneous interactions between relevant biological entities to overcome the time and costs associated with drug development. Our goal is the attention mechanism will extrapolate from newly-observed genes affected by the NiV infectious disease to perform link-prediction on the KG, by learning from the existing topology of relations in the KG and how these predict existing indications. Previous similar methods for drug repurposing by link-prediction on biological networks mostly relied on simplified homogeneous networks, e.g. similarity-matrix based collapses of heterogeneous data into homogeneous versions, thus missing some information. By utilizing an attention mechanism that is able to process a graph with different types of interactions, and between different types of nodes, our link-prediction model exploits the full breath of available information.

nodes.title: Posterior Inference of Network Growth Mechanism Mixtures
nodes.abstract: Real-world networks are often shaped by a mixture of multiple growth mechanisms, yet inference is typically performed from a single observed snapshot with no access to growth trajectories. We address this challenge by formulating posterior inference over mixtures of network growth mechanisms directly from a network snapshot, treating the underlying growth process as latent. By marginalizing over possible growth explanations, we infer a posterior distribution over mechanism mixtures rather than selecting a single best-fitting model.  We visualize this posterior as an energy landscape over the simplex of mixture weights, revealing how uncertainty and trade-offs between mechanisms depend on the underlying generative process. Using simulations, we show that the inferred posterior geometry captures transitions between mixed and near-pure generative regimes. Even without edge-formation information, the snapshot-based posterior preserves the overall structure of the baseline landscape. These results suggest that posterior geometry provides a way to analyze the joint operations of different mechanisms and shed light on the underlying generative process from limited network observations.

nodes.title: Figures as Interfaces: Toward LLM-Native Artifacts for Scientific Discovery
nodes.authors: Wang, Yifang*; Cao, Nan; Wang, Dashun
nodes.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are transforming scientific workflows, not only through their generative capabilities but also through their emerging ability to use tools, reason about data, and coordinate complex analytical tasks.  Yet in most human-AI collaborations, the primary outputs--figures--are still treated as static visual summaries: once rendered, they are handled by both humans and multimodal LLMs as images to be re-interpreted from pixels or captions.  The emergent capabilities of LLMs open an opportunity to fundamentally rethink this paradigm.  In this paper, we introduce the concept of LLM-native figures: data-driven artifacts that are simultaneously human-legible and machine-addressable.  Unlike traditional plots, each artifact embeds complete provenance: the data subset, analytical operations and code, and visualization specification used to generate it.  As a result, an LLM can ``see through'' the figure--tracing selections back to their sources, generating code to extend analyses, and orchestrating new visualizations through natural-language instructions or direct manipulation.  We implement this concept through a hybrid language–visual interface that integrates LLM agents with a bidirectional mapping between figures and underlying data.  Using the science-of-science domain as a testbed, we demonstrate that LLM-native figures can accelerate discovery, improve reproducibility, and make reasoning transparent across agents and users.  More broadly, this work establishes a general framework for embedding provenance, interactivity, and explainability into the artifacts of modern research, redefining the figure not as an end product, but as an interface for discovery.

nodes.title: Estimating network clustering with a latent space model from Respondent-Driven Sampling data
nodes.authors: Jiang, Yun*; Gile, Krista; Nyakowa, Mercy; Fedha, Daniel; Manley, Hannah; Riback, Lindsey; Akiyama, Matthew
nodes.abstract: The social network structure of hidden and hard-to-reach populations can have important implications for understanding risk and planning interventions in epidemiology and public health. Collecting full or even moderately dense network data from such populations is often infeasible due to the absence of a sampling frame, privacy concerns, and resource constraints. Respondent-Driven Sampling (RDS) is a popular chain-referral sampling method designed for such areas, which relies on a dual-incentive strategy, providing compensation to both participants and recruiters for successful referrals. Despite its broad application, conventional RDS primarily captures partially observed recruitment trees and offers limited insight into the underlying network structures. To address this challenge, we first incorporated a recently developed token-based strategy which supplements limited recruitment coupon distribution with token distribution, creating richer data on social network structure (see Figure 1). Inspired by the work using aggregated relational data (ARD), with information from RDS data and token ties, we propose a latent space model to effectively describe the network structure. The model is mathematically determined through a series of equations derived from the method of moment estimators. The performance of model construction from RDS and token data is verified through simulation. The developed model is also employed to estimate unobserved network structures and obtain network statistics from RDS data. Systematical assessments on the effectiveness of our model are presented, especially in describing network clustering, which has high practical relevance but is impossible to infer from standard RDS data.

nodes.title: Latent Variable Models for Estimating Feature Interaction Networks
nodes.authors: Nemati, Mohammadreza; Huang, Zhipeng; Xu, Kevin*
nodes.abstract: Many types of complex systems, particularly biological systems, involve relationships between entities that are often not directly observed. These relationships can be modeled by networks where nodes denote entities of interest and edges are constructed by indirect means, usually by estimating them from non-relational data. One common type of estimated network is a correlation network, where each node has a vector of measurements or features, and an edge between two nodes is constructed by taking the correlation coefficient between the feature vectors for the two nodes.  We consider another type of estimated network, which we call a feature interaction network (FIN). A FIN represents the set of all pairwise interaction coefficients in a generalized linear model (GLM) with pairwise interaction terms between all pairs of the p features. The interaction coefficients can be arranged in a p × p interaction coefficient matrix Θ, where entry θ_jk denotes the coefficient for the interaction term x_j x_k. This matrix can be viewed as a weighted and signed adjacency matrix for the estimated network, where the nodes correspond to the features.  Treating feature interactions as a network allows us to estimate different interaction coefficients θ_jk jointly by considering the estimates of the other interaction coefficients θ_j′k′ , which may be related. Conventional regularization methods such as the lasso (ℓ1 penalty) or elastic net (combined ℓ2, ℓ1 penalty), and even methods specifically targeted to interaction terms such as the hierarchical lasso, do not consider the dependencies between edges (interaction terms) because they do not consider the network structure.  We propose a structured regularization approach for estimating the interaction coefficients using an approximate low-dimensional structure in addition to conventional regularization techniques such as the elastic net. We call our proposed approach LIT-LVM, denoting linear and interaction terms with a latent variable model and can be used with many types of latent structures including low rank and latent distance models.  Our proposed structured regularization for the interaction coefficients offers two main benefits. First, it improves estimation accuracy for the interaction coefficients, leading to better predictive accuracy, especially as p grows relative to the number of samples n, which we demonstrate in simulation experiments and across many real datasets. Second, it provides low-dimensional latent representations of the features that are useful for visualizing and analyzing their relationships, which we demonstrate in an application for modeling donor-recipient compatibility in kidney transplantation.

nodes.title: Omnivory decouples optimal predator fitness from network robustness
nodes.abstract: The principle of maximum output power is as old as the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model. This principle posits that under competition, those system configurations are most likely to prevail that can capture and transform usable energy at higher rates. While Lotka–Volterra dynamics and species abundances are now standard tools in ecology, explicitly using energy fluxes as the primary currency in ecological models has received much less attention. Here, we revisit predator–prey systems from a network perspective, using a Lotka–Volterra–type model formulated in terms of energy fluxes between interacting species. We define the output power of a food web as the total energy flux through one or more focal top predators, and study how output power depends on the encounter rates (interaction strengths) along the network’s feeding links. For a broad class of network structures without omnivory, we find that encounter rates that maximize output power also maximize feasibility, understood as the fraction of intrinsic growth-rate vectors that yield positive, coexistence equilibria. In these cases, optimal predator fitness and network robustness converge — changing the interaction strength(s) toward the maximum-power state simultaneously expands the feasibility domain. This qualitative picture is reversed when the network exhibits omnivory, i.e. when at least one predator feeds on both a basal and a non-basal species. Omnivory motifs appear as feed-forward loops in ecological networks [2]. For such motifs, the encounter rates that maximize output power lie near geometric bottlenecks or boundaries of the feasibility domain, so that maximizing predator power systematically reduces feasibility. This result holds even when omnivory is realized through species other than the focal top predator. Our analysis shows that omnivory introduces a structural tension between individual-level optimization (maximum output power of predators) and network-level robustness (feasibility), leading to divergent optimal outcomes at the scales of species and ecological networks.

nodes.title: Networked Dynamics of Toxicity in Reddit Gaming Communities
nodes.authors: Smith, Serafina; David, Glen; Brunn, Lizzy; Nguyen, Vy; Choi, Eun Cheol*; Luceri, Luca
nodes.abstract:
Online gaming communities are among the most active and socially complex online spaces, yet they remain persistently affected by toxic behavior such as harassment, aggression, and abusive language, which harms user well-being and discourages participation [1].
