While prior work has examined toxicity at the content level, across game genres, or through network dynamics [1,2,3], it remains unclear how community type in terms of game genre and developer scale and user interaction structure jointly shape the prevalence, diffusion, and concentration of toxicity.  We present a comparative, network-based analysis of toxicity across Reddit gaming communities using a 2 × 2 framework that crosses game genre (MMORPG vs. Soulslike) with developer scale (AAA/AA vs. indie). We analyze 550,641 posts and comments from nine gaming subreddits, including a general gaming baseline (r/gaming). Each post and comment is scored using two independent automated toxicity detection systems (OpenAI’s omni-moderation model and Detoxify). Using these scores, we construct thread-level reply trees and directed user-reply networks to examine toxicity across multiple levels of social organization.  	extbf{At the community level}, we compare average toxicity prevalence, severity, and category composition across the 2 × 2 design. Soulslike communities and AAA/AA titles exhibit modestly higher average toxicity than MMORPG and indie communities, driven primarily by conflict-oriented language such as insults, obscenity, and violence-related terms [2]. 	extbf{At the thread level}, we observe robust local contagion across all communities: replies to toxic comments are consistently more toxic, and more toxic threads tend to be larger, deeper, and more structurally viral (Figure 1, left). These patterns align with prior work on toxicity contagion and conversational cascades in online discussion networks [3]. We find no systematic differences in these dynamics across genre or developer scale, indicating that toxicity propagation reflects general properties of online conversational structure rather than community-specific cultures or norms. 	extbf{At the user level}, toxicity is both assortative and structurally concentrated (Figure 1, right). Users with similar toxicity levels preferentially interact, and more central users both produce and receive more toxicity, with network centrality differentiating exposure from expression.  Taken together, these findings show that toxicity in gaming communities is shaped less by genre or developer scale alone and more by interaction structure and network position. Rather than treating specific communities or genres as inherently toxic, the results highlight the importance of network-aware moderation strategies that focus on early conversational contexts and structurally influential users. More broadly, this study provides a comparative, network-based benchmark for understanding harmful behavior in large-scale online interaction systems.   igskip {\small 
oindent[1] Rahul, Kajla, H., Hooda, J., \& Saini, G. (2020, May). Classification of online toxic comments using machine learning algorithms. In 2020 4th international conference on intelligent computing and control systems (ICICCS) (pp. 1119-1123). IEEE. \ 
oindent[2] Dreier, L., \& Pirker, J. (2023, August). Toxicity in twitch live stream chats: Towards understanding the impact of gender, size of community and game genre. In 2023 IEEE Conference on Games (CoG) (pp. 1-4). IEEE. \ 
oindent[3] Shankaran, V., \& Sharma, R. (2024). Analyzing toxicity in deep conversations: A reddit case study. arXiv preprint arXiv:2404.07879. }

nodes.title: The Global Network of Resource Flows from Lithosphere to Technosphere
nodes.authors: Kaye, Maxwell*; Galbraith, Eric; Al Faisal, Abduallah
nodes.abstract: Anthropogenic material fluxes now rival natural geological processes yet are rarely studied within the Earth system framework. Here we present a new approach, global ‘technogeochemical’ flows, and apply it to the roughly 2 Gt/y anthropogenic flows of iron and other critical materials. Using the SESAME-Human Earth Atlas [1], we synthesize disparate data sources to show how critical minerals flow from a few extraction sites, via a few refining plants, to widely distributed in-use stocks. We apply our spatiotemporal approach to other critical materials including copper, lithium, aluminum, lithium, phosphate, and even food production to understand how industrial economic processes are physically embedded in the Earth system.  [1]     Faisal, A. A., Kaye, M., Ahmed, M., Galbraith, E. D. (2025). The SESAME Human-Earth Atlas. Scientific Data, 12(1), 775.

nodes.title: Beyond the CEO Life Cycle: The Influences of Top Management Team and Middle Man- agers from a Faultline Perspective
nodes.authors: Yang, Biying*; Tsai, Chou-Yu; Eckardt, Rory; Dionne, Shelley; Weng, David
nodes.abstract: CEOs’ strategic paradigms are crucial in shaping organizational performance by influencing direction, resource alloca- tion, and competitive positioning. The CEO life cycle framework introduces a longitudinal lens by suggesting that CEOs’ strategic paradigms generally follow an exploration-exploitation-obsolescence pattern, resulting in an inverted U-shaped performance trajectory. This reflects initial performance improvement followed by stagnation and ultimate decline [1]. We extend this framework beyond the sole focus on CEOs’ influence to examine the impacts of faultlines formed between CEOs and top management team (TMT) members, as well as between CEOs and middle managers (MMs), given the critical roles these agents play in strategic formulation and implementation. Drawing on Campbell et al.’s (2023) classification, we conceptualize faultlines based on observable and unobservable attributes shared by CEO and other strategic agents. We propose that while strong faultlines of observable attributes strengthen performance gains during the exploration phase, strong faultlines of unobservable attributes sustain high performance for a longer time during exploitation and attenuate the decline associated with obsolescence. We use a multiplex network approach [2] to model the strength of these faultlines and conduct random coefficient multilevel growth modeling, supplemented by tangent slope analysis, to specify performance trajectory and its changing rates. Hypotheses are tested using longitudinal data gathered from the U.S. higher education context between 2000 to 2022, with university presidents proxying as CEOs, provosts as the TMT members, and deans as MMs. The sample includes 28 presidents, 63 provosts, and 574 deans, yielding 293 year-performance pairs. Performance is assessed across three dimensions: total revenues (financial), operating efficiency (operational), and research salary (innovation). The results provide a more nuanced perspective on the myth of CEO “solo act” performance. Although total revenues show insignificant curvilinear or moderating effects, operating efficiency is significantly enhanced by stronger faultlines of CEO-TMT and CEO-MM unobservable attribute. Specifically, under stronger unobservable attribute faultlines, efficiency increases at a diminishing rate during the initial years of CEO life cycle; conversely, weaker faultlines of unobservable attribute lead performance to enter a plateau stage directly without a salient initial increase. For research salary, this innovation-related dimension increases more rapidly when CEO-MM observable attribute faultline is weaker, suggesting that diversity among MMs may drive early-stage research growth. These findings demonstrate that organizational per- formance is a multidimensional and collective outcome shaped by temporal dynamics, the joint effects of strategic agents at different hierarchical levels, and perceptual changes among those strategic agents.

nodes.title: Tabular Embedding: A Network Representation for High-Performance Computing Applications
nodes.authors: Hasanzadeh Fard, Sanaz*; Dolson, Emily; Andres Moreno, Matthew; Zaman, Luis
nodes.abstract: Recently, multiple computational hardware platforms, such as the Cerebras Wafer Scale Engine (WSE), have combined a vast number of lower-powered processors to achieve overall high throughput. These platforms offer the potential to dramatically scale up applications that are conducive to being run on them. Here, we propose that bipartite network modeling is such an application and present a technique (tabular embedding) for translating it to computational substrates such as the WSE. We present an application in the context of epidemiology, but see no reason that our technique should not generalize. The primary obstacle to be overcome is enabling efficient communication across the WSE's grid of processors.  We achieve an efficient solution to this problem by conceptualizing our epidemiological network as containing two types of nodes: residences and institutions (i.e. school or work). Edges between these nodes indicate the presence of people who live at a given residence and regularly attend a given institution, enabling disease transmission between the two sites. We then develop a tabular layout in which some table cells (which correspond to processors) correspond to houses, some to institutions, and others to edges between houses and institutions (i.e. people). We present an efficient algorithm for modeling disease transmission along networks represented by these layouts.

nodes.title: Characterizing academic collaborations by prestige and discipline composition
nodes.authors: Aoki-Sherwood, Benyoshi; Barnes, Kate; Chavez-Ruelas, Carolina*
nodes.abstract: Social and systemic factors have been shown to structure how scientists collaborate with each other. As is the case with sociodemographic homophily in collaboration [1], many of these factors reflect existing social inequalities that operate in the scientific ecosystem and reinforce existing hierarchies [2]. For instance, controlling for quality, a scientific idea spreads at a higher rate if it originates from a higher prestige institution than from a lower prestige institution [3]. However, academic collaborations are not restricted to the same institutional prestige or discipline, and it remains unclear the degree to which this occurs in the system. In this project, we aim to characterize prestige homophily and cross-disciplinary collaboration rates across five fields to understand how these boundaries structure academic co-authorship.  We linked faculty profiles in a census of U.S. faculty from the Academic Analytics Research Center (AARC) to publication data from OpenAlex and applied the SpringRank network embedding algorithm to generate institutional prestige scores based on faculty placement patterns [4]. From these data, we created undirected co-authorship multigraphs in which nodes are faculty from the AARC census, and an edge connects two individuals for each of their co-authored publications. We compared the within-field prestige homophily for the fields of computer science, mathematics, physics, sociology, and philosophy using a measure of network assortativity for continuous node features [5]. We chose these fields because they represent diverse collaboration paradigms and scholastic traditions. We excluded collaborations within the same institution because they likely reflect proximity rather than prestige homophily.  The five fields exhibit substantial variation in the prestige homophily of within-field collaborations, ranging from 0.14 in physics to 0.31 in math (Fig. 1). Similarly, rates of interdisciplinary collaboration also vary widely between fields. Almost half of co-authorship pairs involving faculty in philosophy are interdisciplinary. For sociology, this fraction falls to 14% (Fig. 1). Mathematics has both the highest prestige homophily and the second-highest interdisciplinary collaboration rate while philosophy has the highest interdisciplinary collaboration rate but the second-lowest prestige homophily, suggesting a complex relationship between these two lenses on scientific collaboration. Our future work will measure these quantities across a more complete set of fields and examine possible covariates for both prestige homophily and interdisciplinary collaboration. For example, we will test whether prestige homophily is more or less prevalent in interdisciplinary collaborations, clarifying the complex way in which institutional prestige influences who collaborates with whom.

nodes.title: A nonparametric model for multiple categorical node features with arbitrary correlations
nodes.abstract: Many observable complex networks produce data that is both compositional (properties of nodes) and relational (network edges). However, the development of flexible network models that effectively use both of these data sources simultaneously remains a challenge. The existing standard for nonparametric network inference with metadata involves representing metadata as additional layers of a multilayered stochastic block model (SBM). When the node features are correlated, this approach leads to erroneous structure in the inferred node partition, as well as simultaneous overfitting and underfitting. After addressing the limitations of a "naive" solution to the correlated layer problem based on combining layers, we introduce a microcanonical model that avoids these issues, providing a method that is more suitable for the inferential task at hand while achieving improved data compression. Using synthetic and empirical data, we demonstrate how our model can be used alongside existing community detection methods for joint inference of latent node features, without a priori assumptions about the correlation between these data sources.

nodes.title: Percolation in Growing Stochastic Block Models
nodes.authors: Pieleanu, Luca; Sabhahit, Narayan*; Laber, Moritz; Krioukov, Dmitri
nodes.abstract:
The stochastic block model (SBM) [1,2] is a paradigmatic random graph model used extensively in Network Science for inference and community detection, as it naturally accommodates community structure. Despite its broad applicability, some of its most fundamental properties remain poorly understood: rigorous results for the percolation transition (the emergence of the giant connected component (GCC)) are only known in a limited set of special cases, all involving a fixed number of communities [3,4]. Some important regimes---most notably with a growing number of blocks---have remained largely unexplored.  Here, we provide a thorough theoretical analysis of a general class of SBMs, including the special case of the sparse SBM with a growing number of communities. This case is particularly significant since these models are very close to soft random geometric graphs [5], whose percolation properties have so far been analytically intractable [6]. We consider an SBM with $N$ vertices, partitioned into $B_{N}$ blocks of sizes $\{n_{i}\}_{i=1}^{B_{N}}$ such that $\sum_{i=1}^{B_{N}} n_i= N$, where $p_{ij}$ denotes the connection probability between nodes belonging to communities $i$ and $j$. We derive self-consistent equations governing the emergence of the GCC in this model. The critical point is characterized by $\lambda_{\max}\left( oldsymbol{M}ight) = 1$, where $ oldsymbol{M} = \{M_{ij}\}_{i,j=1}^{N}$ with $M_{ij} = n_{j}p_{ij}$ and $\lambda_{\max}(\cdot)$ denotes the absolute value of the largest eigenvalue of the matrix. In the special case where $p_{ij} = q/N$ for $i 
eq j$ and $p$ for $i=j$ (with $p,q$ constants) and all the communities are of the same size $s,$ with the number of communities growing as $B_{N} = \lfloor N/s floor$, the percolation transition occurs at $\lambda_{\max}\left( oldsymbol{M}ight) =  ar{k} = (s-1)p +q =1$, where $ ar{k}$ is the average degree of the model. It is noteworthy, however, that the percolation threshold does not generally occur at average degree $1$, contrary to common belief.    igskip {\small

nodes.title: Predicting Scientific Subfield Adoption in Collaboration Networks: The Role of Exposure Timing and Collaborator Composition
nodes.abstract: Understanding how collaboration networks are associated with researchers’ entry into new scientific subfields has implications for research policy and scientific innovation. While diffusion theories often assume that greater cumulative exposure to adopting peers increases adoption likelihood [1], empirical evidence remains mixed, particularly when individuals are embedded in environments composed of collaborators at different stages of subfield engagement [2]. We investigate how exposure timing, accumulation, and composition of collaboration networks jointly predict adoption across five scientific fields.  Using longitudinal co-authorship networks from SciSciNet [3], we analyze five subfields that experienced substantial growth between 2015-2019: Computer Engineering, Physical Geography, Demographic Economics, Evolutionary Biology, and Combinatorial Chemistry. We define three researcher roles based on adoption: Alices, recent adopters who first published during the growth period; Bobs, at-risk researchers who collaborated with Alices but have not yet adopted; and Charlies, established researchers who entered the subfield before 2015. For each Bob, we track yearly changes in their local collaboration neighborhood and predict whether they publish in the subfield in a given year. For each Bob–year observation, we construct network-derived features capturing (i) the timing of first exposure to Alice, (ii) cumulative and newly accruing exposure to Alice collaborators, and (iii) exposure to Charlie collaborators, reflecting embeddedness in an established collaborator environment. Using gradient-boosted decision tree models with an expanding-window temporal cross-validation approach, we evaluate predictive performance using the AUC-ROC metric and assess interpretability through SHAP analyses and grouped ablations.  Across all fields, early exposure to recent adopters (Alices) is positively associated with adoption, while the incremental effect of additional exposure diminishes with prolonged exposure. In contrast, exposure to established collaborators (Charlies) is consistently negatively associated with adoption. Ablation tests support this asymmetry: removing exposure to Charlies produces the largest drop in predictive performance across the fields (mean ΔAUC ≈ −0.08 to −0.10), followed by removal of the timing of first-exposure to Alices (mean ΔAUC ≈ −0.06), while cumulative exposure contributes little once timing and neighborhood composition are accounted for.  Together, these findings challenge cumulative diffusion models that focus solely on exposure accumulation. Instead, subfield adoption in scientific collaboration networks depends on when exposure occurs and the structural composition of collaboration networks. By demonstrating the cross-subfield invariance of these temporal and inhibitory network effects, our work highlights the need for diffusion models that incorporate network roles, timing, and competing social signals in the study of scientific adoption.  [1] Centola, D. The Spread of Behavior in an Online Social Network Experiment. Science 329, 1194–1197 (2010). [2] Goel, S., Watts, D. J. \& Goldstein, D. G. The structure of online diffusion networks. in Proceedings of the 13th ACM Conference on Electronic Commerce 623–638 (ACM, Valencia Spain, 2012). [3] Lin, Z., Yin, Y., Liu, L. \& Wang, D. SciSciNet: A large-scale open data lake for the science of science research. Sci Data 10, 1–22 (2023).

nodes.title: Coordination through Reply Chains in Information Operation Campaigns
nodes.authors: Narain, Stuti*; Narain, Shradha; Kaushal, Rishabh; Iamnitchi, Adriana
nodes.abstract: Social media platforms are exploited in a well calibrated, orchestrated manner by malicious coordinated groups [1], referred to as Information Operations (IOs) or Coordinated Campaigns (CC). The objectives are to garner support or to influence people through misleading information.  IOs pose serious threats to organic dissemination of information. Particularly during election times, IOs can potentially disrupt free and fair democratic processes.   In this work we focus on how malicious actors try to assert influence artificially through replies to the original posts. We present a network-based framework that investigates reply posts involved in IOs. Specifically, we study coordinated replies on social media platforms using IO datasets which comprise 26 IO campaigns consisting of IO posts from different state actors (eg. Russia, China, etc.) identified by platform through their transparency website and control posts, curated by Seckin et al. [2]) .   We restrict ourselves to reply posts and construct a reply graph for each campaign. Each reply post is categorized as belonging to IO campaign or control. Due to space limitations, we focus on two contrasting campaigns [2] from China (large reply network) and Armenia (small reply network). Further, we consider only those replies whose corresponding original posts are present in the dataset. Reply networks are trees rooted in the original posts, where the rest of the nodes are replies to the original posts or to other replies present either in star or chain formation as depicted in Fig~\ref{fig:chain_patterns}. Table~\ref{tbl:stats} shows reply graph statistics with respect to chains, the average delay (in minutes) between original post and direct reply as well as the last reply, and distinct user accounts engaged in these interactions. In the IOs from China, 33\% of chains (3,750 out of 11,204) have IO replies, as compared to Armenia with only 5\% (16 out of 307). The Chinese campaign has 39 mixed reply chains containing both IO and control replies while no such mixed chains were found for Armenia. IO reply chains are shorter, both on average and in terms of maximum chain length, than control reply chains in both campaigns.   In addition, IO reply chains have much higher out-degree and lower in-degree when compared with control reply chains. Temporally, we measure average delay (in terms of minutes) of direct reply and last reply to the original post. IO replies in the Chinese campaign are exceedingly marked with large delays when compared to control replies, a trend which is reversed in Armenian campaign.  Lastly, the Chinese campaigns employ more distinct user accounts than the Armenian campaigns. To conclude, this study investigates signatures in foreign information campaigns on social media that can be shown using reply chains. Such signatures could be used for detecting campaigns in real time.   \label{tbl:stats} \begin{tabular}{cccccc} \hline Metric & \multicolumn{3}{c}{China} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{Armenia} \\ \hline Nodes & \multicolumn{3}{c}{26,285}  & \multicolumn{2}{c}{739} \\ Edges & \multicolumn{3}{c}{17,137} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{463} \\ \hline & IO & Ctrl & Mix & IO & Ctrl \\ \hline Number of Chains & 3750 & 7415 & 39 & 16 & 291\\ Avg Chain Length & 2.42 & 2.68 & 4.08 & 2 & 2.66 \\ Min Chain Length & 2 & 2 & 3 & 2 & 2 \\ Max Chain Length & 17 & 99 & 14 & 2 & 21 \\ Max Out-Degree & 151 & 84 & 2 & 14 & 2 \\ Avg Out-Degree & 3.61 & 2.34 & 0.84 & 6.19 & 0.68 \\ Avg In-Degree & 0.59 & 0.63 & 0.75 & 0.50 & 0.62 \\ \hline Avg Delay Direct Reply & 2094 & 25 & - & 25 & 50 \\ Avg Delay Last Reply & 2151 & 33 & 229 & 25 & 61 \\ \hline Max Distinct Accounts & 6 & 3 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ Avg Distinct Accounts & 1.46 & 1.1 & 2 & 1.06 & 1.04 \\ \hline \end{tabular} \end{wraptable}

nodes.title: From Topological to Functional Resilience in Interdependent Networks
nodes.authors: Ahuja, Akanksha *; Herzberg, Sam N.; Rafel, Albert; Lord, Andrew; Zapata, Alejandra B.; Savory, Seb J.
nodes.abstract: For decades, network science has treated structural percolation as the foundational heuristic for resilience [1,2,3]. While mathematically elegant, this approach is insufficient for critical infrastructure because it conflates the means of connectivity (physical topology) with the ends (service delivery). Classical percolation implicitly assumes an isomorphism between topological and functional layers. While valid for homogeneous random networks, this equivalence breaks down in spatially embedded systems characterized by functional heterogeneity, such as communication backbones, power grids, transport, water and gas, and brain networks [4]. Consequently, standard structural metrics can become poor proxies for service viability in heterogeneous spatial infrastructures, indicating collapse while functional sub-graphs persist [5,6].  We model such systems as physical–functional interdependent networks, where functional placement is a latent, operator-configurable control parameter. Functions (e.g., routing, storage, peering) are assigned weights based on criticality and deployed under centralized (hub-concentrated) and distributed (regionally replicated) policies onto the physical topology.  We simulate disruption cycles on three national optical backbones [7] using biased site percolation followed by hub-prioritized restoration. To quantify resilience, we introduce Service Continuity as the fraction of nodes retaining access to a function and Service Coverage as the fraction of geographic regions with active service presence.   Are structural percolation metrics reliable indicators of service resilience? We identify a systematic premature-collapse bias in topological metrics. At the point where Service Continuity degrades to 0.8, algebraic connectivity exhibits 9-17 times lower values and the largest connected component 4-7 times lower values across the three networks, prematurely signaling system failure while services persist (Figure 1). Can function placement increase resilience without rewiring? On the same physical topologies, distributed placement yields 13–30% higher resilience for both service metrics. This is quantified by the area under the resilience curve as nodes are sequentially removed. As a result, we show resilience can be engineered through intelligent functional reconfiguration without structural modification to the underlying topology.  Is there a universally optimal placement across attack and recovery? We observe a resilience-recovery asymmetry wherein distributed placement exhibits superior resistance during degradation, while centralized placement facilitates steeper restoration. This trade-off implies that no single configuration optimizes performance across both phases, with distributed architectures acting as shields during attack and centralized architectures acting as springs during recovery. This suggests that optimal architecture is phase-dependent, requiring adaptive reconfigurations to maximize resilience.  [1] C. M. Schneider et al., “Mitigation of malicious attacks on networks,” PNAS, (2011). [2] G. Dong et al., “Robustness of network of networks under targeted attack,” Physical Review E, (2013). [3] R. Cohen et al., “Resilience of the Internet to random breakdowns,” Physical Review Letters, (2000). [4] S. Boccaletti et al., “The structure and dynamics of multilayer networks,” Physics Reports, (2014). [5] J. Sterbenz et al., “Resilience and survivability in communication networks,” Computer Networks, (2010). [6] Y. Lou et al., “Structural robustness of complex networks: a survey of a posteriori measures,” IEEE CSM, (2023). [7] R. Matzner, A. Ahuja, et al., “TopologyBench: systematic graph-based benchmarking for optical networks,” JOCN, (2024).

nodes.title: Social Interaction, Campus Engagement, and Academic Outcomes: A Data-Driven Study
nodes.abstract: This study investigates the role of students’ on-campus presence and social networks in academic performance, using real-time Wi-Fi connectivity data and student career records from the University of Parma, Italy. We observe higher academic performance among students who spend more time on campus, particularly those who attend more lessons. In addition, students within tightly connected groups—identified through co-presence across multiple buildings and synchronous movements—tend to exhibit similar academic outcomes, as well as similar degree programmes and attempted exams, all of which are consistent with peer effect. Interestingly, not all groups deliver academic benefits, and small and highly interactive networks display significant variability in achievements. Among them, focused study groups—students attempting the same exams—demonstrate a clear advantage in results, while less focused groups tend to achieve worse results than the average student. These two categories of groups are mainly characterized by spending most of their campus time attending classes, in the case of focused groups, and by devoting a large share of their campus time to activities outside class hours, in the case of less focused groups. Nevertheless, class attendance generally encourages extra time on campus. This analysis provides novel insights into how creating space and opportunities on campus for students to develop social interactions influence academic performances. It also offers valuable guidance for institutions seeking to improve student retention and achievements through data-driven strategies.

nodes.title: Dynamic Bayesian Networks for Modelling Progression of Hepatosplenic Multimorbidity Related to Schistosomiasis
nodes.authors: Zhi, Yin-Cong*; Anguajibi, Victor; Oryema, John; Nabatte, Betty; Opio, Christopher; Kabatereine, Narcis; Chami, Goylette
nodes.abstract: Multimorbidity, which is the existence of two or more chronic conditions within an individual, is poorly understood in low-middle income countries. Schistosomiasis—caused by a parasitic flatworm—is a major cause of liver and spleen multimorbidity. No timelines and risk factors for the progression of schistosomiasis-related hepatosplenic multimorbidity have been previously studied due to the lack of methods to handle complex condition interactions and insufficient data. Here we studied 35 liver and spleen conditions related to severe illness of portal hypertension as dynamic graphical processes using Dynamic Bayesian Networks (DBNs). Point-of-care B-mode ultrasound was used to examine 4330 individuals (aged five and older) from 52 rural villages across Uganda within the SchistoTrack Cohort [1] over the period 2022-2025. 54% of participants were found to be multimorbid. We used DBNs, built on top of Bayesian networks, to introduce temporal modelling by learning two time-slice directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), and with stationarity assumptions, generalized to represent the transitions to over > 2 year. A DBN with intra- and inter-time edges was learned on our participants to reveal clinically accurate disease progression pathways, while through simulations, we obtained forecasts of the evolution of condition risks over time. Covariates as previously deemed important for cross-sectional analyses of hepatosplenic multimorbidity were incorporated to assess key risk factors in hepatosplenic disease progression, to answer further questions on the long-term efficacy of the treatment. As an example, Fig. 1 shows the dynamics between conditions related to periportal fibrosis (diverse types of liver fibrosis concentrated around the main portal vein related to schistosomiasis), where transitions inferred by the DBN indicated that severe periportal fibrosis typically takes two years to progress from when mild liver fibrosis is first observed. During the two year progression period, individuals would also have higher probabilities of developing hypersplenism (enlarged spleen) and a thickened gall bladder wall. Having both would lead to the highest probability of subsequently developing gastro-oesophageal varices, a life-threatening complication of severe portal hypertension. Hypersplenism was additionally associated with increased risk of splenic varices, splenorenal shunts, and cirrhosis. In contrast, praziquantel treatment showed no measurable influence on the temporal progression of hepatosplenic conditions, suggesting the only available treatment for schistosome infection does not reliably affect the downstream evolution of hepatosplenic pathology. More broadly, this work demonstrates the usefulness of DBNs as a temporal tool for understanding complex disease progression and supporting clinical decision-making. [1] Schistotrack: a prospective multimorbidity cohort. https://www.bdi.ox.ac.uk/research/schistotrack.

nodes.title: Recreational boating networks before, during, and after the COVID-19 pandemic: iterating upon Minnesota aquatic invasive species management tools
nodes.abstract: In Minnesota, USA, there are over 10,000 lakes and more than 800,000 registered boats, creating a highly dynamic and complex network of boat activity. Given that approximately 6.5% of Minnesota lakes are infested with aquatic invasive species known to be spread by biofouled boats and boating equipment, preventing further spread through this pathway to the thousands of nearby uninfested lakes presents both a management challenge and priority. A thorough understanding of boat movements and associated risk factors has led to the development of innovative network-based tools to understand introduction risk, develop optimal inspection plans, and identify collaboration networks. However, current tools risk becoming out of date as boater movement patterns are dynamic and existing models may no longer represent this dynamic pathway. Here, we advance our understanding of boater connectivity networks by building upon past work and evaluating the potential impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. To predict boater-mediated lake connectivity between 9,233 lakes across three time-periods we developed networks using data from the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources’ watercraft inspection program from 2018 to 2023 and XGBoost machine learning algorithm. We integrated adjustments to account for observation bias in the input data. Analyses suggest stable networks and an increase in connectivity during the peak-pandemic and decrease in the two years following. This approach combines high-performing predictive methods and existing management efforts to expand data-driven resources and leverage collaboration among managers, scientists, and stakeholders to support decision-making and strategic innovation across regions with patchy data availability.

nodes.title: Coupled dynamics on hypergraphs: Impact of random architecture on unstable fixed points
nodes.abstract: Building upon previous finite-size analyses of globally coupled oscillator systems, we investigate the dynamics of a finite population of Kuramoto oscillators in which pairwise interactions are defined on an Erd˝ os–R´ enyi (ER) random graph, while higher-order interactions are globally (all-to-all) coupled. The higher-order coupling contributes exclusively to the backward transition, thereby generating a bistable (hysteretic) regime. The global nature of the higher-order interaction facilitates analytical treatment and reduces numerical complexity. We demonstrate, through a combination of numerical simulations and analytical arguments, that finite-size fluctu- ations induce a transition to the synchronized state in ER networks. In contrast, analysis in the thermodynamic limit predicts the absence of a forward synchronization transition, reflected in the divergence of the critical coupling as the system size tends to infinity. To elucidate this behavior, we develop a numerical procedure to determine unstable fixed points for network-based interactions. The structure of these unstable solutions explains the indeterminate nature of the forward critical coupling in ER networks and supports the absence (presence) of a forward transition in the infinite-size (finite-size) regime. We further characterize finite-size effects by systematically varying the average degree, connection probability of the ER network, and the higher-order coupling strength. The resulting distributions of the critical coupling exhibit qualitatively distinct features compared to the globally coupled case, consistent with the behavior of the corresponding unstable fixed points.

nodes.title: Intangible Atmospheres: Sonic Measurement and the Limits of Network Visualization in Urban Systems.
nodes.abstract: Network science relies heavily on visualization and measurement practices that assume relational structures can be rendered visible without substantial epistemic loss. This assumption has enabled powerful analytical methods, yet it becomes increasingly unstable in urban contexts where networks are structured by intangible forms of information such as latent variables, probabilistic relations, inferred dependencies, and temporally unstable states. In these settings, networks do not exist as fixed relational objects but as continuously computed and updated processes. This contributed talk examines how standard visualization and measurement techniques systematically mischaracterize such systems. Drawing on examples from urban sensor networks, algorithmic governance infrastructures, and probabilistic mobility models, the presentation distinguishes between networks that are difficult to visualize due to scale or density and networks that are structurally resistant to visualization because their core relations exceed stable spatial and visual embedding. As Newman notes, networks are abstractions that “strip away all but the most essential information,” a strength that becomes a limitation when essential dynamics are precisely what cannot be directly observed. Our analysis of noise sensing networks develops a typology of intangible properties including latentness, uncertainty, temporal drift, and inferential density, and shows how common techniques such as force directed layouts, modularity maximization, stress optimization, and planarity assumptions suppress these dynamics in favor of visually stable structures. In these cases, visualization substitutes legibility for structure, equating what can be rendered with what exists. To address this limitation, the presentation introduces sound as an alternative mode of network measurement. Sonic mappings encode temporal variation, uncertainty, and delay as time based signals, enabling relational dynamics that resist spatial embedding to be measured rather than visualized. Grounded in this perspective, our talk reframes network visualization as one instrument among others rather than a neutral representational surface. Rather than asking how to better visualize intangible networks, it asks what different sensing modalities are actually measuring and what they systematically leave behind. Following Kitchin’s argument that data infrastructures do not simply represent the city but actively “produce new forms of urban reality,” the presentation proposes strategies for making the limits of representation perceptible, including uncertainty encoding, distortion mapping, indicators of dimensional overflow, and temporal sonification. By analyzing noise sensing networks and their representation we aim to foreground the gap between computation, representation, and interpretation; this work contributes to network science by offering a framework for analyzing urban networks whose dynamics remain intangible and probabilistic.

nodes.title: Knowledge Graph-Based Analysis of Curriculum-Industry Alignment: A Case Study
nodes.authors: Yang, Bonan; Banu, Syeda Zunehra; Ercal, Gunes*
nodes.abstract: This study proposes a knowledge graph-based framework to analyze curriculum-industry alignment. Using a U.S. university's Computer Science program as a case study, we construct a three-layer heterogeneous knowledge graph linking courses, skills, and jobs. Results show that the curriculum covers programming and database skills well, with strong foundations in algorithms, theory, and AI. However, industrial-level skills such as cloud platforms and DevOps are absent, and emerging roles have lower accessibility than traditional ones. The framework is generalizable to other disciplines and institutions.

nodes.title: Markovian ε-SIR on the complete graph
nodes.authors: Van Mieghem, Piet*; Van Mieghem, Piet
nodes.abstract: We investigate the continuous-time Markovian SIR epidemic with self-infection on the complete graph. We introduce a labelling of the state space based on Cantor’s zig-zag enumeration and show that, under this ordering, the infinitesimal generator of the process is an upper triangular matrix with a bidiagonal block structure. This structure allows us to derive an exact analytical solution for the time evolution of the state probabilities. However, given the complexity of the explicit solution, we derive an asymptotic expansion valid in the large-system limit, thus allowing a clearer understanding of the model’s limiting behavior as the system size becomes arbitrarily large. We then compare the exact dynamics of the Markovian SIR on the complete graph with the classical Kermack McKendrick (KM) approximation and also derive an analytical criterion to evaluate the accuracy of the approximation. Our analysis indicates that the KM model overestimates the number of infected individuals, in agreement with mean-field approximations. The discrepancy between Markovian SIR and KM is particularly significant near the epidemic threshold, consistent with theoretical predictions.

nodes.title: The Two Sides of VC Attention: Investor Visibility and Startup Memorability
nodes.abstract: In an attention economy, getting venture funding is about capturing attention. Here, we propose a two-sided attention alignment framework that examines investor visibility and startup memorability. Our integrated findings demonstrate that two key factors matter most: the startup's ability to generate media buzz and the investor's social visibility and network reach.  On the investor side, our analysis of nearly 500 U.S. software startups (founded 2011–2016) shows that ventures with more VCs on the board and board members active on Twitter secure greater subsequent funding - yet these features do not translate into higher sales.   Conversely, ventures without VCs on the board and those with boards whose members are not active on Twitter exhibit higher asset turnover efficiency, indicating a stronger conversion of assets into revenue [1]. This suggests a tradeoff between attention-driven funding and operational performance.  On the startup side, in a study of 197 U.K. micro/nano startups (1995–2004), we demonstrate that media memorability—operationalized through news semantic networks and the Semantic Brand Score (prevalence, distinctiveness, connectivity)—increases the probability of receiving VC funding beyond what media coverage and tone alone can explain [2]. In other words, how you're remembered matters more than how often you're mentioned.  Methodologically, both studies quantify attention using network and text-based measures, moving beyond simple counts of mentions or followers. Practically, our findings suggest two implications: boards should cultivate credible, value-adding social presence, rather than visibility for its own sake; and founders should seek media coverage that builds a distinctive, well-connected image, rather than pursuing mention volume alone.

nodes.title: The Political Reorganization of Everyday Life? Uncovering Latent Lifestyle Structures via Digital Traces
nodes.abstract: A common claim in polarization research is that everyday life is increasingly organized along partisan lines: from what we consume online to how we understand ourselves, even non-political content is thought to reinforce political identity [1,2]. We treat this “political reorganization of everyday life” as an empirical hypothesis and test it using network models of online lifestyles inferred from large-scale digital traces. We use a decade-long panel of U.S. Twitter users with linked demographic and voter-registration information (2011–2022)[3]. For each year, we build a weighted bipartite network between demographic groups and web domains, where groups are defined by race, age, gender, region, and party, and edges encode how often a group shares a domain. Excluding news and mega-platform sites allows us to focus on everyday, lifestyle-oriented content (entertainment, shopping, local services, hobbies).  From the group–domain matrices we apply non-negative matrix factorization to obtain K latent “lifestyle bundles,” and represent each group as a point in this bundle space. We then construct a yearly group–group lifestyle similarity network, whose edges reflect normalized correlations between bundle profiles. On these networks, we compute assortativity for race, age, gender, region, and party to compare how strongly each attribute structures lifestyle similarity. Regression models complement this by asking (1) how much variance in bundle memberships partisanship explains relative to other demographics, and (2)how strongly ideology predicts similarity ties once other features are controlled for. We also track these statistics over time to relate changes in sorting to major events such as elections and the COVID-19 pandemic. Conceptually, we treat online lifestyles as a networked object—a temporal stack of lifestyle similarity layers grounded in a demographic–domain bipartite graph—to scrutinize the “political reorganization” thesis. Methodologically, our combination of factor models, similarity networks, assortativity, and regression provides a template for extracting meso-scale lifestyle structure from digital traces. Substantively, preliminary results show that race often generates stronger assortativity than party, that sorting is not monotonic over time, and that cross-cutting lifestyle ties persist, suggesting that while politics matters, everyday online life does not always consolidate primarily along partisan lines.  [1] DellaPosta, D. (2020). Pluralistic collapse: The “oil spill” model of mass opinion polarization. American Sociological Review, 85(3), 507-536. [2] Nyhan, B., Settle, J., Thorson, E., Wojcieszak, M., Barber´ a, P., Chen, A. Y., ... & Tucker, J. A. (2023). Like-minded sources on Facebook are prevalent but not polarizing. Nature, 620(7972), 137-144. [3] Yang, K. C., Goel, P., Quintana-Math´ e, A., Horgan, L., McCabe, S. D., Grinberg, N., ... & Lazer, D. (2025). DomainDemo: a dataset of domain-sharing activities among different demographic groups on Twitter. Scientific data, 12(1), 1251.

nodes.title: Multilayer Network Architecture of Global Politics: Combining Diplomatic Connectivity and Historical Sentiment
nodes.abstract: Global political structure emerges from multiple, overlapping relational layers, including formal diplomatic ties and his- torical relationships. Here, we construct two global networks spanning all 195 UN sovereign nations: a diplomatic network based on mutual embassy representation and common diplomatic membership, and a historical network derived from sentiment-weighted bilateral mentions in narrative foreign-relations texts - both based of the respective nations Foreign Relations Wiki-page. Under Louvain community detection the diplomatic layer produces fewer, more integrated communities, reflecting the unifying role of formal representation, whereas the historical layer yields a more fragmented partition, consistent with persistent geopolitical divisions encoded in historical sentiment. These partitions are used as reference structures to validate a multilayer integration model defined on a weighted, undirected graph, with edge weights formulated as:  wij = σ(α · Eij + β · (Sij − 0.5)) where: Eij ∈ {0, 1} represents embassy connection. Sij ∈ [0, 1] represents sentiment score (0.5 if no sentiment link exists). σ(x) = 1/(1+e^{−x}) represents the sigmoid function. A systematic grid search over (α, β) reveals a smooth modularity landscape in which the degree of geopolitical division varies predictably with layer dominance. Emphasis on historical sentiment reinforces fragmented community structure, while increasing diplomatic weight collapses these divisions into more unified partitions - concluded on the basis of modularity-measures. All parameter configurations were evaluated across three independent runs with fixed random seed, yielding identical results and demonstrating robustness. Together, these results showcase a quantitative framework for integrating and weighting multiple network layers, enabling the unification of historical and diplomatic relations within a single model that captures contrasting geopolitical structures.

nodes.title: From Large Language Models to Large Communication Models: A Network Architecture for Dyadic and Adaptive Human-AI Interaction
nodes.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable fluency, yet they fundamentally lack the prerequisites for communication: they do not maintain an explicit model of their interlocutor, cannot infer pragmatic intent, and operate without temporally coherent experience or internal quality control (Bender & Koller, 2020; Shanahan et al., 2023). In contrast, human communication is inherently dyadic, temporally patterned, and adaptively coordinated (Solomon et al., 2021). We propose a network-communication architecture, the Large Communication Model (LCM), which conceptualizes human-AI dialogue as a coupled, multilayer, adaptive network system. Building on communication theory (Solomon et al., 2021) and cognitive emergence principles (Tyler, 2020), the LCM consists of two interacting network layers:  (1) a User Model Network encoding inferred goals, affective states, and interactional intentions, and (2) an Agent State Network comprising semantic, pragmatic, and experiential nodes.  Edges within and between these networks evolve over time via experience-based updating, implemented through an Experience Store that captures dialogue episodes as temporally ordered subgraphs. This dynamic structure supports iterativity, context renewal, and self-referential reassessment, all properties associated with adaptive cognitive systems (Tyler, 2020). A Communicative Reasoning Layer monitors coherence and alignment across layers by evaluating network-level consistency in turn sequences, sentiment transitions, and intent prediction paths.   We present a proof-of-concept simulation that implements the architectural components via specialized agents. These agents are realized using various AI technologies, including LLMs and Knowledge Graphs. The simulation environment models dynamic communication and validates the system's core functionalities, showing that experience-based updates significantly enhance dialogue quality relative to baseline LLMs, which is in line with evi-dence found in neural systems that long-term memory improves downstream performance (Wang et al., 2023).  Our findings suggest that communicative competence emerges from the interaction of two coupled adaptive network layers whose structure evolves through recurring dyadic exchanges, and which is constantly monitored for quality purposes by a third one, the Communicative Reasoning Layer. This points toward a new generation of AI systems capable of stable, reciprocal, and socially intelligible communication – an important frontier for science and industry working at the forefront of responsible human-AI interaction, or adaptive multilayer systems more generally.  (1)	Bender, E. M. & Koller, A. (2020). Climbing Towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data. ACL Proceedings. (2)	Shanahan, M., McDonell, K. & Reynolds, L. (2023). Role play with large language models. Nature, 623, 493–498. (3)	Solomon, D. H., Brinberg, M., Bodie, G. D., Jones, S. M., & Ram, N. (2021). A Dynamic Dyadic Systems Approach to Interpersonal Communication. Journal of Communication, 71, 1001–1026. (4)	Tyler, C. W. (2020). Ten Testable Properties of Consciousness. Frontiers in Psychology, 11, 1144. (5)	Wang, W., Dong, L., Cheng, H., Liu, X., Yan, X., Gao, J. & Wei, F. (2023). Augmenting Language Models with Long-Term Memory. NeurIPS 2023.

nodes.title: Revisiting fractional stochastic processes through the lens of spreading processes on networks
nodes.abstract: Motivated by empirical observations of epidemic dynamics, where infection and recovery times rarely follow exponential patterns, we aim to develop a theory that extends Markov processes beyond the memoryless assumption.  To incorporate memory effects into Markovian stochastic processes, we replace the standard differential operator in the governing equations with the Caputo fractional derivative, a generalization of the standard derivative to non-integer orders that intrinsically accounts for memory by integrating over the system’s past states. This leads to fractional stochastic processes, which are governed by the (dimensionless) fractional state equation whose solution can be expressed in terms of the so-called Mittag-Leffler function.   We apply the fractional state equation to generalize Markovian spreading processes on networks, aiming to deepen the understanding of the "physics” of fractional stochastic dynamics and to extend classical Markov theory beyond its memoryless limitation. We propose three complementary approaches to construct and interpret fractional stochastic processes in the context of epidemic spreading on complex networks.  We first derive the conditions under which fractional stochastic processes satisfy the semi-Markov property, meaning that the system’s memory resets each time the viral state of the network updates. This description introduces statistical dependencies among all nodal and link processes competing in the system.  Second, we introduce a time inhomogeneous Markovian description of fractional stochastic processes. Applied to epidemic dynamics, this framework reveals that memory effects manifest as time-varying transition rates with power-law behaviors.  Third, we introduce a stochastic time transformation that defines any fractional semi-Markov process as a time-transformed Markov process. We develop a deterministic approximation of this transformation and test it through the exact numerical solution of a fractional susceptible–infected–susceptible (SIS) process on various network topologies.

nodes.title: Mechanistic interplay between information spreading and opinion polarization
nodes.authors: Andrade Oliveira, Kleber*; Ferraz de Arruda, Henrique; Moreno, Yamir
nodes.abstract: The model introduced by de Arruda et al. provides a rich testbed for simulating opinion dynamics in social media. In it, each user has a continuous opinion between -1 and 1, which is incrementally influenced by the content received from their connections. A distinctive feature is that ihe model rewires the underlying network based on the opinion difference between neighbors, allowing a range of opinions (from consensus to polarization) and networks. However, the model assumes that a new post with a random opinion value is generated at each iteration. Here we extend this model on adaptive networks to feature information-spreading mechanisms. We embed into each user a memory list, and a probability at each iteration that the active user, instead of creating a new post, shares a previous post stored in their memory list. We control it with a parameter µ such that µ = 1 (no reposting) recovers the original model. Otherwise, when posts can be reposted (µ < 1), iterations can use the same opinion value multiple times. The new parameter may drive the system into polarization or consensus when all other parameters are fixed. Additionally, we calibrate the model against data by focusing on empirical cascade size distribution from Twitter datasets. Then, we run a genetic algorithm to find which parameters minimize the Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance between the empirical cascade size distribution and that from simulations. We show that even when our optimization target punishes the tail of the cascade size distribution in favor of non-extreme values, the model reasonably agrees with the data. Our formulation opens a new dimension to characterize echo chambers as a transient configuration of the system resulting from competition between posts rather than a steady state. It paves the way for a mechanistic formulation of how it is possible that social media users remain polarized even when opinion-conflicting content is not restricted.

nodes.title: Overcoming Fundamental Limitations in Deep Graph Pooling
nodes.authors: Blöcker, Christopher*; von Pichowski, Jan; Hrabošová, Alžbeta; Scholtes, Ingo
nodes.abstract: Deep graph pooling builds on graph neural networks (GNNs) and aims to embed graphs in a vectorial space for downstream tasks such as graph classification. The perhaps most prominent example is predicting molecular toxicity, which finds application in drug development. While various approaches exist, we consider community-based pooling, in which a community-detection objective is used as a loss function to guide the learning of GNN weights. The basic idea behind community-based pooling is to iteratively detect communities and coarse-grain them until the entire graph is reduced to a single node whose features summarise those of the entire graph. These aggregated features then serve as the basis for classification, for example, via an MLP.  GNNs are utilised because they combine node features with topology, both of which are assumed to provide information for clustering nodes. However, they have a crucial shortcoming that is often overlooked in deep graph learning: their capability to summarise node features in a meaningful way depends on how well those features align with the graph's topology. Consider the molecule in Figure 1a where the node features do not align well with the topological communities. An MLP, or any other learnable function, that assigns communities based on the nodes' features, here represented by colours, must assign nodes with the same colour to the same community. Essentially, this means that the nodes' colours restrict the space of possible communities that can be proposed to the community-detection objective function. In other words: deep graph pooling suffers from the fundamental mismatch that (i) communities are proposed based on the nodes' features while (ii) they are evaluated based on the graph's topology. Whether pooling can be effective boils down to the alignment between features and topology, that is, it depends on the data.  To estimate the effectiveness of pooling for a specific dataset, we develop a framework based on node colourings, which we use as a proxy for node features. For pooling, two aspects are essential: colourings must be valid and transferable. First, a colouring is valid if there are no two nodes with the same colour that belong to different communities (see Figure 1b). In other words: if a mapping from node colours to communities exists, the colouring is valid. Second, a colouring is transferable if the colours from ``seen'' communities can be transferred to ``unseen'' communities (see Figure 1c-d). Transferability is essential because deep graph pooling is used in inductive settings, where models are trained on a training set and then applied to unseen graphs.  Our preliminary results show that datasets with more colours are more likely to have valid colourings, where validity is guaranteed when all nodes have unique colours. However, transferability is guaranteed only when there is only a single colour, and the presence of more colours tends to limit transferability. Because validity and transferability represent competing objectives, we regard the ``quality'' of a colouring as the minimum of the two. Previous work has shown that the working principles of GNNs are essentially equivalent to the so-called colour refinement algorithm, and we find that GNNs increase the number of unique colours by combining features with topological information. That is, while GNNs make colourings more valid, they hamper transferability. For effective deep graph pooling, we need to find a middle ground and make a trade-off; preliminary results suggest enriching node features with Laplacian Positional Encodings or random-walk-based features as a promising approach.

nodes.title: DomiCoarse: dominance-based coarsening that preserves structure and dynamics in complex networks
nodes.authors: Engsig, Marcus*; Tejedor, Alejandro; Fernandez, Marco; Moreno, Yamir
nodes.abstract: The rapid growth of empirical network data has pushed many classical analysis and simulation techniques beyond computational feasibility, motivating the development of network coarsening methods that reduce system size while preserving essential structure and dynamics. Existing approaches typically rely on similarity-based aggregation, spectral embeddings, or community detection, often at the cost of interpretability, scalability, or dynamical fidelity. Here, we introduce a dominance-based coarsening framework in which nodes are hierarchically absorbed according to their structural influence (dominance), producing a reduced network that preserves both local and global connectivity patterns. Coarsening is performed through a new absorption mechanism whereby dominant nodes fractionally incorporate subdominant neighbors and inherit their interactions through a principled rewiring scheme, ensuring degree preservation and conservation of key structural and dynamical properties (see Fig 1a). Focusing on spreading dynamics (SIR), we show that the coarse-grained representation faithfully reproduces the temporal evolution observed on the original network, despite a substantial reduction in network size. In particular, the coarsened network captures both the timing and amplitude of the spreading process, indicating that the reduction does not distort the dominant dynamical pathways (Fig 1b).   Beyond synthetic benchmarks, the dominance-based approach preserves key structural and spectral properties across a wide range of networks, enabling efficient simulations and multiscale analysis of large complex systems. By explicitly encoding hierarchical dominance rather than similarity, this new framework offers an interpretable and scalable alternative to existing coarsening techniques, opening new possibilities for the analysis of network dynamics at previously inaccessible scales.

nodes.title: When Fluid Dynamics Meets Epidemics: A Navier–Stokes–Inspired SIR Flow on Networks
nodes.authors: Zhang, Zonghan*; Benbrahim, Marouane; Rashme, Tamanna; Mu, Lin; Chen, Zhiqian
nodes.abstract: Fluid dynamics and epidemic spreading describe seemingly different phenomena, yet both govern how quantities move, accumulate, and dissipate over structured domains. In fluid systems, momentum, pressure, and viscosity determine how mass is transported through space; in epidemics, infection propagates through contact networks under heterogeneous transmission, recovery, and intervention effects. While fluid dynamics explicitly models transport through conservation laws and flow equations, epidemic spreading is often described using compartmental models such as SIR, which emphasize local reaction dynamics at nodes. As a result, transport-related effects in epidemics, such as directional propagation, spatial smoothing, and inertia in spreading dynamics, are typically treated implicitly rather than modeled directly.  Motivated by this analogy, we introduce a Navier-Stokes-inspired SIR (NS-SIR) formulation that recasts epidemic spreading as a physical flow evolving on a network. Building on the classical SIR framework, we define the \textit{infection velocity} $u_i(t)$ as the rate of change of infection at node $i$ and model its evolution using graph-based differential operators. As illustrated in \textbf{Fig.~1(Middle)}, the dynamics combine pressure-driven transport, viscous diffusion over network structure, external forcing that captures interventions or behavioral adaptation, and recovery-induced damping. Infection pressure is determined by a graph Poisson equation, which provides a closure condition that enforces conservation of epidemic flow across the network. Together, these components yield a network analogue of the Navier-Stokes equations while preserving epidemiological interpretability.  The NS--SIR formulation naturally supports \textbf{data-driven operator discovery}. By decomposing epidemic evolution into explicit graph operators, including gradient, diffusion, pressure-velocity coupling, and nonlinear reaction, the model aligns well with symbolic regression and neuro-symbolic learning approaches. These tools can recover dominant transport pathways, estimate latent advection fields, infer effective viscosity, or identify additional coupling terms from simulated or empirical time series. This perspective enables researchers to assess when classical SIR dynamics provide an adequate approximation and when transport-dominated behavior requires a more expressive physical formulation. Under the assumptions of network homogeneity and flow conservation, the NS-SIR model reduces smoothly to the classical SIR ODEs, demonstrating that the proposed model generalizes standard epidemiological dynamics while capturing spatial, structural, and dynamical heterogeneity.

nodes.title: Global landscape of ai policy
nodes.authors: Kim, Munjung; Park, Jaehyuk; yoon, jisung*
nodes.abstract: The rise of artificial intelligence has drawn sustained attention from stakeholders and policymakers, who face concerns about its potential misuse alongside growing interest in leveraging its capabilities to enhance national competitiveness. Accordingly, a wide range of issues has now come to be addressed under the umbrella of AI policy, spanning regulation, innovation, and economic development. Yet, developing a clear and systematic understanding of the AI policy discourse landscape including how different AI policy discourses evolve over time and how their thematic emphases align with governmental priorities across countries, remains challenging.  To address this gap, we map and analyze 111,375 AI policy–related documents from 136 countries published between 2000 and 2025, drawing on summaries collected in the Overton database. Using sentence-level text embeddings and BERTopic, we identify 23 distinct topics in this corpus, which can be grouped into four broader themes through hierarchical clustering of their centroids: sustainability transitions, technology governance, social inequality, and socio-economic growth (Fig. 1A). We then examine the temporal dynamics of these discourses by analyzing the distribution of AI policy documents across different topics over time. Over the past two decades, attention to economic policy and social inequality has increased markedly, encompassing issues such as gender inequality, online harms, and structural inequalities arising from technological transitions in the workforce (Fig.~1B). Next, we analyze cross-national similarities by computing average embedding vectors for each country' s AI policy documents. Countries in similar regions cluster closely together in this semantic space, and this regional structure remains even after removing all country names from the underlying texts, suggesting that it reflects similar policy orientations rather than simple textual markers (Fig. 1C). Finally, we construct a semantic axis contrasting discourses of data and technology sovereignty with those emphasizing openness and globalism. Projecting country-level policy embeddings onto this axis reveals that African countries tend to align more strongly with globalist discourses, whereas European countries are positioned closer to sovereignty-oriented framings (Fig. 1D). By delineating the global landscape of AI policy, this study provides a reference for understanding overall trends in AI policy discourse worldwide, as well as variations in thematic focus across nations.

nodes.title: Emergence of LLM hallucination in science: Evidence from non-existent citations
nodes.authors: Wang, Yihe; Zhao, Zhenyue; Yin, Yian*
nodes.abstract: We focuses on the emergence of LLM hallucination in science.

nodes.title: Reservoir computers as digital twins for brain dynamics
nodes.authors: Chepuri, Ravi*; Hunt, Brian; Pecora, Lou; Girvan, Michelle
nodes.abstract: The brain is a complex network dynamical system, and faithful representations of its dynamics would enable new ways to analyze and predict brain activity using tools from dynamical systems and network science. In this context, a digital twin is a secondary dynamical system coupled to neural measurements that evolves in synchrony with the brain. Such a twin must synchronize to the underlying dynamics, infer unmeasured variables, forecast short-term activity, and capture longer-term patterns of behavior. Together, these capabilities support the study of individual-specific brain dynamics and, in the longer term, the exploration of how targeted interventions might influence neural evolution toward desired regimes.  We adopt reservoir computing (RC) as the core modeling framework for these digital twins. RC offers an efficient approach to learning dynamical systems in which a fixed recurrent network evolves in response to input signals, while training is restricted to a linear readout. Because computation arises from the reservoir’s intrinsic dynamics, RC is well suited to modeling continuous-time, nonlinear systems. This architecture enables fast training, low data requirements, and robustness to noise and nonstationarity, while allowing reservoirs to be designed with interpretable network structure such as sparsity, heterogeneity, and symmetry.  Here, we report early progress toward establishing the feasibility and interpretability of RC-based digital twins of brain dynamics. Using high-density EEG time-series data, we show that these models can synchronize to observed brain activity, infer latent dynamical variables, and generate stable short-term forecasts. Preliminary analyses further indicate that RCs can reproduce longer-term statistical features of the dynamics, suggesting the ability to capture aspects of the brain’s dynamical “climate.” While the present work focuses on modeling and prediction, it lays groundwork for future control-oriented studies and extensions to additional neural data modalities.  From a network science perspective, this framework treats the digital twin itself as a dynamical network coupled to another complex network—the brain. This view enables the application of concepts such as synchronization, observability, and controllability to assess when a learned twin can reliably track and predict brain dynamics. Moreover, the explicit network structure of the reservoir allows systematic investigation of how connectivity, heterogeneity, and symmetry shape dynamical inference and stability, positioning reservoir computing as a natural bridge between data-driven modeling and network science.

nodes.title: Uncovering Unique, Redundant, and Synergistic Granger-Causal Effects in Networks of Random Processes via Partial Information Decomposition
nodes.abstract: Understanding how complex, system-wide dynamics emerge from the interactions among the units of a distributed network is still a challenging research field in Network Science. Partial Information Decomposition (PID) represents a well established framework decomposing the mutual information (MI) that a target random variable shares with a set of source variables to highlight how it is distributed among the sources, i.e., the unique information exclusively available from each source, the redundant information that can be obtained from at least two different sources, and the synergistic information that is revealed only when multiple sources are considered simultaneously [1]. Since it is formulated for random variables, PID cannot directly quantify causal information among random processes, despite causality being essential for identifying driver–response relationships within complex networks. To overcome this limitation, we exploit the PID framework to decompose Granger causality (GC) from N source random processes {S1, ...SN } to a target process T into unique, redundant and synergistic information flows. (FS→T ) [2]. In this case the PID decomposes the overall information transferred to a target as: FS→T = F R S→T + F S S→T + N i=1 F U Si →T , where F R S→T and F S S→T are the redundant and the synergistic GC from all drivers to the target, and F U Si →T is the unique GC from the ith driver to the target. Exploiting the formalism of state-space models [2] we provide a joint time and frequency domain formulation which highlights distinct high-order behaviors of Granger causal interactions. In a simulated network of vector autoregressive random processes (Fig. 1), we show that varying the coupling parameter c strongly affects redundant and synergistic causal contributions, localized at 35 Hz and 10 Hz and associated with chain-mediated and common-child structures, respectively (Fig. 1 (c,b)). When c = 0, the dynamics are dominated by a single direct interaction, with only the unique contribution FS1 →T present at 10 Hz, reflecting a causal interaction from S1 toward T (Fig. 1(a,d)). Conversely, for c = 1 the unique contribution arises exclusively from the direct causal effect S2 → T , while redundancy emerges at 35 Hz due to information flowing along the chain S3 → S2 → T (Fig. 1(a,d)). Pure synergistic causal interactions are observed only for intermediate values of c, where the causal influences of S1 and S2 on the target are simultaneously present at 10 Hz.

nodes.title: Dynamical analysis of a parameter-aware reservoir computer
nodes.abstract: Despite the widespread success of reservoir computing (RC) in time-series prediction and forecasting, a mechanistic understanding of how RC learns and reproduces dynamical transitions remains largely unexplored. In particular, existing studies have focused primarily on predictive performance, with almost no systematic investigation of dynamical transitions; especially discontinuous transitions such as crises. We first addressed this gap for continuous transitions by showing that when the reservoir is trained to predict systems undergoing Hopf bifurcations, it itself undergoes a Neimark–Sacker bifurcation, with its critical point lying in close proximity to that of the original system. This demonstrated that RC internalizes phase-space structure rather than acting as a black-box predictor [1]. Building on this foundation, we provided the first mechanistic explanation of how RC reproduces discontinuous transitions. By analyzing the internal dynamics of trained reservoirs, we showed that RC faithfully reproduces boundary and attractor- merging crises, undergoing the same crisis mechanisms and exhibiting identical scaling exponents as the target systems, despite its high dimensionality and distinct dynamics [2]. Together, these results establish a unified dynamical-systems framework for understanding how reservoir computing learns both continuous and discontinuous critical transitions.
